Organisation & Completeness Evaluation

value.lit: Now that the basic system is up and running, something's are gonna be hard as the usage increases. Currently I can write and store and share usecases, as the number of usecases grow looking at them all as a flat list isn't going to make sense. I'm need to organise them somehow, 1) so that the list isn't overwhelming, and 2) because I need that organisation for other purposes.

The first type of organisation or grouping that comes to mind (and need) is versioning. I need to keep track of which usecases are in which version so that when I'm working on that I can narrow my focus to relevant usecases and ignore the rest. Groupings should be arbitrary, I don't want to be restricted to versions. JIRA has issue type, components and more; Github uses labels/tags like AwaitingInfo, Enhancement, Defect, etc.

I was thinking, the real-world system, and real-world model... Ucs are descriptions of interaction. A version has nothing to do with it. It's separate. Versions are their own entities, part of a project schedule. Users should be able to change which Ucs are in which version without opening a UC, or affecting the content of a UC. In real-life the contents of a version can change very quickly and spontaneously.

What's similar? … Affected components (isn't that the actors?). I'm not sure if actors will suffice for this. But I know that grouping Ucs by it would be very important. Different teams of people can be responsible for different components and they will often want to only see Ucs for their own component(s). In real life it's: UC (interaction desc) → multiple components → people. People have their own org strategies (esp. team), components have their own relationships (eg. A and B are part of X).
Users might just need to group Ucs together. Maybe Ucs 1-8 all relation to a particular function (eg. stock trading)

Priorities are a big one! Similar to versions, fickle and ephemeral, change often, arbitrary strategies/definitions/schemes/approaches. They need an ordering between them (ie. High > med > low). Versions will need that too actually (ie. 1.9 < 1.9.1 < 1.10 < 1.11)
Any organisational units should be applicable to share UC selection. (Eg. Have a share that shows high-pri for 1.x)

Interestingly versions have their own attributes: release date; status/state = pending, in progress, released, in testing; more?) I might want to create a share that shows Ucs for all unreleased versions.

Also some org units might have different cardinality restrictions. 1 version might be required, 1+ components might be required. If a UC is missing a version of component a user would probably want to be able to see, identify and correct that.

Complication: Use cases are not always implemented in full. It's normal for 90% of a UC to be in scope for version A, and then the remaining 10% to go in version C. Usually this is because parts of the UC depend on another UC that isn't in the same scope. Function requirements are probably atomic enough to be assigned to a single version without issue.

Secondly, I also need to know which usecases are complete and which still need work. Usecases will often be a work in progress, filled with TODOs and TBDs, empty fields. I need a way to access the state of (in)completeness across my all usecases without having to click and manually read each one.

ul.lit: What problems could this feature create?
What kind of precision is required?
How critical to the business value is this feature?

//.lit: SEPARATELY: Browse thru study notes, collect good product/global-scope questions, prepare form, answer the next day.

value.lit: Bulk

I was reading about Feature Trees. It's a very simple method of organisation and mind-mapping. You start with the project/app in the center, draw a line out of it to a box which is a feature. Now along that line draw protruding lines (like brnahces on a tree) which are the use cases required to implement, or just pertaining to, the feature. Simple and effective. (Additionally, the book recommends writing functional requirements underneath the UCs but that's a bit verbose. Although a dynamic and interactive diagram might be perfect! Scroll around, zoom into an area, see the functional requirements appear. Cool.)
Anyway, I thought about this a little more and Features are just collections of smaller capabilities. They fit the same model described above. They have an n:n mapping with UCs.

Acutally, they can have their own priority which would probably be either 1) a multiplier to their children UCs' priorities, or 2) a parent priority that would take precedence over the children's priorities. Interesting. Once these were in-place some nice graphs might be doable, I'm imagining a table or grid, versions at the top, features on the left, and then colours boxes in the middle overlapping versions and... wait that doesn't make sense, there's no overlap. It would just be like the Major Features & Release Plan in V&S docs. Well maybe priority could be introduced. For some reason I imagine different coloured semi-transparent bars overlapping being extremely useful as a visualisation tool. Coming from my subconcious or gut, I'll need to think about that later. One thing is for sure though, automatic or easily generatable diagrams will get lots of points with potential users I think. They appeal. Good diagrams are orders of magnitude more efficient at conveying information that text. They're usually harder to manually draw and maintain than text. Even if I invent my own diagrams, they should provide a lot of business value.

Where was I? Organisation. Yes! Well Features are another organisational device, they may have their own (arbitrary) attributes, how their attributes affect UC attributes (if at all) will likely be arbitrary.

When the system gets versions, I would to see a list of versions and as first-class list items. What I mean is, unlike Github and JIRA where you see a list of issues and then apply filters, I want to just see a list of versions, maybe with a little footnote for each one like “23 UCs” or something. Similar to how at the moment you log in, you see projects, click a project you see UCs. I want to see Versions, click, see UCs. I would like the same thing with Features. It would probably be good to be able to do it for all units of organisation. The ability to do it with multiple levels (eg. ver → component) or maybe matrix/filter style would be the trick!
Two of the most important project goals are simplicity and ease. Therefore the following must be considered:

ul.lit: Sensible and intuitive defaults.
Effort to use.
Effort to learn, understand how to modify.
Separate and minimise intertwined functions & responsibilities.

Reordering fields.
Adding new fields.
Changing existing fields.

value.lit: This field set should apply to all UCs in the project.
To be super-detailed, this
be applied on a UC-by-UC basis, even more extreme would be UCs belonging to a given component get special fieldset modifications. But I think that's way too detailed.
This affects

. If a user decides they want an extra field for a subset of UCs, then unless functionality for that is provided, they will just add to all and either leave blank on irrelevant UCs, or fill with N/A. If they want to leave it empty then it may be considered a TODO within

When the user is configuring their fieldset, it should show them how many UCs are currently using a field and even show them the values. I might think “oh I don't use XXX, I'll just delete it”, where as I might be using it quite effectively in some UCs. Also do we hard delete their data? Maybe we should just soft delete and be able to swtich it on again later.

Now that the basic system is up and running, something's are gonna be hard as the usage increases. Currently I can write and store and share usecases, as the number of usecases grow looking at them all as a flat list isn't going to make sense. I'm need to organise them somehow, 1) so that the list isn't overwhelming, and 2) because I need that organisation for other purposes.

The first type of organisation or grouping that comes to mind (and need) is versioning. I need to keep track of which usecases are in which version so that when I'm working on that I can narrow my focus to relevant usecases and ignore the rest. Groupings should be arbitrary, I don't want to be restricted to versions. JIRA has issue type, components and more; Github uses labels/tags like AwaitingInfo, Enhancement, Defect, etc.

I was thinking, the real-world system, and real-world model... Ucs are descriptions of interaction. A version has nothing to do with it. It's separate. Versions are their own entities, part of a project schedule. Users should be able to change which Ucs are in which version without opening a UC, or affecting the content of a UC. In real-life the contents of a version can change very quickly and spontaneously.

What's similar? … Affected components (isn't that the actors?). I'm not sure if actors will suffice for this. But I know that grouping Ucs by it would be very important. Different teams of people can be responsible for different components and they will often want to only see Ucs for their own component(s). In real life it's: UC (interaction desc) → multiple components → people. People have their own org strategies (esp. team), components have their own relationships (eg. A and B are part of X).
Users might just need to group Ucs together. Maybe Ucs 1-8 all relation to a particular function (eg. stock trading)

Priorities are a big one! Similar to versions, fickle and ephemeral, change often, arbitrary strategies/definitions/schemes/approaches. They need an ordering between them (ie. High > med > low). Versions will need that too actually (ie. 1.9 < 1.9.1 < 1.10 < 1.11)
Any organisational units should be applicable to share UC selection. (Eg. Have a share that shows high-pri for 1.x)

Interestingly versions have their own attributes: release date; status/state = pending, in progress, released, in testing; more?) I might want to create a share that shows Ucs for all unreleased versions.

Also some org units might have different cardinality restrictions. 1 version might be required, 1+ components might be required. If a UC is missing a version of component a user would probably want to be able to see, identify and correct that.

Complication: Use cases are not always implemented in full. It's normal for 90% of a UC to be in scope for version A, and then the remaining 10% to go in version C. Usually this is because parts of the UC depend on another UC that isn't in the same scope. Function requirements are probably atomic enough to be assigned to a single version without issue.

Secondly, I also need to know which usecases are complete and which still need work. Usecases will often be a work in progress, filled with TODOs and TBDs, empty fields. I need a way to access the state of (in)completeness across my all usecases without having to click and manually read each one.

ul.lit: What problems could this feature create?
What kind of precision is required?
How critical to the business value is this feature?

//.lit: SEPARATELY: Browse thru study notes, collect good product/global-scope questions, prepare form, answer the next day.

value.lit: I was reading about Feature Trees. It's a very simple method of organisation and mind-mapping. You start with the project/app in the center, draw a line out of it to a box which is a feature. Now along that line draw protruding lines (like brnahces on a tree) which are the use cases required to implement, or just pertaining to, the feature. Simple and effective. (Additionally, the book recommends writing functional requirements underneath the UCs but that's a bit verbose. Although a dynamic and interactive diagram might be perfect! Scroll around, zoom into an area, see the functional requirements appear. Cool.)
Anyway, I thought about this a little more and Features are just collections of smaller capabilities. They fit the same model described above. They have an n:n mapping with UCs.

Acutally, they can have their own priority which would probably be either 1) a multiplier to their children UCs' priorities, or 2) a parent priority that would take precedence over the children's priorities. Interesting. Once these were in-place some nice graphs might be doable, I'm imagining a table or grid, versions at the top, features on the left, and then colours boxes in the middle overlapping versions and... wait that doesn't make sense, there's no overlap. It would just be like the Major Features & Release Plan in V&S docs. Well maybe priority could be introduced. For some reason I imagine different coloured semi-transparent bars overlapping being extremely useful as a visualisation tool. Coming from my subconcious or gut, I'll need to think about that later. One thing is for sure though, automatic or easily generatable diagrams will get lots of points with potential users I think. They appeal. Good diagrams are orders of magnitude more efficient at conveying information that text. They're usually harder to manually draw and maintain than text. Even if I invent my own diagrams, they should provide a lot of business value.

Where was I? Organisation. Yes! Well Features are another organisational device, they may have their own (arbitrary) attributes, how their attributes affect UC attributes (if at all) will likely be arbitrary.

When the system gets versions, I would to see a list of versions and as first-class list items. What I mean is, unlike Github and JIRA where you see a list of issues and then apply filters, I want to just see a list of versions, maybe with a little footnote for each one like “23 UCs” or something. Similar to how at the moment you log in, you see projects, click a project you see UCs. I want to see Versions, click, see UCs. I would like the same thing with Features. It would probably be good to be able to do it for all units of organisation. The ability to do it with multiple levels (eg. ver → component) or maybe matrix/filter style would be the trick!
Two of the most important project goals are simplicity and ease. Therefore the following must be considered:

ul.lit: Sensible and intuitive defaults.
Effort to use.
Effort to learn, understand how to modify.
Separate and minimise intertwined functions & responsibilities.

value.lit: Ok, later I should run all my requirements through my project-attributes doc and customise the design.
Also how will these organisational units affect publish-views? Should they be present? Toggleable?
How will they affect UCE also? Lots of people like Priority in its own field.
How will these affect UC revision?
I didn't put too much thought into these two functions when I built them into the prototype.
I originally wanted the features for the following reasons:

ul.lit: So I could see/confirm the layout/markup before sharing/printing.
So I could read through and review all my UCs.

value.lit: I see the two random buttons in the prototype as a problem now because:

ul.lit: You can't filter the UCs.
You can't add a title & preface.

value.lit: Actually I think I need the same thing as a share, except with the password. Probably overkill saving the “view” or whatnot. Maybe I could be [main] → [filter] ↔ [view] → [pdf]. Something like that would then allow anon users to filter shares they look at and generate a pdf too. Might be useful. Guessing...

//.lit: [04/12/2013: I also need to know which usecases are complete and which still need work. Usecases will often be a work in progress, filled with TODOs and TBDs, empty fields. I need a way to access the state of (in)completeness across my all usecases without having to click and manually read each one. ]

value.lit: When I'm revising UCs I want the following:

ul.lit: To know which UCs have bad tags, and how many: [DELETED] refs, invalid step refs, invalid UC refs.
To know which UCs have these, and how many: TODOs, TBDs.
To know which UCs have empty (mandatory) fields, and how many.
To be able to see all of the above in context without having to open each UC.
Total number of above.
Ability to filter UC scope.

value.lit: The pros usually add additional info to TODOs and TBDs such as who is responsible for the next step, when they expect to know, etc.
It might be an idea to auto-populate new UCs with TODOs in each field and use that instead of considering empty fields a problem. Or maybe have the ability to switch empty fields on/off.
Users will likely need to configure their own UC fields. This includes:

ul.lit: Removing fields.
Reordering fields.
Adding new fields.
Changing existing fields.

To be super-detailed, this
be applied on a UC-by-UC basis, even more extreme would be UCs belonging to a given component get special fieldset modifications. But I think that's way too detailed.
This affects

. If a user decides they want an extra field for a subset of UCs, then unless functionality for that is provided, they will just add to all and either leave blank on irrelevant UCs, or fill with N/A. If they want to leave it empty then it may be considered a TODO within

I might think “oh I don't use XXX, I'll just delete it”, where as I might be using it quite effectively in some UCs. Also do we hard delete their data? Maybe we should just soft delete and be able to swtich it on again later.

h4.lit: You said previously “ I need […] organisation for other purposes”. What purposes can you think of?

value.lit: I can think of these purposes:

ul.lit: BA filtering UC scope when reading/printing/reviewing UCs.
A share filtering UC scope when being accessed by Readers.
PM/BA determining the scope of their versions.
BA accessing the influence/scope/size of an organisational unit. Inverse of 1.
Eg. How big is 1.0, how much work does Team A have to work with, what's completed.

h4.lit: Who is interested in those purposes and how important is it to them?

value.lit: 1. BA. Very important as project grows. 10 Ucs = slightly important, 50 UCs important. 100+ UCs critical.
2. BA & Readers. Same importance as above.
3. BA/PM. Somewhat important.
4. As above.
Why do those purposes matter?
1. Near impossible to work without being to filter shit out.
2. BA needs to manage amount of information washing over Reader. Also Reader, might not be privy to some requirements.
3. PM/BA needs to estimate effort for Ucs in a version and balance it against time & cost.
4. BA: Confirm allocations match what they've got in their head.
PM: estimate effort/workload and balance it against external factors.
What would happen if you couldn't use the product to organise you Ucs in the groups?
1. I would be very pissed off. I would have to use the editor to view each one-by-one. Wrt printing I'd just have to print everything and cross out certain UC titles.
2. I would have to manually select Ucs. Every time a UC came in-scope or went out-of-scope I'd have to manually update all shares. Annoying.
Actually I misread. If the product didn't have any org features in it then I'd have only 2 options.

1. Use some external tool (Excel) to do the same thing. I'd have a row that matched the requirement Ids and then columns for the org stuff. That would be annoying because I would have to either omit titles from the external doc or copy titles into it and then they'd get out of sync.
2. I'd abuse the titles of Ucs to add extra information. Things like [VERSION: 1.0]. Things would get ugly.
If versions are metadata instead of real UC content, why do some people want them to look like they are part of the UC?

ul.lit: People are used to it. It's the way it's commonly done. Decades of precedent.
The distinction between content and org metadata is fine and not commonly recognised.
When sharing/reviewing a UC the Reader will often like to see associated org data.
Where else to put it?

value.lit: What about groupings other than version, do they all have the same relationship to Ucs? [Vague: Make relationships explicit.]
What attributes do <grouping> have?
See feature-organisation.ods.
If groupings were implemented without attributes, what problems would still exist, or arise?

Looking at the direct attributes, it wouldn't be a big deal. If everything had a single description attribute tht would probably be more than enough. If there were no attributes at all, I don't think it would matter too much.
For relation-attributes however, see feature-organisation.ods.
What will the consequences be if <each one> is missing from the app?
In the UC publish view, how is <grouping> normally integrated?
Why do readers of the doc need to see it?
See feature-organisation.ods.
What's the trade-off between time and value?
Something must be done to solve the scope problem. Everything else is nice to have.
The value should be sufficient to solve the scope problem with high value costing up to a month of time.
How can we judge the success of this solution?
Have an unorganised project of approx 100 Ucs.
Organise all 100 in into 5 versions in under 10 minutes.
Have a version with 40 Ucs in it, redistribute every 5th UC in a different version in under 5 minutes. (eg. 20% to ver A, 20% to ver B, etc.)
[Completeness Evaluation]
How do you deal with this situation currently?
Two ways:
1. View all use cases and manually scan text for keywords like TODO, TBD. If using MS Word I normally highlight in red so they stand out.
2. Keep a separate table of pending tasks.
How will you deal with it until an implementation is provided?
One of the two ways above. Because the UCE doesn't allow me to colour text, I might even deliberately input invalid refs so that I have something red so my eyes see it fast.
Who is this important to? Why?
The UC editor and reviewer.

It's important because the issues these TODO tags represent need to addressed and not forgotten. Because the BA/reviewer will read multiple revisions of a UC, they naturally start to skim making it very easy to overlook important gaps in the requirements.
What will they do after determining completeness?
If complete, maybe share for review, print it out maybe. Alert someone.
What will they do after discovering empty fields?
Fill them in or enter a TODO/TBD with additional info.
What will they do after discovering placeholders?
Try to edit the fields and resolve the incompletions.
What problems could this feature create?

People could be happy with certain fields being empty which would yield false positives. At least in their mind empty fields could be of less value than TODOs so they wouldn't want to wade through a sea of empty-field errors looking for those few TODOs.
How often would it be used?
Very often. Tags would be added nearly every time the UCE is opened for new requirements.
After requirements start to settle the incompletion-overview function would be used regularly until all were gone.
When would it be used?
All the time until the end of a project.
When you say you want to see all incompletions in context without having to open each UC...
- why is this important?
The not-wanting-to-open-UC part is obvious. Much effort.

The context part... well I was just thinking of diffs actually. Hmm, well the incompletions just need enough info to make sense without seeing the entire UC. Otherwise displaying them all in a nice list will lose its value if the user then has to open a UC to understand it.
- what would happen if you couldn't see them in context?
User would have to open containing UC; no point having a nice summary.
- what do you expect to see when you say context? How much context?

ul.lit: You'd definitely want to see the UC name, and containing field name for all incompletions.
If TODOs, TBDs etc are able to include additional data, that data should be displayed.
If not the entire field would need to be displayed.

For invalid step/UC refs some of the surrounding text would be useful. Ideally the containing sentence. If not, maybe just a number of words before the tag (important) and a number after (less important).

value.lit: What kind of incompletions are there?

ul.lit: Empty fields
Deleted step refs
Invalid step refs
Invalid UC refs
User-defined tags: TODO, TBD.

value.lit: What causes them in real-life?
Bad refs – Mistakes. Deletion of ref target.
Empty – Don't know/care. Not enough time.
TODO/TBD – Need to think, need to talk to someone, awaiting external event.
What is the typical lifecycle of such an incompletion?
It occurs, you see it, you ignore it, you resolve or delete it.
Can you think of or do you remember any variations?
No.
Some fields are mandatory? How often does the mandatory-ness change?
Well that's at the discresion of the req manager. It can definitely change over time as the project gets underway.
Also if a use case is missing a version or priority that is often considered an incompletion.
[Field Management]
“Users will likely need to configure their own UC fields.”
Why?
Use cases are not set in stone. Teams have their preferences. Somethings work better than others depending on the team and project.
How often?
Usually only once per-project. Sometimes fields are modified after ending a few UCs.
What will they do if they can't?
Be very dissatisfied with the product. Some teams will refuse to use it.
When?
Usually at the beginning of a project when less than 10 UCs have been entered.
Who?
The BA.
What problems could this feature create?
If a team has a nice setup and needs to redo it for each project they would be unhappy.
When you say change existing fields, what kind of changes can you imagine specifically?
Renaming it.
Changing its mandatory status.
Changing its position wrt other fields.
When deleting a field, what should happen to its content? [Dev question]
It should be gone from all views.
If a field is deleted it has content, should anyone still be able to see it? If so, how?
Well just after deletion it might be nice to see it in the editor so that I can copy & paste bits of it around elsewhere. Otherwise, no.
If a field was deleted by mistake, in error or just by bad judgement, what would the consequences be?
Valuable data could be lost. If there are only a few UCs it would be bad, if there were many it could be disasterous.
What are the time/cost/effort implications of said consequences?
Could be absoluetly massive!
What would you do to remedy the situation?
Would have to either reenter the info from previous copies lying around and/or from BA's memory, likely having to reengage the client.
How can we judge the success of a solution?
Find various UCs online and in books with different structures and ensure the system supports creating them with the same or at least very similar structure.
[Read All UCs & Generate PDF]
When & why would you want to view/read multiple UCs at once?
1. When editing and/or reviewing my UCs to ensure correctness at a larger scope, find problems.
2. To print out a subset to give/show someone.
3. When performing some other tasks that depends on them. (Design, dev, requirements diagrams)
When & why would you want to print UCs?
1. To read and/or review offline.
2. To give to someone else to read/review.
3. For safe-keeping. Hard copies can be a form of redundancy.
How often would you want to perform <answers above>?
1. Very often when working on UCs.
2. Often when working with others.
3. Depends on the task. Occasional~Often.
1. Occasional. Offline is a nice break from the PC.
2. Often when working with others.
3. Not often.
How important is a solution to you? What are the consequences of not having a solution?

Quite important. Without the ability to view and print multiple UCs at once, UCs are trapped in the app. Without the ability to filter these, the ability to view and print multiple UCs at once becomes increasingly useless as the UC count grows.

Now that the basic system is up and running, something's are gonna be hard as the usage increases. Currently I can write and store and share usecases, as the number of usecases grow looking at them all as a flat list isn't going to make sense. I'm need to organise them somehow, 1) so that the list isn't overwhelming, and 2) because I need that organisation for other purposes.

The first type of organisation or grouping that comes to mind (and need) is versioning. I need to keep track of which usecases are in which version so that when I'm working on that I can narrow my focus to relevant usecases and ignore the rest. Groupings should be arbitrary, I don't want to be restricted to versions. JIRA has issue type, components and more; Github uses labels/tags like AwaitingInfo, Enhancement, Defect, etc.

I was thinking, the real-world system, and real-world model... Ucs are descriptions of interaction. A version has nothing to do with it. It's separate. Versions are their own entities, part of a project schedule. Users should be able to change which Ucs are in which version without opening a UC, or affecting the content of a UC. In real-life the contents of a version can change very quickly and spontaneously.

What's similar? … Affected components (isn't that the actors?). I'm not sure if actors will suffice for this. But I know that grouping Ucs by it would be very important. Different teams of people can be responsible for different components and they will often want to only see Ucs for their own component(s). In real life it's: UC (interaction desc) → multiple components → people. People have their own org strategies (esp. team), components have their own relationships (eg. A and B are part of X).
Users might just need to group Ucs together. Maybe Ucs 1-8 all relation to a particular function (eg. stock trading)

Priorities are a big one! Similar to versions, fickle and ephemeral, change often, arbitrary strategies/definitions/schemes/approaches. They need an ordering between them (ie. High > med > low). Versions will need that too actually (ie. 1.9 < 1.9.1 < 1.10 < 1.11)
Any organisational units should be applicable to share UC selection. (Eg. Have a share that shows high-pri for 1.x)

Interestingly versions have their own attributes: release date; status/state = pending, in progress, released, in testing; more?) I might want to create a share that shows Ucs for all unreleased versions.

Also some org units might have different cardinality restrictions. 1 version might be required, 1+ components might be required. If a UC is missing a version of component a user would probably want to be able to see, identify and correct that.

Complication: Use cases are not always implemented in full. It's normal for 90% of a UC to be in scope for version A, and then the remaining 10% to go in version C. Usually this is because parts of the UC depend on another UC that isn't in the same scope. Function requirements are probably atomic enough to be assigned to a single version without issue.

Secondly, I also need to know which usecases are complete and which still need work. Usecases will often be a work in progress, filled with TODOs and TBDs, empty fields. I need a way to access the state of (in)completeness across my all usecases without having to click and manually read each one.
Organisation: initial
Organisation: follow-up

I was reading about Feature Trees. It's a very simple method of organisation and mind-mapping. You start with the project/app in the center, draw a line out of it to a box which is a feature. Now along that line draw protruding lines (like brnahces on a tree) which are the use cases required to implement, or just pertaining to, the feature. Simple and effective. (Additionally, the book recommends writing functional requirements underneath the UCs but that's a bit verbose. Although a dynamic and interactive diagram might be perfect! Scroll around, zoom into an area, see the functional requirements appear. Cool.)
Anyway, I thought about this a little more and Features are just collections of smaller capabilities. They fit the same model described above. They have an n:n mapping with UCs.

Acutally, they can have their own priority which would probably be either 1) a multiplier to their children UCs' priorities, or 2) a parent priority that would take precedence over the children's priorities. Interesting. Once these were in-place some nice graphs might be doable, I'm imagining a table or grid, versions at the top, features on the left, and then colours boxes in the middle overlapping versions and... wait that doesn't make sense, there's no overlap. It would just be like the Major Features & Release Plan in V&S docs. Well maybe priority could be introduced. For some reason I imagine different coloured semi-transparent bars overlapping being extremely useful as a visualisation tool. Coming from my subconcious or gut, I'll need to think about that later. One thing is for sure though, automatic or easily generatable diagrams will get lots of points with potential users I think. They appeal. Good diagrams are orders of magnitude more efficient at conveying information that text. They're usually harder to manually draw and maintain than text. Even if I invent my own diagrams, they should provide a lot of business value.

Where was I? Organisation. Yes! Well Features are another organisational device, they may have their own (arbitrary) attributes, how their attributes affect UC attributes (if at all) will likely be arbitrary.

When the system gets versions, I would to see a list of versions and as first-class list items. What I mean is, unlike Github and JIRA where you see a list of issues and then apply filters, I want to just see a list of versions, maybe with a little footnote for each one like “23 UCs” or something. Similar to how at the moment you log in, you see projects, click a project you see UCs. I want to see Versions, click, see UCs. I would like the same thing with Features. It would probably be good to be able to do it for all units of organisation. The ability to do it with multiple levels (eg. ver → component) or maybe matrix/filter style would be the trick!
Two of the most important project goals are simplicity and ease. Therefore the following must be considered:

ol.lit: Sensible and intuitive defaults.
Effort to use.
Effort to learn, understand how to modify.
Separate and minimise intertwined functions & responsibilities.

value.lit: Ok, later I should run all my requirements through my project-attributes doc and customise the design.
Also how will these organisational units affect publish-views? Should they be present? Toggleable?
How will they affect UCE also? Lots of people like Priority in its own field.
How will these affect UC revision?

Read All UCs & Generate PDF
I didn't put too much thought into these two functions when I built them into the prototype.
I originally wanted the features for the following reasons:

ol.lit: So I could see/confirm the layout/markup before sharing/printing.
So I could read through and review all my UCs.

value.lit: I see the two random buttons in the prototype as a problem now because:

ol.lit: You can't filter the UCs.
You can't add a title & preface.

value.lit: Actually I think I need the same thing as a share, except with the password. Probably overkill saving the “view” or whatnot. Maybe I could be [main] → [filter] ↔ [view] → [pdf]. Something like that would then allow anon users to filter shares they look at and generate a pdf too. Might be useful. Guessing...

I need a way to access the state of (in)completeness across my all usecases without having to click and manually read each one. ]

value.lit: When I'm revising UCs I want the following:

ol.lit: To know which UCs have bad tags, and how many: [DELETED] refs, invalid step refs, invalid UC refs.
To know which UCs have these, and how many: TODOs, TBDs.
To know which UCs have empty (mandatory) fields, and how many.
To be able to see all of the above in context without having to open each UC.
Total number of above.
Ability to filter UC scope.

value.lit: The pros usually add additional info to TODOs and TBDs such as who is responsible for the next step, when they expect to know, etc.
It might be an idea to auto-populate new UCs with TODOs in each field and use that instead of considering empty fields a problem. Or maybe have the ability to switch empty fields on/off.
Field Customisation: prelim
Users will likely need to configure their own UC fields. This includes:

ol.lit: Removing fields.
Reordering fields.
Adding new fields.
Changing existing fields.

value.lit: This field set should apply to all UCs in the project.
To be super-detailed, this
be applied on a UC-by-UC basis, even more extreme would be UCs belonging to a given component get special fieldset modifications. But I think that's way too detailed.
This affects the

feature. If a user decides they want an extra field for a subset of UCs, then unless functionality for that is provided, they will just add to all and either leave blank on irrelevant UCs, or fill with N/A. If they want to leave it empty then it may be considered a TODO within the
feature.

When the user is configuring their fieldset, it should show them how many UCs are currently using a field and even show them the values. I might think “oh I don't use XXX, I'll just delete it”, where as I might be using it quite effectively in some UCs. Also do we hard delete their data? Maybe we should just soft delete and be able to swtich it on again later.
Organisation: Q&A

h4.lit: You said previously “ I need […] organisation for other purposes”. What purposes can you think of? [Q is ambiguous]

value.lit: I can think of these purposes:

ol.lit: BA filtering UC scope when reading/printing/reviewing UCs.
A share filtering UC scope when being accessed by Readers.
PM/BA determining the scope of their versions.
BA accessing the influence/scope/size of an organisational unit. Inverse of 1.
Eg. How big is 1.0, how much work does Team A have to work with, what's completed.

h4.lit: Who is interested in those purposes and how important is it to them?

value.lit: 1. BA. Very important as project grows. 10 Ucs = slightly important, 50 UCs important. 100+ UCs critical.

ol.lit: BA & Readers. Same importance as above.
BA/PM. Somewhat important.
As above.

h4.lit: Why do those purposes matter?

value.lit: 1. Near impossible to work without being to filter shit out.

ol.lit: BA needs to manage amount of information washing over Reader. Also Reader, might not be privy to some requirements.
PM/BA needs to estimate effort for Ucs in a version and balance it against time & cost.
BA: Confirm allocations match what they've got in their head.
PM: estimate effort/workload and balance it against external factors.

h4.lit: What would happen if you couldn't use the product to organise you Ucs in the groups?

value.lit: 1. I would be very pissed off. I would have to use the editor to view each one-by-one. Wrt printing I'd just have to print everything and cross out certain UC titles.

ol.lit: I would have to manually select Ucs. Every time a UC came in-scope or went out-of-scope I'd have to manually update all shares. Annoying.

value.lit: Actually I misread. If the product didn't have any org features in it then I'd have only 2 options.

ol.lit: Use some external tool (Excel) to do the same thing. I'd have a row that matched the requirement Ids and then columns for the org stuff. That would be annoying because I would have to either omit titles from the external doc or copy titles into it and then they'd get out of sync.
I'd abuse the titles of Ucs to add extra information. Things like [VERSION: 1.0]. Things would get ugly.

h4.lit: If versions are metadata instead of real UC content, why do some people want them to look like they are part of the UC?

value.lit: • People are used to it. It's the way it's commonly done. Decades of precedent.

ul.lit: The distinction between content and org metadata is fine and not commonly recognised.
When sharing/reviewing a UC the Reader will often like to see associated org data.
Where else to put it?

h4.lit: What about groupings other than version, do they all have the same relationship to Ucs?
What attributes do <grouping> have?
If groupings were implemented without attributes, what problems would still exist, or arise?

value.lit: Looking at the direct attributes, it wouldn't be a big deal. If everything had a single description attribute tht would probably be more than enough. If there were no attributes at all, I don't think it would matter too much.
For relation-attributes however,

h4.lit: What will the consequences be if <each one> is missing from the app? In the UC publish view, how is <grouping> normally integrated? Why do readers of the doc need to see it?
What's the trade-off between time and value?

value.lit: Something must be done to solve the scope problem. Everything else is nice to have.
The value should be sufficient to solve the scope problem with high value costing up to a month of time.

h4.lit: How can we judge the success of this solution?

value.lit: Have an unorganised project of approx 100 Ucs.
Organise all 100 in into 5 versions in under 10 minutes.
Have a version with 40 Ucs in it, redistribute every 5th UC in a different version in under 5 minutes. (eg. 20% to ver A, 20% to ver B, etc.)

Issues: Q&A

h4.lit: How do you deal with this situation currently?

value.lit: Two ways:

ol.lit: View all use cases and manually scan text for keywords like TODO, TBD. If using MS Word I normally highlight in red so they stand out.
Keep a separate table of pending tasks.

h4.lit: How will you deal with it until an implementation is provided?

value.lit: One of the two ways above. Because the UCE doesn't allow me to colour text, I might even deliberately input invalid refs so that I have something red so my eyes see it fast.

h4.lit: Who is this important to? Why?

value.lit: The UC editor and reviewer.

It's important because the issues these TODO tags represent need to addressed and not forgotten. Because the BA/reviewer will read multiple revisions of a UC, they naturally start to skim making it very easy to overlook important gaps in the requirements.

h4.lit: What will they do after determining completeness?

value.lit: If complete, maybe share for review, print it out maybe. Alert someone.

h4.lit: What will they do after discovering empty fields?

value.lit: Fill them in or enter a TODO/TBD with additional info.

h4.lit: What will they do after discovering placeholders?

value.lit: Try to edit the fields and resolve the incompletions.

h4.lit: What problems could this feature create?

value.lit: People could be happy with certain fields being empty which would yield false positives. At least in their mind empty fields could be of less value than TODOs so they wouldn't want to wade through a sea of empty-field errors looking for those few TODOs.

h4.lit: How often would it be used?

value.lit: Very often. Tags would be added nearly every time the UCE is opened for new requirements.
After requirements start to settle the incompletion-overview function would be used regularly until all were gone.

h4.lit: When would it be used?

value.lit: All the time until the end of a project.

h4.lit: When you say you want to see all incompletions in context without having to open each UC...

h5.lit: - why is this important?

value.lit: The not-wanting-to-open-UC part is obvious. Much effort.

The context part... well I was just thinking of diffs actually. Hmm, well the incompletions just need enough info to make sense without seeing the entire UC. Otherwise displaying them all in a nice list will lose its value if the user then has to open a UC to understand it.

h5.lit: - what would happen if you couldn't see them in context?

value.lit: User would have to open containing UC; no point having a nice summary.

h5.lit: - what do you expect to see when you say context? How much context?

value.lit: • You'd definitely want to see the UC name, and containing field name for all incompletions.

ul.lit: If TODOs, TBDs etc are able to include additional data, that data should be displayed.
If not the entire field would need to be displayed.

For invalid step/UC refs some of the surrounding text would be useful. Ideally the containing sentence. If not, maybe just a number of words before the tag (important) and a number after (less important).

h4.lit: What kind of incompletions are there?

ul.lit: Empty fields
Deleted step refs
Invalid step refs
Invalid UC refs
User-defined tags: TODO, TBD.

value.lit: What causes them in real-life?
Bad refs – Mistakes. Deletion of ref target.
Empty – Don't know/care. Not enough time.
TODO/TBD – Need to think, need to talk to someone, awaiting external event.
What is the typical lifecycle of such an incompletion?
It occurs, you see it, you ignore it, you resolve or delete it.
Can you think of or do you remember any variations?
No.
Some fields are mandatory? How often does the mandatory-ness change?
Well that's at the discresion of the req manager. It can definitely change over time as the project gets underway.
Also if a use case is missing a version or priority that is often considered an incompletion.

Field Customisation: Q&A

h4.lit: “Users will likely need to configure their own UC fields.” -- Why?

value.lit: Use cases are not set in stone. Teams have their preferences. Somethings work better than others depending on the team and project.

h4.lit: How often?

value.lit: Usually only once per-project. Sometimes fields are modified after ending a few UCs.

h4.lit: What will they do if they can't?

value.lit: Be very dissatisfied with the product. Some teams will refuse to use it.

h4.lit: When?

value.lit: Usually at the beginning of a project when less than 10 UCs have been entered.

h4.lit: Who?

value.lit: The BA.

h4.lit: What problems could this feature create?

value.lit: If a team has a nice setup and needs to redo it for each project they would be unhappy.

h4.lit: When you say change existing fields, what kind of changes can you imagine specifically?

value.lit: Renaming it.
Changing its mandatory status.
Changing its position wrt other fields.

h4.lit: When deleting a field, what should happen to its content? [Dev question]

value.lit: It should be gone from all views.

h4.lit: If a field is deleted it has content, should anyone still be able to see it? If so, how?

value.lit: Well just after deletion it might be nice to see it in the editor so that I can copy & paste bits of it around elsewhere. Otherwise, no.

h4.lit: If a field was deleted by mistake, in error or just by bad judgement, what would the consequences be?

value.lit: Valuable data could be lost. If there are only a few UCs it would be bad, if there were many it could be disasterous.

h4.lit: What are the time/cost/effort implications of said consequences?

value.lit: Could be absoluetly massive!

h4.lit: What would you do to remedy the situation?

value.lit: Would have to either reenter the info from previous copies lying around and/or from BA's memory, likely having to reengage the client.

h4.lit: How can we judge the success of a solution?

value.lit: Find various UCs online and in books with different structures and ensure the system supports creating them with the same or at least very similar structure.
Read All UCs & Generate PDF: Q&A

h4.lit: When & why would you want to view/read multiple UCs at once?

value.lit: 1. When editing and/or reviewing my UCs to ensure correctness at a larger scope, find problems.

ol.lit: To print out a subset to give/show someone.
When performing some other tasks that depends on them. (Design, dev, requirements diagrams)

h4.lit: When & why would you want to print UCs?

value.lit: 1. To read and/or review offline.

ol.lit: To give to someone else to read/review.
For safe-keeping. Hard copies can be a form of redundancy.

h4.lit: How often would you want to perform <answers above>?

ol.lit: Very often when working on UCs.
Often when working with others.
Depends on the task. Occasional~Often.
Occasional. Offline is a nice break from the PC.
Often when working with others.
Not often.

h4.lit: How important is a solution to you? What are the consequences of not having a solution?

value.lit: Quite important. Without the ability to view and print multiple UCs at once, UCs are trapped in the app. Without the ability to filter these, the ability to view and print multiple UCs at once becomes increasingly useless as the UC count grows.
Templates: prelim
Right, well there are two things under the template umbrella that I'd like.

ol.lit: Viewing an explaination of how to fill-in a UC field.
Seeing other examples of the field's values.

value.lit: First one. Because we're not all experts sometimes when writing a UC you forget exactly what is supposed to be in a field, exactly where the borders of its scope lie, or maybe there are some important constraints on that field, maybe you'd like some tips on how to write effective values for it. For these reasons it's useful to use or refer to a template. When teams are using Word (or whatever) instead of ShipReq they will often create a template in Word with instructions on what to write in each field. Using ShipReq at the moment, you lose that. When I'm writing a UC FV I'd like to be able to see the instructions for that field for more clarity or help.

Second one. For all the reasons previously stated, in addition to looking at template instructions when stuck, people also commonly go and look at other UCs to see how they were filled out. Consequentially it would be useful if I were working on “Frequency of Use”, to be able to see “Frequency of Use” values in other UCs within the project. I'm not sure about various revisions, probably the latest revision would be best because older revisions could have bad examples that were later fixed. As for seeing values from other UCs outside of the project... I'm not sure. This would also be nice because I might like to copy & paste a value from another UC then change a word or two.
What about deleted UCs? Should I be able to view their FVs? Might not hurt. Might be useful.

So all of that's from a UC Writers perspective. From a maintainer's or head-BA's perspective, I'd also need the ability to customise the template and use it across different projects. I'd want to go in, edit the template value for field X, apply it and then have Writers see it the next time they write a new, or edit an existing UC.

Grouping names: what are your thoughts on two different groupings with the same name in a project? Would you expect that to happen? If so, can you explain why and give an example? Finally how would you cope if same names weren't allowed?

I think it would be incredibly rare. The only scenario I can think of would be if there were two trees of groupings with the same nodes but named differently at the top, so like “Dev Status” and “Test Status” could both a grouping called Pending. If it were applied like a tag/level then “Pending” on its own would be useless without context, it would need to be “ Dev Status: Pending”.
If it were not permitted, I don't think it would be a problem. That would just mean that every now and then one would have to add a little more description to a name. No biggie.
Grouping names will need a length limit. What's the longest name you would need?
“Version: Unreleased and/or pending allocation”
“Feature: Generic artifact storage (DOC, PDF, PNG, XLS)”

Groupings can sometimes apply to a portion of a use case, but not the entire use case. Can you think of an example? If groupings could only be applied to a use case as a whole, what impact would that have? How would you work around it? How much would the ability be worth to you?

When a use cases has many steps, it's not uncommon for a most steps to be implemented in version A, with the few remaining steps being scheduled for a later version, usually because they are dependent on another feature that is out-of-scope for version A. In this case Version, Status, and Feature are examples of groupings that would apply to different parts of the use case differently.
If they could only be applied to the whole... I guess there would be two strategies:

1) Apply only the groupings that apply to the majority and omit the others. The problem with that would be that someone looking at the v2.0 grouping wouldn't be aware that the UC was part of it. Which is bad.

2) Apply everything relevant to any part of the UC. The groupings would make sense but then anyone reading the UC will likely not understand why it has multiple versions, which steps belong to the second version. To avoid this frustration users would probably start using one of the UC text fields to describe the situation. An idea might be to attach comments to groupings when they are applied, e.g. I would apply v1.0 without comment, then v2.0 with a comment saying “Applies to [3.5.c] only”. Of course if such a mechanism were implemented, then the step ref in the comment should be kept in sync when the label changes.
It'd certainly be nice to have but there are more interesting features pending. With the above workaround (2) I'd probably be well content.
Actually, being a detailed kinda guy, if I were able to apply groupings to steps I'd probably spend too much time mucking around with it to make sure it was always accurate.

Currently use cases have a revision number. What specifically is that revision number used for? Why does anyone care? Should the revision number increase when a grouping is applied or unapplied? Would there be issues if the revision number didn't change? Do you foresee any issues with the revision number incrementing by 1 when a grouping is applied or unapplied?

The revision number is used when comparing UC copies to determine which is more recent (up-to-date) than the other. Admittedly I also use it to gauge amount of revision and/or complexity, which isn't a good idea; correcting a typo and adding 100 steps both only increase the number by 1.

The revision should definitely increase if a grouping displayed in a field changes. If the content of the UC stays the same then ideally it wouldn't change, else you might confuse people implying a change only for them not to be able to find one.
If the rev no increased by 1 when a grouping is (un)applied then that would be fine so long as the grouping change were discernable in publish-mode or when exported.
If a grouping were renamed, for use cases with that grouping applied, what would happen if the revision number wasn't updated?

The revision number is basically a contract that users can depend on to say “I'm looking at rev XX, you're holding a copy of XX you printed last week, we can rely on both our presentations of the UC being identical.” Anything that violates that should change the revision number, this includes field changes, grouping changes (renamed), grouping applications. If a grouping change is invisible (eg. grouping description) fine, but a name change needs to update the revision number too, else it's a loophole for people to break the revision-number contract.
Would you ever want to copy groupings between projects? If so, why? Please give an example scenario. What would the consequences be if you were unable to do so?

Sure, I could create a tree of priority groupings, could/should/must, with detailed descriptions including examples and rules. That would be tedious to redo manually, I'd see myself copying that for every new project.

Being unable to copy select groupings (I don't want to copy all) would mean I need to recreate stuff manually. For large and/or detailed grouping trees, YUK. Waste of time. Annoying. I want to focus on real work.

If a grouping that is applied to use cases and displayed as a field is deleted, what would happen if it were deleted automatically from said use cases? What would happen if the revision number of those use cases remained the same? What would happen if the deletion was a mistake?
If I'm deleting a grouping I would expect it to disappear from UCs. Revision number: see contract.

If the deletion were a mistake I would need to recreate it, then reapply it. If I deleted just a grouping tree node, then recreation and reapplication aren't that big a deal (so long as I remember how it should be applied.) If I delete an entire tree then it's much harder. Very, very annoying. But realistically how often would someone accidentally delete a grouping or change their mind later? It'd be pretty rare. If the grouping were a user-specified though, they'd be fucked.

Hard deletion here would introduce a lack of consistency around deletion semantics. Users can delete populated UC fields and recover them, it wouldn't be unfair for them to assume the same practice were possible here.

If a grouping that is applied to use cases and not displayed as a field is deleted, what would happen if it were deleted automatically from said use cases? What would happen if the revision number of those use cases remained the same? What would happen if the deletion was a mistake?
Same as above. Not being displayed as a field implies that it's simply transient metadata for organisational (or other) purposes but I wouldn't want to gamble on that.
What is the deal with groupings that aren't specifically linked to fields anyway? Will they be displayed in export/publish mode?
When using groupings to filter requirements, have you ever needed to see everything without a certain grouping? (i.e. NOT) Can you foresee a problem if this ability were not provided?
Sure, anything that's not status:complete, anything that's not priority:low, anything that doesn't have a priority yet.
It depends on the interface, as a workaround you'd select everything else except the grouping you want to negate. It would be at least slightly annoying. Tremendously so for larger grouping trees.
Can you please give some examples of multiple-grouping criteria you would use to search/filter requirements?
Easy: version 1.0 AND (priority critical OR high).

If groupings were specifiable as trees (with nodes being allowed multiple parents), what would the impact be if you couldn't specify relationships between different grouping trees? Examples? How would you work around these scenarios?
Here are previously collected examples:
Version|Feature → Status I can live without.
Feature → Priority is nice to see but really the app is fine without it.

Feature → Version would be quite useful. Workarounds: 1) manually change version of everything in a given feature. 2) Cheat and put Features in the Version grouping tree, either is children of the target version, or as a parent node with the target version symlink as a child. Something like that.
What would be cool would be if I applied MF-05 to a UC, and the Version field was automatically populated with v1.2, that would be AWESOME.
Organisation: Q&A

h4.lit: Grouping names: what are your thoughts on two different groupings with the same name in a project? Would you expect that to happen? If so, can you explain why and give an example? Finally how would you cope if same names weren't allowed?

value.lit: I think it would be incredibly rare. The only scenario I can think of would be if there were two trees of groupings with the same nodes but named differently at the top, so like “Dev Status” and “Test Status” could both a grouping called Pending. If it were applied like a tag/level then “Pending” on its own would be useless without context, it would need to be “ Dev Status: Pending”.
If it were not permitted, I don't think it would be a problem. That would just mean that every now and then one would have to add a little more description to a name. No biggie.

h4.lit: Grouping names will need a length limit. What's the longest name you would need?

value.lit: “Version: Unreleased and/or pending allocation”
