Low-Level Requirements: prelim
Low-level requirements are critical in the requirements process.
My assumption is that they are atomic because they are nearly always stored as a flat list with each requirement having its own ID. Actually in

, it says that each (LL) requirement should be independent. A problem (I feel) with this is that you end up with lots of requirements that are very long and only different to other requirements by a word or two. Examples:

//.lit: The user shall be able to allocate liability of a financial event by specifying a ratio.
The user shall be able to allocate liability of a financial event by specifying percentages.
The user shall be able to allocate liability of a financial event by specifying prices.

value.lit: People usually use Excel for this so whatever solution we come up with shouldn't be a worse UX than using Excel. Things we normally do in Excel are:

ul.lit: see a bunch in a list and move the cursor around editing them all.
filter rows by some sort of criteria
mass search/replace
copy, paste, edit
add/remove columns
change the type of a requirement (eg. FR to CO)
incomplete stuff I can colour red

value.lit: Excel works quite well actually. The problems that I have using it are:

ul.lit: Have to manually manage req IDs (unique, increment per type)
Keeping track of last-updated date. (revision would be nice as well)
Linking to MFs (grouping by?)

Some reqs arise as a result of two or more features intersecting, this is sort of hard to represent. What I'm doing in one project is having two MF columns, one for strongly-related, one for weakly-related. So far it works well. In some cases I even put a dash for strongly-related and the only feature in weakly-related which was an interesting side-effect.
No way to reference other requirements although I don't remember doing that much. (wait...ever?)
Have to restrain myself adding columns bcos too many scrolls half the stuff off the screen.
Have to store UCs which are already stored elsewhere. Then of course they go out-of-date.
It's annoying having to type “shall be able to” so often.

value.lit: Wow, I think that might be all these is to it. Having them recorded is the important part. What else?...

ul.lit: How they are used later is they are filtered and copied into docs (like SRS).
Eventually each should ideally by approved and signed-off.
It would be good to scan them all for ambiguous language.
The same incompletions functionality that applies to use cases should apply here as well.
I used to imagine having a checklist per requirement that I would tick during review. The attributes in the checklist come from the
book and they are: complete, correct, feasible, necessary, prioritised, unambiguous, verifiable.

value.lit: Looking at examples in Appendix C of
, I note the following:

ul.lit: Other LL requirements are indeed referenced regularly, often Business Rules. An example of an FR reference is

//.lit: fails, the COS shall roll back ...”

ul.lit: Functional Requirements
use a FR-n ID system! Nor are they flat. They are instead hierarchical and named. Example:

cb.code:
Order.Menu:  Viewing a menu
   .Date:      The COS shall display a menu for the date that the Patron specified.
   .Available: The menu for the specified date shall display only those food items ...

ul.lit: I'm seeing some 3-letter requirement-type IDs. (AVL for Availability Requirement)
This guy combines multiple things into the same requirement. Example:

//.lit: The COS shall display the remaining available delivery times for the meal date. The COS shall allow the Patron to require one of the delivery times shown, to change the order to be picked up in the cafeteria, or to cancel the order.

value.lit: The difference between FR-

and text-tree-style functional requirements is disconcerting but I wonder if there's a way I could merge the two instead of anyone having to make the distinction up-front (and stick to it). In the text-based scenario stable IDs would be required under-the-covers; in the numeric scenario the other style might be achieved by groupings and a mechanism to make some FRs children of others.
Requirement-types are arbitrary and need to be customisable. I need to be able to add & remove types, set type mnemonics, modify fields (columns) per type.
High-Level Requirements: prelim
Let's see what types of high-level requirements there are.

ul.lit: Business Objectives & Success Criteria
Business Risks
Major Features
Exclusions & Limitations
Assumptions & Dependencies
Operating Environment
Quality Attributes

value.lit: So what are the differences between HL & LL requirements? I'm not really sure anymore. Maybe none... Some of them (SC, EX, LI, AS, DE) might be a bit ambiguous. How? Um, let's see...

ul.lit: SC: project/business vs functional/implementation
EX: Project scope vs functional/implementation (?)
LI: Project scope vs functional/implementation (?)
AS: “
DE: “

value.lit: Ok, it seems that SC is the only one that would matter and even then probably not much. Scope could be distinguished by grouping or if one really wanted, by creating different requirement-types.

Why are HL & LL distinguished then? I guess HL comes down from the Gods at the beginning of a project and isn't edited anywhere near as much as LL reqs are. In fact, I'd often not want to see HL reqs when I'm working on LL reqs. Vica-versa too; when I'm working on business/scope reqs and making project-wide considerations I don't want to see the fine-grained solution requirements.
Also there's overlap between this and
in which features are stored as groupings. What's missing between traditional MFs and the desired functionality in
is nesting. (i.e. MFs normally don't list sub-features as children reqs... but they could/should!). Nesting sounds like it will come as part of
which means the missing link would be
Issues: Q&A

h4.lit: When would you need to
_ of incompletions?

value.lit: When I'm gearing up to move on to the next phase, finish off a bunch of requirements, when I just want to know how close I am to completion...

h4.lit: When would you need to
and then see the new total number of incompletions?

value.lit: When I want to finish off a subset of requirements. I might want to make sure all the requirements for phase 1 are complete, or maybe everything pertaining to 3 Major Features.

h4.lit: When would you need to see a

__.lit: list of UCs and the number of incompletions in each

h4.lit: ? If that feature were missing, when would you realise that it was missing? What would you do in that case?

value.lit: When I knew I had a bunch of incompletions outstanding and I wanted to

ol.lit: access their impact.
Example: if I see that 80% are in UC-3 which I don't care about right now, and see that UC-4 and UC-5 (both of which I want complete now) have 3 each.
quickly understand why there are as many as there are.

Example: there are 10 total, I think whaaaaaaaat there should only be 2 or 3, I see a list of UCs with number of incompletions in each, the combination of UC title and incompetion count is enough to jog my memory and/or for me to understand the validity of the total number.

value.lit: I would realise the lack of this feature...

ol.lit: If I saw a total number and thought “really? why?” and couldn't see a breakdown.
If in the above scenario a few UCs had so many that they dominated the breakdown and have it hard to discern the 1 or 2 odd ones here-and-there that I was actually interested in.
If I were interested in the completion of a subset of UCs not easily specified by a filter.

value.lit: In that case I would...

ol.lit: spend more time and energy parsing the breakdown.
spend more time and energy fucking around with filters.
jump from UC to UC manually looking for completions.

h4.lit: , would you ever want to see the number of incompletions therein? If that feature were missing, when would you realise that it was missing? What would you do in that case?

value.lit: I would want to know what was outstanding as incompetions. Seeing a single number somewhere on the screen would be one option, but then after seeing the number if it were non-zero I'd next want to find the incompletions themselves and probably edit them. If I were just expecting and looking for a zero, then cool. Presuming all incompletions are coloured red I would probably just quickly scan the screen for anything red.

Honestly I probably wouldn't realise the feature was missing. If I did, the UC would probably be massive, I'd be on a small screen with some shitty trackpad and even then I'd just blame the laptop. Ok, well if incompletions were not coloured red and didn't jump out at me, that's when I'd notice but then there'd be the problem of “ok, I see a number, now where are they?”.

h4.lit: What are some examples of
that you would store with a

value.lit: Often nothing but when present, a free text explaination such as “Analyse further”, “Ambiguous”, “Unverified”, “Conflicts with [uc-6]”.

h4.lit: What are some examples of
that you would store with a

value.lit: “Waiting on Bob”, “Pending arch meeting”

h4.lit: Why would you ever need to add
? How would you work without that ability?

value.lit: If there were a few common reasons for incompletion. “Ambiguous.” “Pending decision on A.B.C.”. Without that ability it would be super annoying to have to read every note attached to a TODO to determine it's underlying cause or classification. Things like ambiguity can usually be clarified anytime as they are an issue with language; a BA with free time would want to see those and filter out incompetions pending external information. On the other hand, if a specific decision is holding up many requirements, once that decision has been made a BA would want to see all incompetions that are waiting on it and resolve them all at once. Without custom incompetion types the BA'd have to read through everything which could make the incompetion summary useless; they might decide it would be less effort to go from memory to certain UCs they remember are related and manually scan for those specific incompetions there.

h5.lit: What determines whether a UC

value.lit: Company/project/team rules. Importance of a field.

h5.lit: What can cause that decision to

value.lit: The wind. It's fairly arbitrary. Just based on human minds which are fickle. Also a field can seem mandatory and then after writing a few UCs people change their minds.

h5.lit: How often would you anticipate such a change?

value.lit: If it's an organisation-driven mandate, then it will occur rarely/occasionally. If the decision is being made by a smaller team or single person and/or the project/org is young, then it will likely change often.

h5.lit: What are the consequences of a field being mandatory or not?

value.lit: Mandatory fields should be monitored for emptiness. UCs shouldn't be marked as complete or forgotten when mandatory fields are blank. During review they are sought out and resolved.

h4.lit: If one were
, would they ever need to
at the same time? What would happen if they couldn't select mandatory-ness when editing fields?

value.lit: Yes, I think they would. If I couldn't decide when adding a field, that would mean the system defaults it to either being mandatory or optional; if that default is ever not what I want them I'm going to find it annoying to have to go to another screen to check and modify that.

h4.lit: If the system didn't provide the ability to record
as incompletions, what affect would that have on your requirements work? What are some examples of loose-note incompletions?

value.lit: I'd have to…

ol.lit: maintain a separate list elsewhere. As a team of 1 I'd be fine with that, having a piece of paper and TODO list if it would be the full picture which it wouldn't be because the system would already be tracking a bunch of other incompletions. That would mean I'd have to look at my own list, and look at the screen, and mush them in my head. Also if I weren't working from home and didn't have my list of me I'd be unhappy.
bastardise the system. I would start storing TODOs in inappropriate requirements just to have them tracked.

value.lit: Example loose-note incompetions:

ul.lit: ABC is has gone out-of-scope. Revise all the XYZ stuff to remove references/considerations.
Consider custom fields for MF-[12]2.
Modify everything under MF-0[67] to apply to all kinds of reqs instead of just UCs.

value.lit: Low-Level Requirements: Q&A

h4.lit: Does anyone ever
? What else needs to happen when a req is deleted? What about references to it?

value.lit: Yes. Normally I just grey it out. The ID isn't recoverable. ID should always point to the same logic requirement. As for references, if there are any I guess I'd delete them. Sometimes it might make sense to keep them. Eg. “Blah blah. (Replaces FR-23)”

h4.lit: If references to a deleted ref were around, would you need to know about it? Why? What if the system didn't show you where they were?

value.lit: I'd always want to be alerted to it, yes. Cos those refs would usually be invalid. If they system didn't show me, it'd be annoying to manually check through everything and I would end up missing stuff.

h4.lit: Does anyone ever need to
what
was? Why? What happens if they can't?

value.lit: Yes. There could be an offline reference to it, or I may want to see what it was to undelete it. If I can't, that would mean it was gone forever, right? That's bad. (See next Q)

h4.lit: Does anyone ever
? Why? What happens if they can't?

value.lit: Definitely. Requirements change, scope changes and those changes often cause requirements to be deleted and then later restored and tweaked. If data were lost, that would be terrible. Unacceptable.

h4.lit: When
, please opine about the following effects:

ol.lit: Associated requirements are deleted.
Associated requirements are moved into a different req type. If no other req types exist, prevent operation.
If associated requirements exist, prevent operation.

value.lit: If I were deleting a requirement-type that would imply I was reorganising. Consequentially I wouldn't want anything automatically deleted (bye bye option #1). If it were very easy (as it should be) to change requirements' types in bulk then #3 works. #2 seems like a nice fallback but it's a shortcut which implies that it isn't easy to change requirements' types in bulk. That's bad. I thought we made it easy in
, it should be like that.

h4.lit: Why is vertical scrolling ok but
so bad?

value.lit: Vertical scrolling is normally fine because you scroll entire reqs on and off the screen. With horizontal scrolling you end up scrolling useful info about your focused req off the screen, not to mention the entire screen is then full of less-useful info about
reqs, most of which you don't care about.

h4.lit: Please list
that LL reqs sometimes get. Please also list ones that are specific to certain requirement-types.

cb.code:
MF        The primary Major-Feature driving the requirement.
MF-β      A Major-Feature that also contributes to the need for the requirement.
Created   When the req was first written.
Updated   When the req was last updated.
Version   The version in which the requirement is scheduled.
Priority  Obvious.
Example   An example of the requirement being applied.
Notes     Any additional information that clarifies the requirement.
TODO      Reasons why the requirement needs more work.

value.lit: Business Rules have columns:

ul.lit: Type of rule
Static or dynamic
Source

value.lit: These look like they could be accomplished by groupings (MF-07) but does one lose something doing it that way instead of a table with dedicated columns?

h4.lit: Why do you
? What would bother you if you had to store all of the info that you'd normally store in custom columns, in the req description?

value.lit: I need the columns because reqs have important attributes (RWM) and I need them recorded.
When it's in a grid/table, it's

ol.lit: Easy to see when something is missing.
Easier to comprehend. Less cognative effort required because I don't need to read to find the info I need.

Some columns are not necessary to the reader. The reader may read them once and from that time onwards the req makes enough sense on its own. They provide additional context that is required for less than 50% of reads.

h4.lit: When would you ever want to assign a req
? Why? What would the effect be if the system didn't allow this?

value.lit: When a requirement exists in an intersection between two or more features. If the system didn't allow this then either 1) I'd have to scan some other column to find secondarily-related reqs (eg. MF-β), or 2) I'd have to use refs to point a reader to the other requirements.

h4.lit: When sorting requirements, please provide
. When would you want to sort? After you accomplish your task, when would you need to sort next?

value.lit: • Sort by updated-date. When reviewing. Afterwards I'd restore the default sort order.

ul.lit: Sort by MF . semantic-IDs . numeric-ID. When editing to check organisation. Afterwards nothing; this is my default sort order.
Sort by desc. When looking for duplicates. Afterwards I'd restore the default sort order.

h4.lit: When sorting, if you were unable to select
instead of ASC, how would that affect you? How important would that be to you?

value.lit: Don't really care. Look up instead of down. Although it might annoy me with a priority column. Even then, not very much I don't think.

h4.lit: When sorting is active and you change the content of a requirement which puts it out-of-order, should the view

//.lit: SI: It doesn't, but a little button appears saying “Out-of-order. Re-sort” which user can click when done.

value.lit: Normally immediate resorting'd be awesome, instant feedback and all that. Only foreseeable problem its when I want to fill in semantic-IDs for a bunch of reqs in a row. I'd find it really annoying to have to refind the next blank req I was up to before the resorting moved my cursor (if that's how it works).... then again, if resorting happened after the cursor move that'd be fine.

h4.lit: When reqs are displayed hierarchically using their semantic-IDs, sometimes there are

__.lit: rows that serve just as a header

h4.lit: for their children without being a req themselves. If this behaviour were not supported how would it affect you? How strongly would you feel when you realised it was missing? How would you work around it?

value.lit: This behaviour is recommended in More About Software Requirements in the section discussing child/parent requirements. Granted that's not exactly for semantic-IDs but it's the same principal. If it were not supported it could lead to children reqs contradicting parent reqs, or reqs that aren't actually reqs being used to hack the system and achieve the same effect.

h4.lit: When viewing a list of reqs, for each req on the screen, do you
? For each column please state your preference, and what the consequence would be if you could only see it for one req at a time.

cb.code:
MF        Meh. I'll have semantic IDs. Technically out-of-scope for now anyway.
MF-β      Meh. I'll have semantic IDs. Technically out-of-scope for now anyway.
Created   Normally meh, but matters when I'm sorting by it. If I'm sorting by it that means I'm interested in the timeline, what? I have to click each one? That's annoying.
Updated   As above.
Version   I wouldn't mind so long as I have that nice grouping applier & view promised in MF-07.
Priority  As above.
Example   Ok not to see.
Notes     Ok not to see.
TODO      Annoying unless I can easily go from LL-req editor to list of incompletions in LL-reqs.

value.lit: High-Level Requirements: Q&A

h4.lit: How do you currently use MFs?

ul.lit: Comprehend what the project will do/provide.
Communicate the intended direction of the project (roadmap).
Break work down into smaller units.
Plan versions. (eg. MF-1,2,3 in v1.0, MF-7 in v1.1, etc.)
Find missing requirements by analysing feature/scope intersections.
Organise low-level requirements.

h4.lit: How do you currently use FEs?

value.lit: Actually, as requirements in the strict sense, I don't. However they are present in concept throughout the entire collection of project reqs in the following forms.

ul.lit: As headers amongst low-level requirements for organisational purposes.
As MFs sometimes.

h4.lit: When would you want to treat MFs differently to FEs? Or vica-versa.

value.lit: FEs would be too detailed and thus inappropriate for the following:

ul.lit: *Comprehend what the project will do/provide.
*Communicate the intended direction of the project (roadmap).
*Plan versions. (eg. MF-1,2,3 in v1.0, MF-7 in v1.1, etc.)
Find missing requirements by analysing feature/scope intersections.

value.lit: MFs and FEs both could be used for:

ul.lit: Break work down into smaller units.
Organise low-level requirements.

value.lit: To be honest, it's like this: in most cases, for everything with a
, at first one would only want to see MFs but there are times/cases where one would want more information and would benefit from being able to see a breakdown of FEs.

h4.lit: If MFs & FEs were all stored together, how would that affect your usage?

value.lit: Often I would only want to see top-level FEs because logically they would be MFs. I'd want to hide the detail of FEs. I'd probably store FEs under a different reqtype or not at all. Maybe I'd use groupings instead.

h4.lit: Why are MFs normally stored separately from FEs?

value.lit: Because MFs encompass many smaller parts. Usually MFs correspond with users' perceptions of functionality, where are FEs don't. For most of the business planning, FEs are not even considered because MFs are the starting points and at the right level of detail as the level of planning.

h4.lit: What is the relationship MFs have with other requirements? What do you need to do in that sense?

ol.lit: Nearly all low-level requirements (90%+) are driven by MFs. Consequentially, MFs are used to filter low-level requirements, and for that to happen, of course, the LL reqs need to be attributed to MFs. It's also important for traceability.

LL-reqs can be strongly or weakly associated to multiple MFs. Example: MF-A is strongly related because MF-A cannot exist without the req. MF-B is weakly related because if MF-B is implemented, then MF-A needs to cater for it via this requirement, however MF-B can exist without it.

Because I usually use MFs to scope what I'm working on, I often want to filter my view of requirements by the associated MF. I usually just use the strongly related MF to filter what I'm interested in (whitelist). I'm often working on a few MFs at once. It would be nice to also see weakly related reqs of MFs I'm currently working on, I guess.
MFs are also used for progress tracking. Eg. For MF-03, reqs are in 'approved' status, prototype is in 'review' status, etc.

value.lit: MF & groupings
MF & sem-ids
MF & req relationships
Collaboration: change mgnt & approval: prelim
This MF is about the following:

ol.lit: Reaching consensus.
Dealing with change.
Understanding the course of the project (i.e. the past & present).

value.lit: Taken from major_features.ods:

//.lit: Stakeholders (esp devs/testers) are often unaware when requirements change.
General practice: baseline, change request, request reviewed/rejected/approved, request affects reqs.
Stakeholders (esp. PMs) don't understand why project delayed: features should provide all info to justify, blame and improve.
Req change process often lacks transparency.

h4.lit: Reaching consensus.

value.lit: There are nearly always many parties involved in a project. A subset (normally user-class leaders) need to agree on the requirements and approve them for implementation. They need to read a req subset (eg all for v1.0), approve | reject with a reason (usually a request for change, sometimes denial) | ask questions or request change before deciding. There can be a lot of back and forth between parties and BAs. Once someone agrees on something they shouldn't be asked to agree on it again (system detail) unless it changes, or they change their mind themselves. People will want to see how the process is going, there should be transparency and a summary of how far along the process is, and how contentious it is too. Once consensus is reached, the set of requirements are tagged like in VCS and are considered a baseline.

h4.lit: Dealing with change.

value.lit: Once a baseline is established, we still need to cater for inevitable change. The formal process is to create a Change Request, have everyone review then approve/reject it, then if approved have the change applied to the requirements, with traceability back the the Change Request. Afterwards it's important to make sure that everyone involved on the project knows that the change has occurred and ensure that they don't continue working to stale requirements. Due to its severity some kind of auditable “acknowledged” flag for non-approvers may be important to prevent situations like a team BA approving change and the dev coding away saying later “she never told me it changed!” or “I didn't see any updated requirements!”

That whole formal CR process feels a bit stuffy to me though and it quickly went out the window at Macquarie. It can definitely be too much overhead for small and/or low-risk changes, which leads to a loss of faith and then adherence to the process. Everyone gets busy and lazy and as the pressure mounts people became stressed and very time-and-attention-poor. A good CR process is essential because without it, change doesn't get tracked, communicated, or reviewed. The trick is to have it be quick and easy enough to keep it in the process. Also it's a question of granularity & necessity: if a single CR impacts multiple reqs, does it make sense for half of the req changes to be approved and integrated without the other half? Answer = Sometimes|Never|Always.
Also another form of change is leaving req content intact, but moving them in/out of scope.

Another though, if the whole process is transparent, when dev A is viewing a changed req pending approval, the System had better make it damn clear visually, that they are looking at something unapproved. Close the loophole on people sending around link on unapproved shit and coercing implementation before approval with excuses like “Oh I didn't realise it wasn't approved.”

h4.lit: Understanding.

value.lit: Understand what?

ul.lit: Why a change occurred.
Who approved.
Why is so much change happening? (eg. reqs are ambiguous, contradictory, missing, infeasible, vague)

Understanding can be push/pull. Above is pull, i.e. when someone wants to understand. In other cases, one may want to push that understanding out to people. This is obvious and frequent when reqs change and one wants to synchronise everyone's understanding of the project (i.e. its future/implementation).
How many, and which changes where rejected, why they were rejected and why they were submitted.

h4.lit: Software Requirements 3rd Ed

value.lit: • Change requests have a detailed workflow of status & assignee, with special explicit roles & responsibilities for each step.

ul.lit: Change requests can be cancelled after approval.

value.lit: x

ul.lit: When a change request is approved and requirements are modified, if those requirements have statuses (eg. Tested, Deployed, Implemented) they should be reset. If users were using groupings as statuses automation support would be needed.
Serious daddys will want to record analysis of tech feasibility, cost, alignment with business requirements, resource constraints, risk, impact.
CR may include a
priority from originator's PoV
priority from Change Control Board's PoV
originator's user group (eg. Marketting, Team B, Testers)
target impl date
allocation to specific iteration/release
type (same as issue types in jira et al)
person assigned to each role in {verifier, modifier}
CR is approved (presumably in higher-level) before reqs updated and verified (verification = approval in my ramblings above)
Recommended criteria for a change-control tool:
Customisable CR attributes.
Customisable workflow & with multiple statuses (¿Key-Value?)
Customisable role-based authorisation on state transitions.
Audit trail of CR: Σ{change, time, user}
Email notification on CR creation & change.
Custom reports & charts. (Examples on page 483)
Companies may develop change impact questionnaires that need to be filled out as part of a CR. (Examples on page 485~488)

value.lit: Continue from Ch 29, pg 491.

value.lit: Continue from Ch 29, pg 491.
History/Audit: prelim

ol.lit: View a project as it was at a previous point in time.
Tag a point in time.
See list of changes.
Compare. See the difference between two points in time.

value.lit: Scope should be the entire project, so: requirements, groupings, fields, config.
Exceptions: tags.
Tags should be modifiable, deletable. One should be able to create a tag for a previous point in time, not just the current time.
Changes are immutable. If changes are to include comments then they will need to be mutable but I don't think it's worth it; more work to dev and maintain, process becomes tedious for user.

Granularity of a change. Easiest would be atomic, eg. “User A changed UC-3 step 3.0.5.a from X to Y at T”. When viewing a list of changes, that would get tedious as users often edit the same req many times in succession. There is value in each change being recorded (rather than aggregating & saving) because one can quickly and easily delete valuable data without realising. Instead view-based tactics should probably be employed, such as wrapping up sequential changes to the same req in the list display with maybe the number of collapsed changes and a button to expand and see them all. What about bulk operations, like applying a grouping to 50 reqs, or field-set editing where all changes will probably be applied on save? Maybe that strategy being inconsistent with the reqs (presuming auto-save) is the problem; if there's auto-save for reqs there should probably be auto-save for everything including project cfg. Then undo and/or revert becomes very important.

Comparison. I imagine specifying two points in time (one defaulting to now) and seeing a list of changes between the two. Not the list of changes in between those points in time, no, just what is totally different once all changes are squashed. (eg. don't show me 20 changes to FR-4, just show me 0 or 1.)

Another idea is viewing side by side with changes highlighted. Honestly though, these are solution ideas, tool ideas. The use cases and motivations for using any of these should be analysed and I'm not doing that now.

Another thing, usage columns may need to show two values: currently-in-use, historically-in-use. Reason being that it may be confusing to see usage 0 but only be able to soft-delete instead of hard-delete.
Undo & Auto-Save: prelim

ul.lit: Changes to projects need to be persisted.
Multiple users can view a project simultaneously.
Multiple users can edit a project simultaneously.
When editing, a user needs to revert & abort the change (avoiding a save and audit-trail update).
After editing, a user needs to revert 1 or more immediate changes.
Must minimise user need to resolve conflicting concurrent changes.

value.lit: Foreseeable issues:

ul.lit: annoying UX burden, data-loss risk, may cause change-set mindfulness & strategising in idiots like me.
more network connectivity (degrade perf & UX), more server-side effort, more DB space, change logs too detailed (created step, indented step, edited step x10).

value.lit: Idea: If timed saves gets the go-ahead, should probably prevent data from being saved for 30 sec to avoid noisy mistakes. So if user edits field A, 30 save timer starts, then 29 sec later user edits B. 1 sec later and the 30 sec timer is up, field B shouldn't be saved because it is only 1 second old. Consider this a min-bound; similarly, a max bound would be desirable. If for what ever reason a person spends 20min on the same req then something goes wrong, you losing the entire 20min of work would be unacceptable. It should probably not wait longer than 3min or so.
Going to think about some phase-2-relevant concerns.

ul.lit: Data loss must be avoided at all costs. Doing it for grouping types, fields etc. Req content must've be losable. Minimum required = save history, don't even need to make it accessible at first.
Immediately mistakes are not uncommon. Hence immediate undo is required.

value.lit: Undo an undo.

ul.lit: Connection problems.
Validation problems. (Eg. cyclic structs, etc.) Delayed saving probably fine but validation is better asap.
Knock-on effects such as add/del/reind step handled how? Am I conflating saving + history concerns with multi-user anti-conflict concerns? Seems like it.
Actually

shouldn't be a problem until there are multiple users (in a single-user ctx it only affects multiple tabs). Maybe simplest would be best until phase 3? Consider history reqs for data structures but whack save buttons on things and/or auto-save (LL-reqs) where appropriate for now. UX inconsistency but maybe ok short-term, changability cost shouldn't be high.

value.lit: Collaboration: authoring: prelim
Collaboration: authoring (prelim)

Requirements are normally authored by BAs on a project. A project often has multiple BAs, usually on the same team. I'm sure places exist where they would want non-BAs contributing directly to requirements (without the bottleneck of going through the BA), in fact some places may want to store and track all requirements but not even have a BA; PM & devs would edit reqs themselves.

So the system needs to allow multiple users writing to the same project, sometimes even the same requirement concurrently. I also want to have multiple browser tabs open on the same computer (so same session, user, IP, MAC) and have concurrent editing without a problem.

As with any multi-user situation permissions and security management is necessary. Here are some examples of groups/roles: PM, Head BA, BAs, project owner, stakeholder, tester, dev, “reader” (grant read-access to some randoms), business users, client, ops, designers. Plus there will be cases where different teams need to collaborate on intersection reqs, in which roles on their own may not suffice; it may become necessary to restrict access to a subset of a project. Example: I'm building a big system and component B requires collaboration with team X; I may want to grant team X read access to the entire project, edit access to reqs with grouping ComponentB, and create-req access with the constraint that reqs they create are tagged with grouping ComponentB.
Intersection with

here, if there are multiple authors on a project then it may become necessary to be able to assign incompletions to 0:n users and have users be able to filter out other people/team's tasks in incompletion views.
Intersection with
, all changes will need to be attributable to a user.
A project owner will need to be able to hand ownership over to another user.

The scope of a project isn't governed by the laws of nature; sometimes a project splits off into smaller ones, sometimes separate projects merge into a single one. I'd say that these cases legitimately fall under the umbrella of collaboration. Actually there'd probably be a real-world need to have collaboration between projects without merging them (esp with intersections). What would be required? Maybe just references? Shared groupings? Who knows. Needs analysis. Plus it could probably safely be moved to its own MF.

With collaboration comes conflict, mistakes, idiocy; quite likely someone good or authoritative may be unsatisfied with the contribution of other authors and may want to: undo their shit, reject a contribution with a request of amendments, ask them questions about a contribution, create a discussion around a contentious contribution, ask another user to review/confirm an amendment. Most of these could (and probably should) be pushed outside the scope of ShipReq in which case the focus shifts to anything required to facilitate the manual process. Very importantly, this leads to...
Notifications and watching! This should probably be its own MF. I want to know of all changes that happen on a project, or that affect a subset of the requirements. I want an email for each one
. I want an email every 2 hours, every week, etc. summarising all the changes that occurred in that time period. I want to be able to notify other users in the system, eg. “@golly pls confirm”.
Collaboration: stakeholders (prelim)
Most of this will be similar to
. By stakeholders I mean people who have an interest in the project but are not on the project team, so clients, sponsors, business users, group heads, etc.
What do they need? There are two classifications here, 1) interest in the detail, 2) interest in the high-level.

With an interest in the detail are future end-users, marketers & sales, often the client/sponsor for smaller projects of work, or in smaller organisations (esp. small businesses). They need to periodically review and approve requirements, comment on requirements to ask questions, request amendments. Similar to MF-11 you don't want them reviewing the same shit needlessly, if they approve something once and it doesn't change, they should never have to re-review it again. They should be able to filter out everything they've reviewed previously and read through only the remainder of reqs. This is I think a powerful feature. Consensus is conventionally hard and off-putting but paramount to success. I want to attack the pain points of

ol.lit: – don't demand the user remember what they haven't checked yet, or ask them to check the same thing twice. If a req was previously approved, modified and now needs re-approval, the system should make it super-obvious what changed (sometimes a req's text is large, a change small, and requiring the user to reread the entire requirement would be another instance of TMI).
– Protects involved parties like contracts do. People can't bullshit and say they didn't agree, or didn't see, or wasn't told.

– This is a massive deterrent. The review/approval process needs to be as quick and easy as possible which implies it must be as simple as possible too. Should come up with a bunch of QRs and UCs for this.

value.lit: There are surely other pain points; they should be discovered. Oh and before I forget, approval should be freely revocable; prefer to push consequences outside the system as much as possible.

With an interest in the high-level are people concerned with the progress of the project, to assess whether the project is on forecasted track (bcos their responsibility (PM) or financial (client/sponsor)), to assess where contributions are coming from, to assess what's holding the project up, to understand why progress has stalled. In these cases they'd mostly be interested in stats, charts, summaries. They're usually time-poor and non-technical won't give a shit about how ShipReqs or how to navigate it. They'd probably want a link, maybe a list of (arbitrary) things that make sense from their perspective (“Blockers”, “Features & Progress”), then a single piece of info, maybe with the ability to go from their into more detail.

Actually there's the business stuff too. Requirement types like Major Feature, Business Objective, Success Critera, Business Risk, etc. are of interest to an entire class of users that have no interest in FRs, COs, etc.
Also a manager would want to know what tasks/work in pending on people in his/her team. eg. Minion A has 120 reqs to review.

I nearly forgot! One of the most important ingredients for requirement & project success is communication and socialisation. Stakeholders, everyone involved in the project, even if transitively, should be able to

ul.lit: see what the latest requirements are.
see what's changed since they last looked, cared, committed to memory.
be notified what something they care about (or someone else wants them to care about) changes.

value.lit: Stakeholders often lack transparency.

Another idea, as noted above stakeholders need to provide feedback on reqs in the system, will they need to record comments/feedback on reqs not in the system (yet). How do they communicate that (existing req's are fine) but there are req's missing / other concerns haven't been addressed?

Similarly to how stakeholders want to approve/reject everything in an intensional subset of reqs, and only be shown updates henceforth, BAs would have use for an only-slightly abstracted feature: creating personal checklists. Say the BA or designer is creating a UI prototype externally, they'd want to tick all items in scope as they update the prototype, have those ticks disappear (or be archived) when a req is updated. The checklist should be able to be made private or else it will clutter everyone else's view. Similarly, this is also how a head BA could enforce a {Complete, Correct, Feasible, Necessary, Prioritised, Unambiguous, Verifiable} checklist for reqs.
Deletion (prelim)

There have been some major changes that affect all requirements pertaining to deletion. Project storage is now via an append-only event-stream. As such history is never lost and can easily be made accessible to the user.
Deletion subjects:

ol.lit: Config
◦ Tags
* Issue types
* Fields
* Req types
Content
* Generic requirements
* Req code groups

h4.lit: Why would you delete a tag?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things. They often imprecise.

change all the time; after seeing how statuses are being used one might decide to merge some, remove some; tags representing components can be changed to cover different subsets of a solution. It's also why we need to see usage – tags that rarely get used are often potential candidates for deletion.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means it shouldn't be used anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: It should be removed from existing data. Simple enough when it's just attached to something but it's a bit more involving when it's being used in text, like “Machine
do ...”. In those cases you can't just remove it, you need to either change it to another tag that fits or reword it.

h4.lit: What if you couldn't delete tags?

value.lit: I'd have to rename them stupidly to avoid people using them anymore. I'd want them to display in some kind of obvious way, like big and red so that BAs could see that they are obviously wrong. In fact, maybe they could be part of the issue system, like how other types of issues are reported and easily actionable.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Maybe if it's still in use – because then it's not really deleted. And the intent of tag deletion is removal of use.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things like I said. Plus sometimes there's trial and error involved. You want tags to be decently distributed; you might delete stuff, add new tags, allocate them and realise you're worse off.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd need to add the tag back (quick) and then re-tag everything (annoying).

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
tag → parent tag
tag → child tags
tag → field
tag → req/code-group tag-pool
tag → req/code-group text

h4.lit: Why would you delete an issue type?

value.lit: Like tags, it's just a fickle idea to reduce data into subsets.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means the issue shouldn't be usable anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Those issues either need to change type, or be removed.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete?

value.lit: Same as tags. Rename stupidly. Or actually, rename it the next time I want to create a new issue type.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: If it's in use still – because something needs to be done about those issues.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same as tags.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: Add it back (quick). Maybe change some existing issues to use it again.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code: issue type → req/code-group text

h4.lit: Why would you delete a field?

value.lit: I'd delete a field if it wasn't really being used in practice.
I'd delete a tag field if I deleted (without replacing) the underlying tag.

I'd delete an implication field if I changed the req-types, say if I merged two req types into one and they both had fields. Actually if I deleted the underlying req type for any reason, I'd need to get rid of its field. Although that should probably be automatic right?

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: That fields is gone. Can't add new data to it, can't see it anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Hmm, I guess I'd probably still want to be able to see it if I need to. Like if it's a text field, if I deleted the field I probably wouldn't move that field data around. I'd just leave it but one day I might need to read it again and extract anything useful.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete a field?

value.lit: Nothing I guess. But I'd want to rename the field at least to indicate not to add any new data to it.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Well I just wouldn't want to lose data. If it deleted the field and the data with it, that would be bad. Gathered BA data (usually from interviews and analysis) should never be lost.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: New requirements might come in that revalidate the existence of the field, so I'd wanna get it back again and populate new rows with it.
Or someone with different needs might say they really need it.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind after a field was deleted.

value.lit: I'd want to add it back again and not lose any data.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
field ←req type (implication)
field ←tag
field ←req types (applicability)
field → req field values

h4.lit: Why would you delete a req type?

value.lit: It might not be getting used. Or we might just look at our data differently and decide we have a better way of representing it.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means we shouldn't have any reqs allocated to it anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: They should be reallocated to a different req type. Or rarely, just deleted.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete a req type?

value.lit: Rename it like my answers to the other questions.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: We might have printed a report, or stored references to our reqs in other systems. In both cases the external entity will have an ID to a req that contains the req type. If we deleted the req type, it would break their references. They should always be able to use those references to look up the same reqs. If a req type is deleted, reqs should still remember their old req types & IDs. We should still be able to delete req types though.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same reason for why I would delete one.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd want to add the req type back, maybe change reqs back to use it again.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
req type → field (implication)
req type → field (applicability)
req type → pubid register
req type → associated generic reqs
req type → refs in text

h4.lit: Why would you delete a req?

value.lit: An underlying (implying) requirement could change.
Or people's minds could change, or new information is discovered. Then existing requirements become obsolete and need to be deleted.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: Turned off. Not visible anymore unless someone wants to search or look through stuff that's been removed.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Refs to it (from non-deleted stuff) should probably be considered errors. Not just text refs, but implications too. In that nothing alive should be implied by something dead. Wait, unless it's implied by something else in which case just ignore the dead implication.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete?

value.lit: Tag them as deleted.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Dunno.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same reason you might delete one. Things change, life changes.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd probably just want to undo it. All it's old implications should be restored. It's req codes and their groups should come back. Text refs to it should be fine again (i.e. not an error anymore).

h4.lit: What if the req codes are in use now?

value.lit: I guess the ones being undeleted should be renamed. they should still form the same trees though – I wouldn't want one renamed but not others. Why? Cos I'd need to manually take care of the conflict and for that I'd want to look at what exists now and then the entire undeleted tree with its structure as it was.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
gen req ← refs in text
gen req ↔ gen reqs (implication)
gen req ↔ req codes associated
gen req -- req code groups (indirectly)
gen req → req data (fields, tags, text)
gen req ← pubid register

h4.lit: Why would you delete an RCG?

ol.lit: If I reorganise my reqs and it becomes obsolete.
If I delete a bunch of reqs and the code group is only useful in that group of reqs.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

ol.lit: I don't need it anymore. I don't want to see it anymore.
I want it removed with the reqs being removed.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Refs to it need to be removed. Children are fine if they still exist.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete groups?

value.lit: I'd rename them, eventually recycle them and hate you.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Nope.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

ol.lit: Reorg, becomes relevant again.
I undelete a group of reqs that came under the group.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

ol.lit: Recreate it. Think of and re-enter a meaningful title again. Would be nice if I didn't have to do this.
Recreate all the req code groups I used to have. I'd have to remember titles or think of new ones. All of that would be annoying. Ideally they'd all just come back with the req undeletion.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
rcg → req code children
rcg → text refs

value.lit: Also we should be able to record a reason for deletion. For example I might select 10 things, delete them all at once and give the reason “FR-201 makes this redundant” or something.

h4.lit: Why?

value.lit: Because it's not always going to be obvious why something is deleted. You might search for something, find it dead and not be able to think of a reason why it shouldn't be alive, then you could undelete it without understanding that it's incompatible with another requirement.

h4.lit: What would you do if the system didn't support this?

value.lit: I guess I'd edit requirements before I delete them so include the reason. But that would be super annoying when deleting an entire block of reqs.

There have been some major changes that affect all requirements pertaining to deletion. Project storage is now via an append-only event-stream. As such history is never lost and can easily be made accessible to the user.
Deletion subjects:

ol.lit: Config
◦ Tags
* Issue types
* Fields
* Req types
Content
* Generic requirements
* Req code groups

h4.lit: Why would you delete a tag?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things. They often imprecise.

change all the time; after seeing how statuses are being used one might decide to merge some, remove some; tags representing components can be changed to cover different subsets of a solution. It's also why we need to see usage – tags that rarely get used are often potential candidates for deletion.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means it shouldn't be used anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: It should be removed from existing data. Simple enough when it's just attached to something but it's a bit more involving when it's being used in text, like “Machine
do ...”. In those cases you can't just remove it, you need to either change it to another tag that fits or reword it.

h4.lit: What if you couldn't delete tags?

value.lit: I'd have to rename them stupidly to avoid people using them anymore. I'd want them to display in some kind of obvious way, like big and red so that BAs could see that they are obviously wrong. In fact, maybe they could be part of the issue system, like how other types of issues are reported and easily actionable.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Maybe if it's still in use – because then it's not really deleted. And the intent of tag deletion is removal of use.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things like I said. Plus sometimes there's trial and error involved. You want tags to be decently distributed; you might delete stuff, add new tags, allocate them and realise you're worse off.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd need to add the tag back (quick) and then re-tag everything (annoying).

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
tag → parent tag
tag → child tags
tag → field
tag → req/code-group tag-pool
tag → req/code-group text

h4.lit: Why would you delete an issue type?

value.lit: Like tags, it's just a fickle idea to reduce data into subsets.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means the issue shouldn't be usable anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Those issues either need to change type, or be removed.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete?

value.lit: Same as tags. Rename stupidly. Or actually, rename it the next time I want to create a new issue type.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: If it's in use still – because something needs to be done about those issues.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same as tags.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: Add it back (quick). Maybe change some existing issues to use it again.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code: issue type → req/code-group text

ShipReq as it stands is currently only useful for 1-person projects. Two people can't work together unless they share the same login, which is terrible for everyone including me. It's critical that I add multi user support.

I need a team to be able to use ShipReq collaboratively. It can be really simple at first: just allow the project owner to add/remove users to a collaborators list. Once on the list, a collaborator will see the project in their projects list and be able to work on it just like the owner. Simple.

Another expansion of the simplicity would be to allow the transference of ownership. I don't know if that means the new owner would need to accept ownership first but whatever, it's more about enabling capability at this point to get the business running. Plenty of time for goldplating and improvement once I've got real users, I mean features that won't be used until I've got a good bunch of users needn't be built now. Btw, Whatsapp has a simple model wrt to group chats. One or more admins, transfer as much as you like. Leave and ownership transfers to a random collaborator. Simple & easy.
So once multiple users can work on a project together it then introduces a bunch of new requirements. Maybe these should be separate but still, here are the thoughts:
If I'm working on a project with someone, I'm going to want to know what changes they're making. Potential solutions are:

ul.lit: audit trail
diff of project between T₁ (eg. when I last logged in), and T₂ (eg. now) – I mean actual diff, not list of events

value.lit: Also want to communicate with other collaborators. Potential ideas: inline chat, tag them in places to leave them inline msgs. Maybe have columns (fields) with user(s) as values to represent assignees; in which case will probably want to have a many-to-many acyclic tree like tags so that user groups can exist and fields can be constrained by upper bounds (eg. assigned testers vs devs vs clients). Whatever the combination of solutions, when I login I want to be able to quickly see <whatever>: if I have any unread chat msgs, if I've been recently tagged, where and why I'm still tagged, what shit I've been assigned, etc. I want it to be quick and easy. If I have to go to ReqTable and write my own filters then that's not quick and easy.
Will definitely need new filter capabilities. Example:{createdBy,updatedBy,containsRefTo} {me,@blah}
An interesting side-effect of multiusernessness is that it introduces the consideration of personal vs global data/config. The one example is saved views.

ul.lit: Users should be able to create their own saved views
Users shouldn't have to see everyone else's
Users (eg admin) should be able to create saved views for the team

value.lit: What else is there apart from saved views?
Most of the thought above considers multiple authors. Not all users are req authors though. Other user classes that jump out are:

ul.lit: devs
testers
clients / product owner

value.lit: Full detail here is probably going to far; there's no need to plan the entire roadmap right now. Some quick high-level thoughts now, detail later...

ul.lit: devs & testers – want to know what's assigned to them, see what version, and modify the status & assignee
devs, maybe testers, maybe clients – probably want notifications when certain reqs change
clients – want to know the overall/high-level status, eg. how is MF-1 progressing, how much is done, pending, what releases will it go in to
everyone – for scope X, who made certain changes (i.e. if a req changes and even everyone gets notifications, great, but also need to know who made changes so they can talk to them if problem)
everyone – suggest/demand changes but not make it themselves
everyone – ask questions about a req?

value.lit: Now let's talk about audit trails. Users need to be able to see them, they'll be long so also filter and scroll around. Users need to be able to view the entire project audit trail, but it's also important the each req display the relevant events in ReqDetail. What I mean by that is if you open up a req in ReqDetail, down the bottom there should be a read-only field that shows a subset of the global audit trail that are events that have impacted the selected req (like the history tab in jira). It might be annoying to always display the list because it could be very long and distracting, maybe the field could have a little button saying “show” or something?
It also might be a common case to want to see all changes made by other users since the current user's last change.

So looking at a big list is going to be useful sometimes but other times it's going to be useless because there's too much repetitive (and intermediate) information. In such cases a user needs the ability to see a diff between revisions. The user should be able to choose the start/end revs.
Also, once we have read-only mode available, user should be able to view the project at an old revision.

Baselining is a common thing is requirements. Our audit trail means we're a bit like git for requirements. We can tag revisions and see changes since, etc. I'm not sure if users should be allowed to modify revision tags, probably – ShipReq isn't a preventative product. Probably there should be permissioning at the project level and maybe even the tag level to declare who can and can't modify tags once they're created. Notifications are out of scope right now but maybe in future all relevant parties will be notified when a tag is modified. In fact, tagging should be part of the audit trail (unlike Git).

Treating ShipReq like git leads to an obvious question: do we allow branching? DEFINATELY not in the medium term, I've got no fucking customers. But in future... maybe? An idea is that requirement existence (i.e. IDs) exist globally, but are included in some branches. This means that merging a branch doesn't result in multiple reqs fighting for the same ids, and it also captures an interesting aspect of the reality in that when you focus on a branch, the other stuff still exists in reality, it's just not part of the branch you're looking at. But merge conflicts would be inevitable. Don't want to have to think about that right now.

This means that merging a branch doesn't result in multiple reqs fighting for the same ids, and it also captures an interesting aspect of the reality in that when you focus on a branch, the other stuff still exists in reality, it's just not part of the branch you're looking at. But merge conflicts would be inevitable. Don't want to have to think about that right now.

value.lit: Consider forward implication when calculating imp fields
Requirement Relationships (i.e. implication)

Deepti was entering requirements following Chen's process where you do interviews first, then create MFs after analysing them. In that case you have IV → MF but her IV's MF field was blank (and showing as an issue) because imp field calculation only considers backwards-implication, not forwards. Thinking about this it seems to make sense that forward-implication be considered too. The reason I'm not just gonna jump in and implement it is that I'm concerned about transitivity. Should it be transitive? Seems like it definitely should, but then what about large, complex trees? Will there be problems? Needs analysis.... And here we are.

h4.lit: Will there be problems with transitive forward implication?

value.lit: Let's just consider some scenarios:

ul.lit: IV-1 should definitely have MF-1 as in it's MF field
As above, IV-2 should definitely have MF-1 as in it's MF field, it seems logical that IV-1 should too. eg.

//.lit: "here's how we can solve X, with MF-1!"

ul.lit: → MF-1
and then
With transitive forward-implication being considered we end up with

cb.code:
FB1 - MF1,2
  FB2 - MF1
  FB3 - ∅. INTERESTING!! should be MF1 because FR1's MF is MF1 via backward-implication
  IV1 - MF1,2
  IV2 - MF1,2
  UC2 - MF1,2
  MF1 - should be off but if not, should only be self. DANGEROUS would be if MF-2 were included because UC2→MF1, and UC2 has MF1,2 in it's MF field
  MF2 - should be off but if not, should only be self. DANGEROUS would be if MF-1 were included because UC2→MF2, and UC2 has MF1,2 in it's MF field

h4.lit: What's the precise new rule?

value.lit: Let's do it step by step...
Firstly, what's the expectation?
Well it was quite hard to think about this in a vacuum so I sat down with Deepti and her dad to go through it. What really, really helped was giving some reqs some realistic meanings as follows:

ul.lit: MF1 - Allow users to upload document
MF4 - Provide users with self-hosted email accounts
FR1 - Users must login
FB3 - Requirements from security guys' about logging in
FB4 - Requirements from security guys' about logging in

value.lit: This makes it much easier to see that FB3 and FB4 should be associated with MF1 and MF4.

It also (finally) provides an conceptual intuition for why the current implication field calculation is good, but doesn't go far enough. Basically an MF forms an umbrella that houses all of its subtasks (i.e. forwards-facing implication tree). What we're missing is that any reqs
of that umbrella that imply something
the umbrella, need to also be in scope (i.e. be associated with the MF). The exception is external MFs that imply reqs in scope, are to be excluded and no traversed for further implications.

**.lit: It makes so much sense and I'm so happy this has been discovered.

UC2 - 1,2
MF1 - 1
MF2 - 2,3   ERROR: cycle

MF4 - 4

h4.lit: What's the precise new rule?

value.lit: Well it was quite hard to think about this in a vacuum so I sat down with Deepti and her dad to go through it. What really, really helped was giving some reqs some realistic meanings as follows:

ul.lit: MF1 - Allow users to upload document
MF4 - Provide users with self-hosted email accounts
FR1 - Users must login
FB3 - Requirements from security guys' about logging in
FB4 - Requirements from security guys' about logging in

value.lit: This makes it much easier to see that FB3 and FB4 should be associated with MF1 and MF4.

It also (finally) provides an conceptual intuition for why the current implication field calculation is good, but doesn't go far enough. Basically an MF forms an umbrella that houses all of its subtasks (i.e. forwards-facing implication tree). What we're missing is that any reqs
of that umbrella that imply something
the umbrella, need to also be in scope (i.e. be associated with the MF). The exception is external MFs that imply reqs in scope, are to be excluded and no traversed for further implications.

**.lit: It makes so much sense and I'm so happy this has been discovered.

h4.lit: What else will this change affect?

ul.lit: - all imp-field values (eg. MF)

be present in the graph. Especially now that the rule has become more complicated, it's even more important that users understand how those values came to be there. Otherwise, there would end up being cases where users are confused as to why a value is in their imp field, so much so that they may think they're looking at a bug.
) - this hasn't been analysed yet so I'll consider it later

make sense. Both MFs are relevant to each other and it's helpful to be shown when filtering by one or the other because it adds important, contextual information (which is a huge, core value of ShipReq). If I'm looking at MF-3 it's good to have a reminding that it's actually a subtask of a larger context.

Deepti was entering requirements following Chen's process where you do interviews first, then create MFs after analysing them. In that case you have IV → MF but her IV's MF field was blank (and showing as an issue) because imp field calculation only considers backwards-implication, not forwards. Thinking about this it seems to make sense that forward-implication be considered too. The reason I'm not just gonna jump in and implement it is that I'm concerned about transitivity. Should it be transitive? Seems like it definitely should, but then what about large, complex trees? Will there be problems? Needs analysis.... And here we are.

title.lit: GitHub integration (prelim)

value.lit: ShipReq should integration with Github in order to be very helpful and appealing to developers and dev teams.

h4.lit: Why?

value.lit: It's a pain in the ass for devs to log in to ShipReq (or JIRA or whatever) to update tasks every time they complete something. Very error prone too. Github know this and for their own issues, allow you to type in things like "Fixes <ID>" in commit messages in which case they will automatically closed issues on push (into
). In our case, instead of "closing" issues, certain verbs will add and/or remove certain tags from reqs. This will become insanely powerful, and insanely beneficial to users, when combined with

as it will allow devs to update entire ecosystems of reqs from single tag changes on commits. The fact that these features don't exist at the moment make it very hard for me to keep my requirements' statuses up-to-date, which prevents me from understanding what is and isn't complete and where I'm up to.

h4.lit: How do you see it being used?

ol.lit: I've already mentioned modifying reqs' status via git commit messages.

The second thing that comes to mind is simply references. Anytime a req is mentioned in a commit message (in a special format maybe?), that commit and its details should then be shown in a field in ReqDetail. I'm not sure exactly what details. One that might be super useful would be if ShipReq always showed you what branches the commits were currently in. That way it'd be really easy to see if they'd been merged (or even released if tags included as well as just branches). Holy shit, that would be awesome actually. Back on Harold, the testers (and admin) would never know what was released to prod, what was in UAT, etc. Killer!!!

An idea that just came to mind is scanning code itself. I wonder if that would be useful? Sounds pretty computationally heavy though, so given that it's an idea I'm unsure has value, it should be very low priority.
Ideally the integration would go in the other direction as well, and turn references in commits on Github into links back to the reqs in ShipReq.
An idea from the JIRA integration repo: we should also convert refs in Github issue text to links to ShipReq.

value.lit: Maybe there are other cool and useful things but there is a burning need for first two points above and I think the focus should be just on those two points, and then anything else can come later with much, much less urgency.

h4.lit: Other

value.lit: This feature should really be just

rather than just Github. Gitlab is the 2nd most popular and should be supported next. To that end, there should be a bunch of service-agnostic endpoints on ShipReq that any service can talk to. Hell, it should even be publicly-documented so that anyone can integrate anything they want once ShipReq gets traction.
It also

make sense to try to make this VCS-agnostic too. Actually, nah, the idea that the subset of paying users I'm going to be getting in the next 2 years are going to need a VCS other than Git integrated in completely unrealistic.
This will need to be highly-configurable. There are so many different git workflows out there, and even so many different ways of using ShipReq. Users should be able to completely configure:

ul.lit: which branches/tags are monitored per context
the commit verbs and actions
which Git users correspond to which ShipReq users. Should be many-to-many. (Eg. of why not many-to-one = pair programming)

value.lit: How to handle commits/tags that later disappear?

I just realised! In addition to git commits, PRs should also receive the same (similar?) treatment! For example, the git commits might contain no info about ShipReq but a PR might be created that says something like

or whatever. Maybe the concept should remain the same except commits and PRs should be deduped by removing the commits that are already covered by the PR. Similarly, maybe if all commits in a PR refer to the same req, then the PR should be added (and then the commits removed) so just the PR is shown. (?)
And similarly, if enabled by configuration, ShipReq should also show the Github issues that reference a req. This is lower priority.

h4.lit: Key takeaways

ul.lit: Certain verbs should add and/or remove certain tags from reqs
Certain verbs should only take affect when they appear on certain branches
References from ShipReq to commits should be shown regardless of the branch
If possible, turn refs in Github into links back to ShipReq
handle commits/tags that later disappear

which, as of
, has different logic.

value.lit: Offline mode

ul.lit: Make ShipReq work when offline
A user should be able to bring their laptop to a sprint planning meeting, do all of their work without internet, go back to their desk, and have ShipReq upload all the changes.
Conflict resolution will need to be possible

value.lit: Not being able to get working internet in meeting rooms used to happen all the fucking time. To be able to tell a user they can do all of their work offline (without losing any functionality) is a huge selling point.

ul.lit: Make ShipReq work when offline
A user should be able to bring their laptop to a sprint planning meeting, do all of their work without internet, go back to their desk, and have ShipReq upload all the changes.
Conflict resolution will need to be possible
Ideally users should also be able to reload the tab / open new tabs without internet connectivity

value.lit: Collapsible trees (prelim)

The need for this feature comes about pretty quickly once you've got more than a non-trivial amount of requirements in the system. When everything is in a flat list/grid, it's so hard to know what the hell is going on. I'm not gonna sit there and read everything (which means I'm not going to read anything cos understanding one line doesn't help me understand the est - it doesn't scale). I know for a fact that all of my content is hierarchical. Hierarchies are a great solution to this problem because it would allow me to look at the bunch of high-level stuff, and then drill into detail as I desire. So the abstract feature I need is comprised of the following:

ol.lit: present a DAG/tree of content so that the DAG/tree structure is visible to the user
allow me to {collapse,expand} {single,multiple,all} rows in the req table
allow the DAG/tree structure to be configurable - eg. implication, req codes, statuses

value.lit: Status is an interesting one. I might want to view all my content grouped by MF and then status. Something like this:

cb.code:
+ MF-1
- MF-2
  + needs_analysis (4) (25%)
  - ready_for_dev (4) (25%)
    - FR-1
    - FR-8
    - FR-11
    - FR-13
  + implemented (8) (50%)
+ MF-3
+ MF-4

value.lit: I don't know if this will overlap with
or not, but it would also be pretty nice if I could see the status breakdown in a single cell without having to expand the MFs. An idea could be just a coloured bar. Example:

ul.lit: So it looks like tags are like
s that can be nested.
Req codes are obvious (in isolation at least)
Implication tree is a must.

I just wonder about imp-field values (now that they're different from imp-tree). I think it's a "must" too. Actually they definitely are. If I'm interested in grouping by MF, I don't care how the implication was specified, I just want to see everything in that umbrella.

h4.lit: ReqTable?

value.lit: I'm pretty sure this should go into ReqTable.

ol.lit: It's purpose is bulk / multiple reqs. This feature adheres to that.
If I made a completely different screen I'd still want to have all of the req table functionality anyway (especially it's flexibility in letting me see specific aspects of reqs in bulk)

The view would have to be drastically different to justify adding a new screen. That means not a grid. Maybe there is some need for this but it would be insanity to only do that and not modify the ReqTable. It would mean users would find exactly what they're looking for on one screen and then have to manually try to reconstruct the same filter on the ReqTable. Yuk.

to address that.

The next part is the most troublesome. Whatever reqs I just directly updated always have a big implication tree behind them. In order to keep all those reqs' status up-to-date, I have to manually access each one and update them.

value.lit: It's just not feasible.

ul.lit: it's tedious
it's time-consuming
I've forgotten a lot so I need to do lots of reading and thinking
it's error-prone -- so, so easy to make a mistake
I often come back later to ShipReq to see (ask) what the current state is, not to tell it what it is

value.lit: Automating this would not only make users' lives so much easier, but be a significant selling point seeing as I don't know of anything else that does this.

It'd have to be very flexible too. I guess there's a lot to work out because I have no idea how the solution's going to work. All I know is that I want to update a few leaf reqs, then look at the tree to see what effect it's had.

value.lit: So thinking about what we need to implement this:

ul.lit: there needs to be some configurable way of combining tags. Partial semigroup rules + defaulting to order = full semigroup and might be a great idea. Could even show the user a table of
this should be opt-in. Maybe per tag-field, or per tag?

Users should be able to copy/paste rules. Why? Smart/power users manually clicking around is one thing, but there might be a bunch of cases where you want to share config with someone or other projects of your own; users might want to backup their config.

in line with core ShipReq values, users need to be able to see a derived tag, and understand why that's the case. That means that even if we have a derived tag field with just a single value in it, users should be able to inspect it somehow (just hover?) and understand 1) that it was derived, 2) how it came to be, and 3) what the quantities were.

  #b     -  6 (2 SIs, 4 FRs)
  #c     -  1 (SI)
  #d     -  1 (SI)
  no tag -  1 (SI)

Calculation / Derivation
  = #a + (#b + (#c + #d))
  = #a + (#b + #d)
  = #a + #f
  = #d

value.lit: So thinking about what we need to implement this:

ul.lit: there needs to be some configurable way of combining tags. Partial semigroup rules + defaulting to order = full semigroup and might be a great idea. Could even show the user a table of
this should be opt-in. Maybe per tag-field, or per tag?

Users should be able to copy/paste rules. Why? Smart/power users manually clicking around is one thing, but there might be a bunch of cases where you want to share config with someone or other projects of your own; users might want to backup their config.

in line with core ShipReq values, users need to be able to see a derived tag, and understand why that's the case. That means that even if we have a derived tag field with just a single value in it, users should be able to inspect it somehow (just hover?) and understand 1) that it was derived, 2) how it came to be, and 3) what the quantities were.
