The need for this feature comes about pretty quickly once you've got more than a non-trivial amount of requirements in the system. When everything is in a flat list/grid, it's so hard to know what the hell is going on. I'm not gonna sit there and read everything (which means I'm not going to read anything cos understanding one line doesn't help me understand the rest - it doesn't scale). I know for a fact that all of my content is hierarchical. Hierarchies are a great solution to this problem because it would allow me to look at the bunch of high-level stuff, and then drill into detail as I desire. So the abstract feature I need is comprised of the following:

ol.lit: present a DAG/tree of content so that the DAG/tree structure is visible to the user
allow me to {collapse,expand} {single,multiple,all} rows in the req table
allow the DAG/tree structure to be configurable - eg. implication, req codes, statuses

value.lit: Status is an interesting one. I might want to view all my content grouped by MF and then status. Something like this:

cb.code:
+ MF-1
- MF-2
  + needs_analysis (4) (25%)
  - ready_for_dev (4) (25%)
    - FR-1
    - FR-8
    - FR-11
    - FR-13
  + implemented (8) (50%)
+ MF-3
+ MF-4

value.lit: I don't know if this will overlap with
or not, but it would also be pretty nice if I could see the status breakdown in a single cell without having to expand the MFs. An idea could be just a coloured bar. Example:
So, it looks like:

ul.lit: tags are like
s that can be nested
Req codes are obvious (in isolation at least)
Implication tree is a must.

I just wonder about imp-field values (now that they're different from imp-tree). I think it's a "must" too. Actually they definitely are. If I'm interested in grouping by MF, I don't care how the implication was specified, I just want to see everything in that umbrella.
Tag groupings should be composable with imp-tree/field groupings (eg. version and then MF-tree)
Req types should be grouping criteria too

h4.lit: ReqTable?

value.lit: I'm pretty sure this should go into ReqTable.

ol.lit: It's purpose is bulk / multiple reqs. This feature adheres to that.
If I made a completely different screen I'd still want to have all of the req table functionality anyway (especially it's flexibility in letting me see specific aspects of reqs in bulk)

The view would have to be drastically different to justify adding a new screen. That means not a grid. Maybe there is some need for this but it would be insanity to only do that and not modify the ReqTable. It would mean users would find exactly what they're looking for on one screen and then have to manually try to reconstruct the same filter on the ReqTable. Yuk.

value.lit: Collapsible trees (Q& A)

h3.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that the new feature could cause?

ul.lit: I see a potential conflict between where I imagine the collapse/expand buttons will go, and the existing row-selection checkboxes
I'm worried about screen clutter - where will the collapse/expand-all buttons go? what about the grouping criteria?
I'm worried about the increased complexity for users
If we have a constraint that the grouping criteria is also the leading sorting criteria, that might simplify things

h3.lit: What aspect most excites you?

value.lit: It will be really nice to be able to browse. I'm looking forward to that.

But honestly, the idea of the tag-percentage bars makes me actually excited. I love it! It's gonna be a really nice, quick easy way to gauge progress (usually of an MF). A huge part of the goals of ShipReq are to be able to have a shit-ton of complex data, and be able to work with it, and comprehend it really quickly and easily.

h3.lit: What aspect will be most valuable to you? Least valuable?

value.lit: The most valuable aspects:

ul.lit: being able to see what's there, what's not, really quickly
being able to see where things are up to, and understand what the next steps are
being able to read requirements as easily as a book, but tree-structured so one can focus on their area of interest
being able to select my own aspect (eg. some tag of mine) and understand the breakdown in specific contexts

value.lit: The least:

ul.lit: I don't know. Maybe none? I can't think of anything non-critical.

h3.lit: What is the real reason for wanting to solve this problem?

value.lit: Enabling quick comprehensibility for users.

h3.lit: Can we copy something that already exists?

value.lit: Not really. When sorting by req-code we generate a tree in a column but we need much more than that, and that solution is really simple and hard-coded to req codes. We'd actually have to discard a bunch of that functionality and re-implement req code trees using this new feature.

h3.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution? What are the expectations?

value.lit: I should be easily able to browse my requirements in a structured way. Not very quantitative but I'm having this problem with ShipReq requirements themselves in ShipReq, so I've got real data and I'm a real user. My own experience with the new feature will be enough to gauge it's success.
I expect that I'll be able to look at all reqs in the req table (i.e. no filter), and see a reasonable amount of rows.
Working on v2.2; what am I doing? How am I going? What's next?

ol.lit: Rows will mostly be MFs. I'd sort by a version tag so that the stuff I'm interested in right now would be in the middle (between done and postponed). In fact I would ONLY want to see MFs, I wouldn't want to see interviews even if they imply MFs. I'd rather just see MFs, then expand an MF to see the IV that implied it.

Once I click an MF I'm not sure if I want to see a flat list, or whether it should be a tree by implication. There might be cases where the flat list sorted by a status tag would be useful. But really, if

is done then looking at just the next level in the implication tree should be enough because those nodes should have a status (or status combo bar) that represents the status of everything in that tree. In which case I'd just browse the few I cared about that had certain statuses. In fact, I may even want to filter a few statuses out, else it might be too noisy. YES! Actually, let's say I filter out "rejected", I'd want "rejected" removed from status bars, and I'd want nodes that are 100% rejected to be removed entirely. Holy shit that would be beautiful and powerful.
I could add some filters (like tag filters described above), and filter MF's by version, save it as a view and I'd easily be able to solve my problem of not knowing where I'm up to in my work.

Depending on my mood, I might prefer to pick up a dev task or an analysis task. I should be able to add a tag filter and enjoy all the same benefits as above. Really it's the same need: scope, browse, comprehend.

I'm worried about the increased complexity for users
If we have a constraint that the grouping criteria is also the leading sorting criteria, that might simplify things

h3.lit: Should the collapse/expand state be saved as part of saved views?

value.lit: That's a tricky one. I don't think it's a good idea to retain collapse state literally by ReqId or similar because it's too fickle, plus what about when new reqs are added? Manually expand and re-save all the time? That strongly goes against ShipReq's core values of no-busywork.

We could default to all collapsed or all expanded. Easy. But which would we choose? There are pros and cons to both. Actually this might be an area where it'd be smart to just pick a default, and leave it alone until we have opposing customers. After all there's an expand/collapse all button for if anyone wants the opposite of the default. And seeing as the goal of this is simplification and gradual, targeted increases in information, defaulting to AllCollapsed makes the most sense.

Otherwise, we could try to smart about it. If everything's expanded when we save state, we save it as AllExpanded. Vica-versa for collapsed. When the tree is partially expanded we could just retain whatever the previous value was. I'm not sure it's worth it though and we could even make this change later as an intermediate step between no-control and full-control.

h3.lit: Does this feature need to wait until
is analysed or implemented?

value.lit: Technically, I don't think so. Derivative tags will only affect the value of tag fields. Even when we're grouping by tag and showing tag combo bars, that's not affected by how tag fields get their content.

introduces some new quantum/multi state that tag fields can be in. But even then, we already support multiple tags per field, so we'd only be affected if tags in tag fields were to start having weights (eg. "34 done, 3 pending" as opposed to just "done pending").
So actually,
. Let's just wait until that specific detail has been analysed.

h3.lit: Will you need keyboard shortcuts for collapse/expand?

value.lit: For each row, absolutely. Not sure about all. We should add it for good measure. And I guess now we'll need to start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help modal, or maybe just show keyboard shortcuts on hover? Could do both. Actually, let's definitely start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help page; there may be shortcuts in future where adding a hover isn't feasible. Plus it's a standard practice. Could put it next to "Send Feedback" in the menu.

h3.lit: How will imp-field trees differ from imp-trees?

value.lit: Imp trees are unidirectional. The tree's will only look forward. Actually that's not tree, we should allow backwards looking trees too, simply for flexibility. I'm not sure when that would ever come in handy though.... Actually, maybe when I'm focused on a single req and want to start browsing back up the tree to understand something about it. I've done this before but I had to keep clicking on reqs in the req detail imp graph page to find what I wanted. So let's definitely allow backwards facing trees. If it's really useful we can even add a button to the req detail page that when clicked, takes you to the req table page, filters only on that req, expands all and let's you browse it's reverse imp tree.

Holy shit, how do we now distinguish top-level filters from tree children filters??! Do we need to? I hope not. Let's see.... we should actually just be able to filter by "implies:ReqId" in the above scenario. Yeah we should be fine with just one filter.
So that's normal imp trees but what about imp-field trees. The formula has changed since
So for the top level rows, (immediate) backwards implication should as if it's forward. So
should be as if it were
. What about transitivity? What about forward-facing tree on external sources?

value.lit: Not really. When sorting by req-code we generate a tree in a column but we need much more than that, and that solution is really simple and hard-coded to req codes. We'd actually have to discard a bunch of that functionality and re-implement req code trees using this new feature.

h3.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution? What are the expectations?

value.lit: I should be easily able to browse my requirements in a structured way. Not very quantitative but I'm having this problem with ShipReq requirements themselves in ShipReq, so I've got real data and I'm a real user. My own experience with the new feature will be enough to gauge it's success.
I expect that I'll be able to look at all reqs in the req table (i.e. no filter), and see a reasonable amount of rows.
Working on v2.2; what am I doing? How am I going? What's next?

ol.lit: Rows will mostly be MFs. I'd sort by a version tag so that the stuff I'm interested in right now would be in the middle (between done and postponed). In fact I would ONLY want to see MFs, I wouldn't want to see interviews even if they imply MFs. I'd rather just see MFs, then expand an MF to see the IV that implied it.

Once I click an MF I'm not sure if I want to see a flat list, or whether it should be a tree by implication. There might be cases where the flat list sorted by a status tag would be useful. But really, if

is done then looking at just the next level in the implication tree should be enough because those nodes should have a status (or status combo bar) that represents the status of everything in that tree. In which case I'd just browse the few I cared about that had certain statuses. In fact, I may even want to filter a few statuses out, else it might be too noisy. YES! Actually, let's say I filter out "rejected", I'd want "rejected" removed from status bars, and I'd want nodes that are 100% rejected to be removed entirely. Holy shit that would be beautiful and powerful.
I could add some filters (like tag filters described above), and filter MF's by version, save it as a view and I'd easily be able to solve my problem of not knowing where I'm up to in my work.

Depending on my mood, I might prefer to pick up a dev task or an analysis task. I should be able to add a tag filter and enjoy all the same benefits as above. Really it's the same need: scope, browse, comprehend.

h3.lit: Should the collapse/expand state be saved as part of saved views?

value.lit: That's a tricky one. I don't think it's a good idea to retain collapse state literally by ReqId or similar because it's too fickle, plus what about when new reqs are added? Manually expand and re-save all the time? That strongly goes against ShipReq's core values of no-busywork.

We could default to all collapsed or all expanded. Easy. But which would we choose? There are pros and cons to both. Actually this might be an area where it'd be smart to just pick a default, and leave it alone until we have opposing customers. After all there's an expand/collapse all button for if anyone wants the opposite of the default. And seeing as the goal of this is simplification and gradual, targeted increases in information, defaulting to AllCollapsed makes the most sense.

Otherwise, we could try to smart about it. If everything's expanded when we save state, we save it as AllExpanded. Vica-versa for collapsed. When the tree is partially expanded we could just retain whatever the previous value was. I'm not sure it's worth it though and we could even make this change later as an intermediate step between no-control and full-control.

h3.lit: Does this feature need to wait until
is analysed or implemented?

value.lit: Technically, I don't think so. Derivative tags will only affect the value of tag fields. Even when we're grouping by tag and showing tag combo bars, that's not affected by how tag fields get their content.

introduces some new quantum/multi state that tag fields can be in. But even then, we already support multiple tags per field, so we'd only be affected if tags in tag fields were to start having weights (eg. "34 done, 3 pending" as opposed to just "done pending").
So actually,
. Let's just wait until that specific detail has been analysed.

h3.lit: Will you need keyboard shortcuts for collapse/expand?

value.lit: For each row, absolutely. Not sure about all. We should add it for good measure. And I guess now we'll need to start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help modal, or maybe just show keyboard shortcuts on hover? Could do both. Actually, let's definitely start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help page; there may be shortcuts in future where adding a hover isn't feasible. Plus it's a standard practice. Could put it next to "Send Feedback" in the menu.

h3.lit: How will imp-field trees differ from imp-trees?

value.lit: Imp trees are unidirectional. The tree's will only look forward. Actually that's not tree, we should allow backwards looking trees too, simply for flexibility. I'm not sure when that would ever come in handy though.... Actually, maybe when I'm focused on a single req and want to start browsing back up the tree to understand something about it. I've done this before but I had to keep clicking on reqs in the req detail imp graph page to find what I wanted. So let's definitely allow backwards facing trees. If it's really useful we can even add a button to the req detail page that when clicked, takes you to the req table page, filters only on that req, expands all and let's you browse it's reverse imp tree.

Holy shit, how do we now distinguish top-level filters from tree children filters??! Do we need to? I hope not. Let's see.... we should actually just be able to filter by "implies:ReqId" in the above scenario. Yeah we should be fine with just one filter.
So that's normal imp trees but what about imp-field trees. The formula has changed since
So for the top level rows, (immediate) backwards implication should as if it's forward. So
should be as if it were
. What about transitivity? What about forward-facing tree on external sources?
Say we had:

cb.code:
node[style=filled fillcolor="#88ee00"]
MF1 [label="MF1\n(1)"]
MF2 [label="MF2\n(2)"]
MF3 [label="MF3\n(3)"]
node [fillcolor="#dddddd"]
FR1 [label="FR1\n(1,2)"]
IV1 [label="IV1\n(1)"]
IV2 [label="IV2\n(1,2)"]
IV3 [label="IV3\n(1,3)"]
IV4 [label="IV4\n(3)"]

IV3 -> IV1 -> MF1 -> FR1
MF2 -> IV2 -> FR1
IV3 -> IV4 -> MF3

value.lit: I expect when I filter req table in flat mode by impField=X1, every row I see should be somewhere in the tree of X1 when it's top-level and I'm grouping by impField:X.
Meaning that the following reqs must be under their MF trees:
I expect to see

cb.code:
* MF1
  * IV1 (backwards)
    * IV3 (backwards)
      * IV4 ????????????????????????????? no
  * FR1
    * IV2 (required by property)
* MF2
  * IV2
    * FR1
      * MF1 ????????????????????????????? no
* MF3
  * IV4
    * IV3
      * IV1 ????????????????????????????? no - it's doesn't have MF3 in its MF field

h3.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that the new feature could cause?

ul.lit: I see a potential conflict between where I imagine the collapse/expand buttons will go, and the existing row-selection checkboxes
I'm worried about screen clutter - where will the collapse/expand-all buttons go? what about the grouping criteria?
I'm worried about the increased complexity for users
If we have a constraint that the grouping criteria is also the leading sorting criteria, that might simplify things

h3.lit: What aspect most excites you?

value.lit: It will be really nice to be able to browse. I'm looking forward to that.

But honestly, the idea of the tag-percentage bars makes me actually excited. I love it! It's gonna be a really nice, quick easy way to gauge progress (usually of an MF). A huge part of the goals of ShipReq are to be able to have a shit-ton of complex data, and be able to work with it, and comprehend it really quickly and easily.

h3.lit: What aspect will be most valuable to you? Least valuable?

value.lit: The most valuable aspects:

ul.lit: being able to see what's there, what's not, really quickly
being able to see where things are up to, and understand what the next steps are
being able to read requirements as easily as a book, but tree-structured so one can focus on their area of interest
being able to select my own aspect (eg. some tag of mine) and understand the breakdown in specific contexts

value.lit: The least:

ul.lit: I don't know. Maybe none? I can't think of anything non-critical.

h4.lit: Should it be illegal for reqs
covered by the above property to be included?

value.lit: Yes. ShipReq logic needs to be completely comprehensible to users. If they group by imp-field and then suddenly expanding MF1 is showing content that doesn't have MF1 in its MF field, then it's going to be confusing, and will probably be considered a bug. Answering yes to this question makes the logic really simple from a user's point of view. There's nothing hidden from them, they can see all the data relevant to explain the logic of this feature.

Collapsible trees (Q & A)

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that the new feature could cause?

ul.lit: I see a potential conflict between where I imagine the collapse/expand buttons will go, and the existing row-selection checkboxes
I'm worried about screen clutter - where will the collapse/expand-all buttons go? what about the grouping criteria?
I'm worried about the increased complexity for users
If we have a constraint that the grouping criteria is also the leading sorting criteria, that might simplify things

value.lit: It will be really nice to be able to browse. I'm looking forward to that.

But honestly, the idea of the tag-percentage bars makes me actually excited. I love it! It's gonna be a really nice, quick easy way to gauge progress (usually of an MF). A huge part of the goals of ShipReq are to be able to have a shit-ton of complex data, and be able to work with it, and comprehend it really quickly and easily.

h2.lit: What aspect will be most valuable to you? Least valuable?

value.lit: The most valuable aspects:

ul.lit: being able to see what's there, what's not, really quickly
being able to see where things are up to, and understand what the next steps are
being able to read requirements as easily as a book, but tree-structured so one can focus on their area of interest
being able to select my own aspect (eg. some tag of mine) and understand the breakdown in specific contexts

value.lit: The least:

ul.lit: I don't know. Maybe none? I can't think of anything non-critical.

h2.lit: What is the real reason for wanting to solve this problem?

value.lit: Enabling quick comprehensibility for users.

h2.lit: Can we copy something that already exists?

value.lit: Not really. When sorting by req-code we generate a tree in a column but we need much more than that, and that solution is really simple and hard-coded to req codes. We'd actually have to discard a bunch of that functionality and re-implement req code trees using this new feature.

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution? What are the expectations?

value.lit: I should be easily able to browse my requirements in a structured way. Not very quantitative but I'm having this problem with ShipReq requirements themselves in ShipReq, so I've got real data and I'm a real user. My own experience with the new feature will be enough to gauge it's success.
I expect that I'll be able to look at all reqs in the req table (i.e. no filter), and see a reasonable amount of rows.
Working on v2.2; what am I doing? How am I going? What's next?

ol.lit: Rows will mostly be MFs. I'd sort by a version tag so that the stuff I'm interested in right now would be in the middle (between done and postponed). In fact I would ONLY want to see MFs, I wouldn't want to see interviews even if they imply MFs. I'd rather just see MFs, then expand an MF to see the IV that implied it.

Once I click an MF I'm not sure if I want to see a flat list, or whether it should be a tree by implication. There might be cases where the flat list sorted by a status tag would be useful. But really, if

is done then looking at just the next level in the implication tree should be enough because those nodes should have a status (or status combo bar) that represents the status of everything in that tree. In which case I'd just browse the few I cared about that had certain statuses. In fact, I may even want to filter a few statuses out, else it might be too noisy. YES! Actually, let's say I filter out "rejected", I'd want "rejected" removed from status bars, and I'd want nodes that are 100% rejected to be removed entirely. Holy shit that would be beautiful and powerful.
I could add some filters (like tag filters described above), and filter MF's by version, save it as a view and I'd easily be able to solve my problem of not knowing where I'm up to in my work.

Depending on my mood, I might prefer to pick up a dev task or an analysis task. I should be able to add a tag filter and enjoy all the same benefits as above. Really it's the same need: scope, browse, comprehend.

h2.lit: Should the collapse/expand state be saved as part of saved views?

value.lit: That's a tricky one. I don't think it's a good idea to retain collapse state literally by ReqId or similar because it's too fickle, plus what about when new reqs are added? Manually expand and re-save all the time? That strongly goes against ShipReq's core values of no-busywork.

We could default to all collapsed or all expanded. Easy. But which would we choose? There are pros and cons to both. Actually this might be an area where it'd be smart to just pick a default, and leave it alone until we have opposing customers. After all there's an expand/collapse all button for if anyone wants the opposite of the default. And seeing as the goal of this is simplification and gradual, targeted increases in information, defaulting to AllCollapsed makes the most sense.

Otherwise, we could try to smart about it. If everything's expanded when we save state, we save it as AllExpanded. Vica-versa for collapsed. When the tree is partially expanded we could just retain whatever the previous value was. I'm not sure it's worth it though and we could even make this change later as an intermediate step between no-control and full-control.

h2.lit: Does this feature need to wait until

value.lit: Technically, I don't think so. Derivative tags will only affect the value of tag fields. Even when we're grouping by tag and showing tag combo bars, that's not affected by how tag fields get their content.

introduces some new quantum/multi state that tag fields can be in. But even then, we already support multiple tags per field, so we'd only be affected if tags in tag fields were to start having weights (eg. "34 done, 3 pending" as opposed to just "done pending").
So actually,
. Let's just wait until that specific detail has been analysed.

h2.lit: Will you need keyboard shortcuts for collapse/expand?

value.lit: For each row, absolutely. Not sure about all. We should add it for good measure. And I guess now we'll need to start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help modal, or maybe just show keyboard shortcuts on hover? Could do both. Actually, let's definitely start maintaining a keyboard shortcut help page; there may be shortcuts in future where adding a hover isn't feasible. Plus it's a standard practice. Could put it next to "Send Feedback" in the menu.

h2.lit: How will imp-field trees differ from imp-trees?

value.lit: Imp trees are unidirectional. The tree's will only look forward. Actually that's not tree, we should allow backwards looking trees too, simply for flexibility. I'm not sure when that would ever come in handy though.... Actually, maybe when I'm focused on a single req and want to start browsing back up the tree to understand something about it. I've done this before but I had to keep clicking on reqs in the req detail imp graph page to find what I wanted. So let's definitely allow backwards facing trees. If it's really useful we can even add a button to the req detail page that when clicked, takes you to the req table page, filters only on that req, expands all and let's you browse it's reverse imp tree.

Holy shit, how do we now distinguish top-level filters from tree children filters??! Do we need to? I hope not. Let's see.... we should actually just be able to filter by "implies:ReqId" in the above scenario. Yeah we should be fine with just one filter.
So that's normal imp trees but what about imp-field trees. The formula has changed since

should be as if it were
. What about transitivity? What about forward-facing tree on external sources?

value.lit: Derivative tags (Q & A)

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

What should happen when a child has multiple tags?
We've studied derivative statuses, what about versions?

subject tag field vs anything else?

Req table is a different kettle of fish because conciseness matters.
Hell we could even use the cell
to render the bar without padding. That's pretty concise.

h2.lit: Should it be illegal for reqs
covered by the above property to be included?

value.lit: Yes. ShipReq logic needs to be completely comprehensible to users. If they group by imp-field and then suddenly expanding MF1 is showing content that doesn't have MF1 in its MF field, then it's going to be confusing, and will probably be considered a bug. Answering yes to this question makes the logic really simple from a user's point of view. There's nothing hidden from them, they can see all the data relevant to explain the logic of this feature.

h2.lit: What if we rendered backwards tree forwards from their furthest, relevant leaf?

value.lit: Let's look at a real example.

cb.code:
[backwards ok]
* MF-13: Requirement Relationships (i.e. implication)
  * IV-26: Consider forward implication when calculating imp fields
    * FR-5: ReqTable #must be able to display reqs as a tree by implication
      * UC-1: Create and complete requirements (backwards)
        * WF-2: Requirements gathering and analysis (backwards)

[no backwards]
* MF-13: Requirement Relationships (i.e. implication)
  * IV-26: Consider forward implication when calculating imp fields
    * FR-5: ReqTable #must be able to display reqs as a tree by implication
  * WF-2: Requirements gathering and analysis
    * UC-1: Create and complete requirements
      * FR-5: ReqTable #must be able to display reqs as a tree by implication

value.lit: I think the
case makes the most sense. When I'm browsing there is an expectation that each time I descend a level, the scope gets narrower; I'm
information.
So yeah, done: all tree branches should be forward facing in terms of implication.

h2.lit: Exactly how will nesting group-by criteria will work?
Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.

h2.lit: We've studied derivative statuses, what about versions?

value.lit: Versions are interesting because they kind of go in the opposite direction as statuses.
Consider this case:

cb.code:
node[style=filled fillcolor="#eeeeee"]
MF1 [fillcolor="#99ff00" label="MF1\n(v2)"]
FR1 [fillcolor="#ddaaff" label="FR1\n(v1)"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

value.lit: First of all,

ul.lit: If I removed v2 from MF1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v1
If I removed v1 from FR1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v2

value.lit: Where as we've got a few options with this feature...

ol.lit: Bidirectional propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1,IV1,FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: What's really interesting about this is that we combine manual values with derived values.
Bidirectional propagation (manual trumps)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FR1 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
FR2,IV2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: Backwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
IV1,FR1 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
FB1,MF1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: Forwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
MF1,IV1,IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
//FB1

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

value.lit: Unidirectionalism clearly doesn't work, so it's a toss-up between (1) and (2).

h2.lit: What about propagation directions and manual-merging with statuses?
Will there be any confusion / ambiguity / difficulty in the intersection between derived tags, and default tags?

value.lit: It might be an idea to visually mark synthetic tags so that they're distinguishable from manually-assigned tags.

ul.lit: reqtable vs req detail
subject tag field vs anything else?

value.lit: In req detail (at least), it would be cool to display the final tag value, and then next to it, a colour bar indicating the makeup.
Req table is a different kettle of fish because conciseness matters.
Hell we could even use the cell
to render the bar without padding. That's pretty concise.

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution?

value.lit: For
: this will be successful if I can use ShipReq as I have been, but with the following differences:

ul.lit: when I finish an implementation task, I shouldn't need to update anything else
when I mark a req as analysed, I shouldn't need to update anything else
I should always be able to look at an MF's status to comprehend it's collective state (and obviously that data should be correct).

value.lit: For
, I basically only want to set them on MFs, and have them automatically propagate to everything else.
For any derived tag I need to be able to quickly comprehend why the resulting value was derived.

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences this could cause?

ul.lit: It could be confusing if a req has a manually-assigned status tag but you're expecting it to auto-update
It increases complexity when a users add new tags

h2.lit: Are there any constraints or rules to which the feature must conform?

value.lit: Apart from what was outlined in
, no.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.

h2.lit: We've studied derivative statuses, what about versions?

value.lit: Versions are interesting because they kind of go in the opposite direction as statuses.
Consider this case:

cb.code:
node[style=filled fillcolor="#eeeeee"]
MF1 [fillcolor="#99ff00" label="MF1\n(v2)"]
FR1 [fillcolor="#ddaaff" label="FR1\n(v1)"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

value.lit: First of all,

ul.lit: If I removed v2 from MF1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v1
If I removed v1 from FR1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v2

value.lit: Where as we've got a few options with this feature...

ol.lit: Bidirectional propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1,IV1,FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: What's really interesting about this is that we combine manual values with derived values.
Bidirectional propagation (manual trumps)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FR1 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
FR2,IV2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: Backwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
IV1,FR1 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
FB1,MF1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

ol.lit: Forwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
MF1,IV1,IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
//FB1

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

value.lit: Unidirectionalism clearly doesn't work, so it's a toss-up between (1) and (2).
The questions come down to:

ul.lit: Should FR1 inherit the v2 tag? No. I'm telling you this is coming in v1.
Should MF1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. It's being delivered in both v1 & v2.
Should FB1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. We've no way of knowing what info in FB1 is relevant to the v1 part of MF1, or the v2 part of MF1.

value.lit: Hmmm so we actually want a different solution from the four above, one that looks like this:

cb.code:
FR1 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
FB1,MF1,IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3

value.lit: So the rule seems to be:

ul.lit: Backwards propagation merges with manual, forwards propagation halts at manual

value.lit: Is that just an aspect of versions specifically, or is it universal?

value.lit: Unidirectionalism clearly doesn't work, so it's a toss-up between (1) and (2).
The questions come down to:

ul.lit: Should FR1 inherit the v2 tag? No. I'm telling you this is coming in v1.
Should MF1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. It's being delivered in both v1 & v2.
Should FB1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. We've no way of knowing what info in FB1 is relevant to the v1 part of MF1, or the v2 part of MF1.

value.lit: Hmmm so we actually want a different solution from the four above, one that looks like this:

cb.code:
FR1,FR4 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff" penwidth=3]
MF1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00" penwidth=3]
FB1,IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
IV3 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

value.lit: So the rule seems to be:

ul.lit: Backwards propagation merges with manual, forwards propagation halts at manual

Derivative tags (Q & A)
Derivative tags (Q & A, continued)

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution?

value.lit: For
: this will be successful if I can use ShipReq as I have been, but with the following differences:

ul.lit: when I finish an implementation task, I shouldn't need to update anything else
when I mark a req as analysed, I shouldn't need to update anything else
I should always be able to look at an MF's status to comprehend it's collective state (and obviously that data should be correct).

value.lit: For
, I basically only want to set them on MFs, and have them automatically propagate to everything else.
For any derived tag I need to be able to quickly comprehend why the resulting value was derived.

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences this could cause?

ul.lit: It could be confusing if a req has a manually-assigned status tag but you're expecting it to auto-update
It increases complexity when a users add new tags

h2.lit: Are there any constraints or rules to which the feature must conform?

value.lit: Apart from what was outlined in
, no.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.
Versions are interesting because they kind of go in the opposite direction as statuses.

ol.lit: The purpose of this feature is to avoid users needing to manually go and re-tag everything. If we go with option (a) then users would have to manually go and re-tag all the children, meaning we've failed in the goal of this feature. Where as option (b) achieves this. And it makes sense. If users don't like the behaviour then they shouldn't create an umbrella issue and close it directly. But the ability to close an entire umbrella is very important and we can't exclude that from the solution. Option (b) it is.

Version derivation
Versions are interesting because they kind of go in the opposite direction as statuses.
Consider this case:

cb.code:
node[style=filled fillcolor="#eeeeee"]
MF1 [fillcolor="#99ff00" label="MF1\n(v2)"]
FR1 [fillcolor="#ddaaff" label="FR1\n(v1)"]
FR4 [fillcolor="#ddaaff" label="FR4\n(v1)"]
FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

value.lit: First of all,

ul.lit: If I removed v2 from MF1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v1
If I removed v1 from FR1, I'd expect the whole graph to be v2

value.lit: Where as we've got a few options with this feature...

ol.lit: Bidirectional propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1,IV1,FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
IV3,FR4 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

ol.lit: What's really interesting about this is that we combine manual values with derived values.
Bidirectional propagation (manual trumps)

cb.code:
FB1,MF1 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
FR1,FR4 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
FR2,IV2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
IV3 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

ol.lit: Backwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
IV1,FR1,FR4 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]
FB1,MF1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2
IV3 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

ol.lit: Forwards-only propagation (merge with manual)

cb.code:
FR1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
MF1,IV1,IV2,FR2,FR3,IV3,FR4 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

value.lit: Unidirectionalism clearly doesn't work, so it's a toss-up between (1) and (2).
The questions come down to:

ul.lit: Should FR1 inherit the v2 tag? No. I'm telling you this is coming in v1.
Should MF1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. It's being delivered in both v1 & v2.
Should FB1 inherit the v1 tag? Yes. We've no way of knowing what info in FB1 is relevant to the v1 part of MF1, or the v2 part of MF1.

value.lit: Hmmm so we actually want a different solution from the four above, one that looks like this:

cb.code:
FR1,FR4 [style=filled fillcolor="#ddaaff" penwidth=3]
MF1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00" penwidth=3]
FB1,IV1 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]
IV2,FR2,FR3 [style=filled fillcolor="#99ff00"]
IV3 [style=wedged fillcolor="#ddaaff:#99ff00"]

FB1 -> MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2
MF1 -> IV2 -> FR3
MF1 -> IV3 -> FR4

value.lit: So the rule seems to be:

ul.lit: Backwards propagation merges with manual, forwards propagation halts at manual

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution?

value.lit: For
: this will be successful if I can use ShipReq as I have been, but with the following differences:

ul.lit: when I finish an implementation task, I shouldn't need to update anything else
when I mark a req as analysed, I shouldn't need to update anything else
I should always be able to look at an MF's status to comprehend it's collective state (and obviously that data should be correct).

value.lit: For
, I basically only want to set them on MFs, and have them automatically propagate to everything else.
For any derived tag I need to be able to quickly comprehend why the resulting value was derived.

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences this could cause?

ul.lit: It could be confusing if a req has a manually-assigned status tag but you're expecting it to auto-update
It increases complexity when a users add new tags

h2.lit: Are there any constraints or rules to which the feature must conform?

value.lit: Apart from what was outlined in
, no.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.

If we go with option (a) then users would have to manually go and re-tag all the children, meaning we've failed in the goal of this feature. Where as option (b) achieves this. And it makes sense. But the ability to close an entire umbrella is very important and we can't exclude that from the solution. Option (b) it is.

value.lit: This looks like a good approach to me.

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution?

value.lit: For
: this will be successful if I can use ShipReq as I have been, but with the following differences:

ul.lit: when I finish an implementation task, I shouldn't need to update anything else
when I mark a req as analysed, I shouldn't need to update anything else
I should always be able to look at an MF's status to comprehend it's collective state (and obviously that data should be correct).

value.lit: For
, I basically only want to set them on MFs, and have them automatically propagate to everything else.
For any derived tag I need to be able to quickly comprehend why the resulting value was derived.

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences this could cause?

ul.lit: It could be confusing if a req has a manually-assigned status tag but you're expecting it to auto-update
It increases complexity when a users add new tags

h2.lit: Are there any constraints or rules to which the feature must conform?

value.lit: Apart from what was outlined in
, no.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in ones Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.
Possibilities:

ul.lit: <-- winner

value.lit: The only difference between
is whether manual assignments remain; all derivations are the same. So let's think about the differences.

ul.lit: looking at an interview - you don't know whether it's fully implemented or not. Users might resort to later, clearing out the status so that it's derived. That would be terrible, because the user still has to go around modifying intermediate nodes. That's against the goal

how would filters be affected? From a filter's point of view, I'd probably expect it to match against both the manual assignment and the override. Specifically, if I filter by analysed interviews, I should still see them all, even though they've been overridden by things like
. And if I filtered interviews by
I would of course still expect them all to appear; to not do that would be crazy.

value.lit: Ok that settles it.
is the winner.
I've just added
which adds a derivative tag to a manual assignment and it's
better!

ul.lit: filtering works as expected without any changes
it's clearer in that if a user adds a manual tag, you see it
user's gain control in that if a user adds a manual tag, it's there; it's not overridden

It might be an idea to visually mark synthetic tags so that they're distinguishable from manually-assigned tags.

title.lit: Derive tags according to this algorithm

h3.lit: From

ol.lit: Defaults are applied
Start processing from roots and work down to leaves, for each node...
Merge current node's manual or default values in to its children
Start processing from leaves and working back to trees, for each node...
Take the results of all its children, merge and (a) add to manual tag value if it differs, or (b) remove the default and add the merge results.

h3.lit: From

ol.lit: Start processing from roots and work down to leaves, for each node...
Merge current node's manual or default values in to its children

**.lit: unless the child has a manual value

ol.lit: Start processing from leaves and working back to trees, for each node...
Take the results of all its children, merge and (a) add to manual tag value if it differs, or (b) remove the default and add the merge results.

h3.lit: Done!

value.lit: Both blocks are identical except for the bold bit in the
section. Applying the bold bit to
doesn't change the results so I think we're good.

The set of tags must be reduced inductively by taking two and returning one, until it is no longer possible. The rules for
are specified as part of config that the users maintains. If there is no rule for
then both values
remain in the results.
All inputs and calculations in the derivation must be maintained such that

ul.lit: they can be presented to a user as text (i.e. a multi-step formula)
they can be used to create colour bars indicating all content considered
empty values (i.e. relevant nodes with no tags) should also consume space in colour bars

value.lit: System
derive tags according to this algorithm

h4.lit: From

ol.lit: Start processing from roots and work down to leaves, for each node...
Merge current node's manual or default values in to its children
Start processing from leaves and working back to trees, for each node...
Take the results of all its children, merge and (a) add to manual tag value if it differs, or (b) remove the default and add the merge results.

value.lit: This looks like a good approach to me.
I just discovered in
that when you have a manual tag X in a parent, and a default of Y in a child, the normal derivation rules of X + Y apply. When X + Y = Y, we retain the Y. This is great when X=
and Y=
but it's a problem when X=
. The expectation is that implementing a parent cascades to mark all children as implemented too. It seems we can't have this. So it is a problem or not?

ul.lit: Deepti immediately agreed with the limitation. Having to manually mark each subtask as implemented seems very natural to her. When I explained that I want to automate all of that she suggested having a button in ReqDetail to apply tags to all children.

If we don't have it, I worry that there are still cases where you have to manually chase down reqs to fix there tags - just that it'd be limited to children (instead of children and parents in the world before derivative tags).
Even with this limitation, the vast majority of the derivative tags problem scenarios are solved. I could just accept that as a win, revisit this in future if it comes up.....
I guess to fully solve this to my satisfaction, the remaining problem space is:

//.lit: how can one implement additional rules that only affect children and either trump the existing rules, or allow for special cases of default overriding?

> I pressed escape and Jira just discarded my comment that I had been drafting for 30 minutes
> I wonder if there’s an extension that stores all text inputs in local storage for recovery. It’s hard to know in advance when you’re about to start drafting a long response.
We should do better. Maybe drafts should be stored in local storage until committed. In fact, what about this:

ol.lit: User writes lots of text, accidentally hits escape
ShipReq stores text in local storage and closes without a prompt.
User re-opens editor, we initialise it with the previous draft value.

value.lit: Something like that. There'd be a bunch of considerations to make though...

ul.lit: How do they discard the draft themselves. Being able to revert your edits (esp deletes) is essential.
Might be an idea to look to gmail and it's draft management for this (and for established UX)
Need to consider cases where the original is updated after the draft. How to merge?

Safety concerns around local storage. Also portability concerns. Maybe best to store all drafts on server-side: better security, more useful, once a draft UI is included users will expect it everywhere anyway. (like gmail)

h2.lit: How can we judge the success of the solution?

value.lit: For
: this will be successful if I can use ShipReq as I have been, but with the following differences:

ul.lit: when I finish an implementation task, I shouldn't need to update anything else
when I mark a req as analysed, I shouldn't need to update anything else
I should always be able to look at an MF's status to comprehend it's collective state (and obviously that data should be correct).

value.lit: For
, I basically only want to set them on MFs, and have them automatically propagate to everything else.
For any derived tag I need to be able to quickly comprehend why the resulting value was derived.

h2.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences this could cause?

ul.lit: It could be confusing if a req has a manually-assigned status tag but you're expecting it to auto-update
It increases complexity when a users add new tags

h2.lit: Are there any constraints or rules to which the feature must conform?

value.lit: Apart from what was outlined in
, no.

There are constraints imposed by ShipReq's core business goals like comprehensibility, and simplicity. But the fact that we'll always explain how derived tags came to be, should address this. Not sure anything else is needed.

h2.lit: Which aspects of the feature are most critical to creating business value?

ul.lit: automation
reliability / correctness
explanation / provenance

h2.lit: What aspect of the feature most excites you?

ul.lit: How much time & effort it will save me from having to manually keep everything up to date
How much confusion it will save me / how much it will clarify my own mountain of content

h2.lit: Should we have multiple weighted values instead of a consolidation?

value.lit: So instead of (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev = ready_for_dev), what would it be like to just retain (18 completed + 1 ready_for_dev) as the value? Well for starters, the req would effectively have both tags (completed, ready_for_dev). Bubbling this up to the top you'd end up with MF's having nearly all the tags which seems like it'd be useless. I think just choosing one tag and displaying the breakdown/calculation is the way to go.

Apart from it being informational, what else would it affect? It would affect filtering. So filtering by rejected would now include all the non-rejected MFs and a bunch of intermediate (possibly valid) intermediate reqs too. That sounds like shit.
I think it's a clear "no".
Don't forget that when a semigroup rule for two values doesn't exist, we simply take both without consolidation. That's orthogonal to this question and decision.

h2.lit: What should happen when there's no tag in a child?

value.lit: Some possibilities:

ol.lit: it doesn't affect the tag selection, but it does affect the explanation & percentage bar.
they could be identified by making the tag field mandatory - then it'll appear in one's Issues

we could just abort and propagate emptiness all the way up - that way it's clear that there's an error. On the other hand, now you'll see entire tree branches show up on the issues page, with it being unclear which req is the core req needing to be fixed
we could also kind of cheat and display
beside the derived tag. It wouldn't show up on the Issues page though so that could be confusing.

value.lit: Seeing as simplicity and consistency from a user's point of view are core business goals, I guess we should accept (1) and (2) above, and reject (3) and (4).
Yeah cool, done.

h2.lit: What should happen when a child has multiple tags?

value.lit: Simple. Treat them the same as we'd treat combining tags from multiple reqs that have a single tag each.
Shouldn't be any different.
Possibilities:

ul.lit: <-- winner

value.lit: The only difference between
is whether manual assignments remain; all derivations are the same. So let's think about the differences.

ul.lit: looking at an interview - you don't know whether it's fully implemented or not. Users might resort to later, clearing out the status so that it's derived. That would be terrible, because the user still has to go around modifying intermediate nodes. That's against the goal

how would filters be affected? From a filter's point of view, I'd probably expect it to match against both the manual assignment and the override. Specifically, if I filter by analysed interviews, I should still see them all, even though they've been overridden by things like
. And if I filtered interviews by
I would of course still expect them all to appear; to not do that would be crazy.

value.lit: Ok that settles it.
is the winner.

which adds a derivative tag to a manual assignment and it's
better!

ul.lit: filtering works as expected without any changes
it's clearer in that if a user adds a manual tag, you see it
user's gain control in that if a user adds a manual tag, it's there; it's not overridden

analysed        + rejected        = analysed

deferred        + rejected        = deferred

implemented     + rejected        = implemented
needs_analysis  + needs_more_info = needs_more_info

value.lit: Tag derivation is not commutative!
Consider these derivation rules:

analysed        + deferred        = analysed

analysed        + rejected        = analysed
deferred        + implemented     = implemented
deferred        + needs_analysis  = needs_analysis
deferred        + needs_more_info = needs_more_info
deferred        + ready_for_dev   = ready_for_dev
deferred        + rejected        = deferred
implemented     + needs_analysis  = needs_analysis
implemented     + needs_more_info = needs_more_info
implemented     + ready_for_dev   = ready_for_dev
implemented     + rejected        = implemented
needs_analysis  + needs_more_info = needs_more_info
needs_analysis  + ready_for_dev   = needs_analysis
needs_analysis  + rejected        = needs_analysis
needs_more_info + ready_for_dev   = needs_more_info
needs_more_info + rejected        = rejected
ready_for_dev   + rejected        = ready_for_dev

= needs_more_info

1.lit: Just like in github and twitter how you can @ people.

title.lit: Users
be able to reference users in rich text

value.lit: Requirements are normally authored by BAs on a project. A project often has multiple BAs, usually on the same team. I'm sure places exist where they would want non-BAs contributing directly to requirements (without the bottleneck of going through the BA), in fact some places may want to store and track all requirements but not even have a BA; PM & devs would edit reqs themselves.

So the system needs to allow multiple users writing to the same project, sometimes even the same requirement concurrently. I also want to have multiple browser tabs open on the same computer (so same session, user, IP, MAC) and have concurrent editing without a problem.

As with any multi-user situation permissions and security management is necessary. Here are some examples of groups/roles: PM, Head BA, BAs, project owner, stakeholder, tester, dev, “reader” (grant read-access to some randoms), business users, client, ops, designers. Plus there will be cases where different teams need to collaborate on intersection reqs, in which roles on their own may not suffice; it may become necessary to restrict access to a subset of a project. Example: I'm building a big system and component B requires collaboration with team X; I may want to grant team X read access to the entire project, edit access to reqs with grouping ComponentB, and create-req access with the constraint that reqs they create are tagged with grouping ComponentB.
Intersection with
here, if there are multiple authors on a project then it may become necessary to be able to assign issues to 0:n users and have users be able to filter out other people/team's tasks in issue views.
Intersection with
, all changes will need to be attributable to a user.
A project owner will need to be able to hand ownership over to another user.

The scope of a project isn't governed by the laws of nature; sometimes a project splits off into smaller ones, sometimes separate projects merge into a single one. I'd say that these cases legitimately fall under the umbrella of collaboration. Actually there'd probably be a real-world need to have collaboration between projects without merging them (esp with intersections). What would be required? Maybe just references? Shared groupings? Who knows. Needs analysis. Plus it could probably safely be moved to its own MF.
With collaboration comes conflict, mistakes, idiocy; quite likely someone good or authoritative may be unsatisfied with the contribution of other authors and may want to: undo their shit (
), reject a contribution with a request of amendments, ask them questions about a contribution (
), create a discussion around a contentious contribution (

), ask another user to review/confirm an amendment. Most of these could (and probably should) be pushed outside the scope of ShipReq in which case the focus shifts to anything required to facilitate the manual process. Very importantly, this leads to...
Notifications and watching! This should probably be its own MF. (
]) I want to know of all changes that happen on a project, or that affect a subset of the requirements. I want an email for each one
. I want an email every 2 hours, every week, etc. summarising all the changes that occurred in that time period. I want to be able to notify other users in the system, eg. “@golly pls confirm”.

I want to first start by considering what Github does. They're very simple / minimalistic. So Github has:

org -> user [label="membership\n{outsideCollaborator: bool}\nn:n"]
org,user -> invitation [label="1:n"]
org -> team [label="1:n"]
team -> user [label="maintainers\nn:1..n"]
team -> team [label="parent/child\n1:n"]

cb.code:
edge[arrowhead=none]
project[fillcolor="#99ff88"]
user[fillcolor="#99ff88"]
ug [label="user group" fillcolor="#ccddff"]
pg [label="project group" fillcolor="#ccddff"]
p [label="project |\nproject group"]
u [label="user |\nuser group"]
uup [label="user |\nuser group |\nproject group"]

ug -> ug [label="parent/child\nn:n"]
pg -> pg [label="parent/child\nn:n"]

user,ug -> u [label="π"]
project,pg -> p [label="π"]
u,pg -> uup [label="π"]

value.lit: So very clearly we have the same abstract here:

cb.code:
data Tree a b         = Value a | Group b [Tree a b]
data ProjectOrGroupId = Tree ProjectId ProjectGroupId
data UserOrGroupId    = Tree UserId    UserGroupId

value.lit: with a few constraints:

ul.lit: must be acyclic
the top-most groups must always have user-based owners

value.lit: Also

ul.lit: Users should be able to invite other users to join a project / user group
Invitations can be direct: user A invites users B & C directly

Invitations should be indirect too: an admin should be able to generate something like an invitation code (or maybe just a join-page-url) and then hand that out to the team before they even have their user accounts, then after creating an account they use it, system asks owner to confirm join request (or maybe just ask for password?).

value.lit: Ideally I'd like to wrap all of the above into just

ul.lit: read only - (not yet for projects)

desc.lit: meh probably not at all for other types right?

ul.lit: collaborator - read & write content but not perms, membership etc
admin
project group CRUDL - except project / project-group owners need to consent to inclusion if the user differs
user group CRUDL - except users / user-groups need to consent to inclusion
project permission CRUDL
user group permission CRUDL
project (group) owners should be able to invite projects / project groups owned by other people
owners should be able to invite specific users to their user group
owners should be able to create invitation pages for users to join their user group
view sent invitations (and their statuses)
revoke pending invitations
inverse CRUDL views - subjects should be able to see what groups they're in and remove themselves
ensure user groups always have a valid owner
ensure project groups always have a valid owner
ensure permissions & permission groups always have a valid owner
ensure owners never lose permission to administer data they own
(direct) invitations are only valid once
disallow read-only project access for now

All changes should result in storage of immutable events. I guess this would therefore be global, the state derivation of which all the time would be slow, therefore, we should probably both store the events

maintain mutable tables (like typical DB usage). In which case we've basically got a typical DB setup with an audit trail table. Eventually we'll provide a means for users to inspect the audit trails.

h4.lit: User universe

ul.lit: all paths in the user universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the user universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P.
all owners are members. An owner is a child with admin permission.
all user groups have owners: either a user or another user group. Make sure that all UGs have at least one admin child.

all user groups must have at least one user as a transitive owner - we can't allow user groups to become orphans without human admins. The DAG already structure enforces finite paths. Ensure all admin paths end in Us and not UGs.

h4.lit: Project universe

ul.lit: all paths in the project universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the project universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P.

all P* have owners: either a U* or a PG. Either we need an attribute on P*, or we merge the U* and P* DAGs... Adding an attribute to P* would allow cyclic graphs when U* & P* are combined. We don't want infinite paths. So I guess we

**.lit: merge the DAGs into a single U* & P* universe

ul.lit: seeing as both universes define edges the same way (i.e. child is a member), we should do the same for
and say P grants access A to U.
