value.lit: Similar to lint tools for code. Will scan requirements and look for dubious patterns/issues. Eg. ambiguous language “should”, “probably”, etc.
Some linting is for correctness, clarity (non-ambiguity); some is for style.
Example of a style lint: remove trailing full stops from single sentence
s
According to user config, we could have some rules applied automatically on save; or a user can choose to preview and run a rule manually.
Notes:

ul.lit: Permissions are commented-out for now. The plan is to start with only two types of built-in permissions — adminᵖᵘ & (collaboratorᵖ | memberᵘ) — and later when I have more users implement permissions as a DAG just like users & projects.

Omitting the ability to invite a known P* to a PG. Pretty obscure. Add it later for completeness but ignore for now, especially seeing as one can create an invitation page and accomplish the same thing (provided they have a means to contact admin of the project).

users need to be able to quickly see everything that they're a member of
users need to be able to quickly see all pending invitations
users can see their contacts lists
every time users come [in contact] or [become accessible to each other (eg are both on the same project)], they should be added to each others' contact lists
users can remove people from their contact lists so long as they aren't on the same projects / teams anymore
users should be able to see how/why a user is on their contact list
ensure user groups always have a valid owner
ensure project groups always have a valid owner
ensure permissions & permission groups always have a valid owner
ensure owners never lose permission to administer data they own

people can never send themselves invitations. If they're an admin of both target and invitee then no invitation is needed.

maintain mutable tables (like typical DB usage). In which case we've basically got a typical DB setup with an audit trail table. Eventually we'll provide a means for users to inspect the audit trails.

value.lit: Allow discussion between team members in various scopes (primarily in a req, or across a set of reqs)
Consider using and/or integrating with:
Notes:

ul.lit: Permissions are commented-out for now. The plan is to start with only two types of built-in permissions — adminᵖᵘ & (collaboratorᵖ | memberᵘ) — and later when I have more users implement permissions as a DAG just like users & projects.

Omitting the ability to invite a known P* to a PG. Pretty obscure. Add it later for completeness but ignore for now, especially seeing as one can create an invitation page and accomplish the same thing (provided they have a means to contact admin of the project).

h4.lit: User universe

ul.lit: all paths in the user universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the user universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P
all owners are members – an owner is a member (child) with admin permission
all user groups have owners: either a user or another user group. Make sure that all UGs have at least one admin child.

all user groups must have at least one user as a transitive owner - we can't allow user groups to become orphans without human admins. The DAG already structure enforces finite paths. Ensure all admin paths end in Us and not UGs.

h4.lit: Project universe

ul.lit: all paths in the project universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the project universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P

all P* have owners: either a U* or a PG. Either we need an attribute on P*, or we merge the U* and P* DAGs... Adding an attribute to P* would allow cyclic graphs when U* & P* are combined. It doesn't seem like that should be ok but let's test it out...
Seems ok! Necessary even because this is a perfectly valid vase. When viewing access from the P* perspectives:

cb.code:
P* -> U*
========
* P1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* P2
  * UG2 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* PG1
  * U1 (admin)

ul.lit: What about from U* perspectives?

cb.code:
U -> UGs
========
* U1
  * UG1 (admin)
  * UG2 (admin)
* U2
  * UG1 (member)

U -> P*
=======
* U1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * P1 (admin)
    * P2 (member)
* U2
  (none)

ul.lit: Wow ok. Seems fine. I would've thought any cycles = bad. I should come up with an intuition as to why it's not just ok, but necessary.

desc.lit: Justify why links between U* & P* universes are undirected. Is it because we never jump back and forth between universes?

ul.lit: all P* have owners
all P* must have at least one user as a transitive owner

Omitting the ability to invite a known P* to a PG. Pretty obscure. Add it later for completeness but ignore for now, especially seeing as one can create an invitation page and accomplish the same thing (provided they have a means to contact admin of the project).

h4.lit: User universe

ul.lit: all paths in the user universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the user universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P
all owners are members – an owner is a member (child) with admin permission
all user groups have owners: either a user or another user group. Make sure that all UGs have at least one admin child.

all user groups must have at least one user as a transitive owner - we can't allow user groups to become orphans without human admins. The DAG already structure enforces finite paths. Ensure all admin paths end in Us and not UGs.

h4.lit: Project universe

ul.lit: all paths in the project universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the project universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P

all P* have owners: either a U* or a PG. Either we need an attribute on P*, or we merge the U* and P* DAGs... Adding an attribute to P* would allow cyclic graphs when U* & P* are combined. It doesn't seem like that should be ok but let's test it out...
Seems ok! Necessary even because this is a perfectly valid vase. When viewing access from the P* perspectives:

cb.code:
P* -> U*
========
* P1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* P2
  * UG2 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* PG1
  * U1 (admin)

ul.lit: What about from U* perspectives?

cb.code:
U -> UGs
========
* U1
  * UG1 (admin)
  * UG2 (admin)
* U2
  * UG1 (member)

U -> P*
=======
* U1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * P1 (admin)
    * P2 (member)
* U2
  (none)

ul.lit: Wow ok. Seems fine. I would've thought any cycles = bad. I should come up with an intuition as to why it's not just ok, but necessary.

desc.lit: Justify why links between U* & P* universes are undirected. Is it because we never jump back and forth between universes?

ul.lit: all P* have owners
all P* must have at least one user as a transitive owner

For known users: send an invite.
For strangers: create an invitation page.
For UGs of which I'm an admin, I can do it directly without an invitation.
Requires user lookup doesn't it? I don't want to do that, both for security/privacy reasons, and for my own time & effort.
For known users: send an invite.

For UGs of which I'm an admin, I can do it directly without an invitation.
Admin can reorganise projects however they like so long as they already have access.
Otherwise, avoid invitations, at least for now.

value.lit: So in summary the capabilities are:

ul.lit: invite U* to UG
invite U* to P
invite U* to PG
invitation page to UG
invitation page to P
invitation page to PG
project group CRUDL - except project / project-group owners need to consent to inclusion if the user differs
user group CRUDL - except users / user-groups need to consent to inclusion
project permission CRUDL
user group permission CRUDL
invite known U* to a UG
invite known U* to a P*
admin can add their own UGs to their own UGs
admin can add their own P* to their own PGs
create invitation page for U* to join one's P*
create invitation page for U* to join one's UG
view sent invitations (and their statuses and their audit trail including accepts/rejects)
revoke pending invitations
invitation pages CRUDL (and their statuses and their audit trail including accepts/rejects)
inverse CRUDL views - subjects should be able to see what groups they're in and remove themselves
users need to be able to quickly see everything that they're an owner of
users need to be able to quickly see everything that they're a member of
users need to be able to quickly see all pending invitations
users can see their contacts lists
every time users come [in contact] or [become accessible to each other (eg are both on the same project)], they should be added to each others' contact lists
users can remove people from their contact lists so long as they aren't on the same projects / teams anymore
users should be able to see how/why a user is on their contact list
ensure user groups always have a valid owner
ensure project groups always have a valid owner
ensure permissions & permission groups always have a valid owner
ensure owners never lose permission to administer data they own
(direct) invitations are only valid once
disallow read-only project access for now
people can never send themselves invitations. If they're an admin of both target and invitee then no invitation is needed.

All changes should result in storage of immutable events. I guess this would therefore be global, the state derivation of which all the time would be slow, therefore, we should probably both store the events

maintain mutable tables (like typical DB usage). In which case we've basically got a typical DB setup with an audit trail table. Eventually we'll provide a means for users to inspect the audit trails.
Keep up the ShipReq values

desc.lit: more ShipReq core values here!

ul.lit: of easy comprehensibility - meaning admin need to be able to quickly see entire trees with all relations.

It would be probably be wise to defer implementation (at least in the UI) of project groups for now. Multi-user support is by far the most important thing - project groups won't be used for a very long time...
Most (all) invitations and invitation pages should appear both in users' home dashboards, and in the entities themselves (i.e. project, project groups, user groups)

value.lit: As such, it's now very, very important that we have a solution to

so that if an open editor suddenly becomes illegal, we can close it immediately knowing that the users changes are saved somewhere. Likewise after a user gets their access back (or whatever), their draft editor value should become available again.
I just discovered in
that when you have a manual tag X in a parent, and a default of Y in a child, the normal derivation rules of X + Y apply. When X + Y = Y, we retain the Y. This is great when X=
and Y=
but it's a problem when X=
. The expectation is that implementing a parent cascades to mark all children as implemented too. It seems we can't have this. So it is a problem or not?

ul.lit: Deepti immediately agreed with the limitation. Having to manually mark each subtask as implemented seems very natural to her. When I explained that I want to automate all of that she suggested having a button in ReqDetail to apply tags to all children.

If we don't have it, I worry that there are still cases where you have to manually chase down reqs to fix there tags - just that it'd be limited to children (instead of children and parents in the world before derivative tags).
Even with this limitation, the vast majority of the derivative tags problem scenarios are solved. I could just accept that as a win, revisit this in future if it comes up.....
I guess to fully solve this to my satisfaction, the remaining problem space is:

//.lit: how can one implement additional rules that only affect children and either trump the existing rules, or allow for special cases of default overriding?

Management of users's drafts

Let's start with the basics. We need to store the draft somewhere as the user types. We needn't save on every single keystroke (for performance reasons), but there should be some kind of window., likely temporal. Another way of looking at that window, is how much user change are we happy to lose in the case of disaster? 10, 20, 30 sec worth? Kind of arbitrary so just use a gut-feel to compromise between client performance, server impact, and data protection for the user.
My first thought was local storage but there are some issues with that:

ul.lit: security risk -- we're talking about IP here although it's only a little I guess, plus encryption could be employed to reduce risk
drafts are bound to a single browser cache - that's pretty shitty for users. Users should be able to switch devices and access their drafts

value.lit: Meaning that we're going with a server-side solution. What options do we have?

ul.lit: DB - just chuck it all in Postgres
setup some other data store?

value.lit: I guess the only reason not to use Postgres is for performance reasons right?

ul.lit: more svr ↔ DB traffic
more DB storage
table performance - there's going to be A LOT of frequent writes (eg. potentially approx every
sec per concurrent user)

value.lit: Oh shit there's something else to consider:

**.lit: how do we save drafts when a user is offline?

support for this to be truly useful, and keep in mind that
is going to be a big selling point in the future.
So it looks like we've have two stores:

ol.lit: local storage that syncs itself up when an online connection is restored
a server-side store capable of
of RW IO

cb.code:
subgraph cluster_server {
  graph[label=Server]
  db
}
subgraph cluster_client {
  graph[label=Client]
  e [label="editor"]
  ls [label="local\nstorage"]
}
d [label="remote draft"]
ev [label="event"]

edge[color=red fontcolor=red]
e -> ls [label="local store"]
ls -> e [label="local restore"]
db -> d -> e [label="remote store" dir=back]
db -> d -> e [label="remote restore"]

edge[color=blue fontcolor=blue]
db -> ev -> e [label="commit" dir=back]
db -> ev -> e [label="external change"]

value.lit: Let's start with the basics. We need to store the draft somewhere as the user types. We needn't save on every single keystroke (for performance reasons), but there should be some kind of window., likely temporal. Another way of looking at that window, is how much user change are we happy to lose in the case of disaster? 10, 20, 30 sec worth? Kind of arbitrary so just use a gut-feel to compromise between client performance, server impact, and data protection for the user.
My first thought was local storage but there are some issues with that:

ul.lit: security risk -- we're talking about IP here although it's only a little I guess, plus encryption could be employed to reduce risk
drafts are bound to a single browser cache - that's pretty shitty for users. Users should be able to switch devices and access their drafts

value.lit: Meaning that we're going with a server-side solution. What options do we have?

ul.lit: DB - just chuck it all in Postgres
setup some other data store?

more DB storage

sec per concurrent user)

not be a forceful modal. The user should still maintain full control over their app's state, meaning a few things:

h3.lit: Accidental closure

value.lit: Going back to the original feedback, we also need to protect users from their own mistakes. If I close an editor and then realise

//.lit: "oh shit, that was the wrong editor/decision"

value.lit: , how can I get my old value back? Keep in mind that closing and editor to deliberate discard your changes and then opening it back up again is a very common ability that must be preserved. Even if we separated the "close editor" action from the "discard draft", it's the same problem in that how do we handle the user explicitly discarding their draft and then realising that was the wrong move? There's only so much protection we can provide (or at least is feasible for me to implement right now)...

desc.lit: Try out a few UCs for this...

h3.lit: What does Gmail do

value.lit: What does gmail do? It keeps drafts as separate entities that are manually discardable. Our situation is a bit different. We don't want floating, separate drafts. A draft is associated with exactly one editor — every where the editor goes, so too should the draft. We could associate multiple drafts with a single editor but that seems stupid. I imagine it would just be a proxy for users to manually manage changes they've made on different devices with using the conflict editor. Actually...

h3.lit: History UX per field

value.lit: ...as weird as it sounds that could be a path if you consider that editors always have multiple versions (i.e. it's committed history plus any drafts). I wonder if it would make sense to solve draft management and field history in the same stroke? We'd still need a conflict editor though because regardless of where the two states come from, we don't want users having to manually copy-paste to merge themselves with no automated support. Holy shit, this could actually turn into a real solution. Come to think of it, all of the above is written as if there are always, ever only two revisions needing a merge. What happens when a user has a local draft, a remote draft and a new event comes in? Now there are three sources. If we're saying that merges can be postponed does that mean there can also be an unsaved live change in the editor too, being a fourth source?
Interestingly, this might solve the accidental closure issue: even when a user discards a draft, just keep a non-current draft available in the remote store that the user can view in the history. (?)

//.lit: Fuck this is feeling a bit 'troublesome' in terms of tech, performance, architecture but don't worry about that. Know that I'm good enough to craft a good tech solution. Instead just focus of the user's experience.

value.lit: Pricing thoughts
We need to start introducing some core ShipReq ethical values. Current thoughts:

ul.lit: Beyond survivability, profit should be a side-effect of improving lives / providing value

A reasonable amount of profit can and should be used to reward efforts; the rest should be put towards a commonly-good goal, initially reinvesting in ShipReq so that we can provide more value, once we're "rich", investing in initiatives like Scala Center, OSS, community work, even fighting climate change.

Extremely low profit entities (eg. single-person businesses just getting off the ground) should be able to use the service for free. As an entity's capital grows, so too should our fees. I don't know that poking around in customers' finances is itself a good or ethical idea, but team size is probably a pretty good indication. The more people in a business's teams, the more employees they're able to afford... well at least that seems like a reasonable heuristic. So many free for one person, unlimited projects and events, but start charging per head when teams are used.

The more people in a business's teams, the more employees they're able to afford... well at least that seems like a reasonable heuristic. So many free for one person, unlimited projects and events, but start charging per head when teams are used. This means we'll either:

ol.lit: need to implement and enforce quotas
all users must provide credit card info to sign up -- if they don't use teams, they aren't charged
something in between the two. No CC info required, but in order to create teams one will need to add a CC to their account

Let's with a bit of modelling (for a single editor) to see what we're dealing with...

ul.lit: client
editor: ∅ | V
local storage: ∅ | V
event received: ∅ | V
event sent: ∅ | V
draft sent: ∅ | V
draft received: ∅ | V
server
store: ∅ | V

value.lit: Hmm looking at it that way it doesn't seem that complicated (so long as we have a sense of a time), maybe we just need sensible propagation logic and a single conflict editor.

cb.code:
subgraph cluster_server {
  graph[label=Server]
  dbd [label="draft\nDB"]
  dbe [label="event\nDB"]
}
subgraph cluster_client {
  graph[label=Client]
  e [label="editor"]
  ls [label="local\nstorage"]
}
d [label="remote\ndraft" fontcolor=red fillcolor="#ffeeee"]
ev [label="event" fontcolor=blue  fillcolor="#eeeeff"]

edge[color=red fontcolor=red]
e -> ls [label="store"]
ls -> e [label="restore"]
dbd -> d -> e [label="store" dir=back]
dbd -> d -> e [label="restore"]

edge[color=blue fontcolor=blue]
dbe -> ev -> e [label="commit" dir=back]
dbe -> ev -> e [label="external change"]

value.lit: It would be good to automatically resolve trivial conflicts without the user being notified.
Otherwise we need to popup a dialog for the user to deal with it. A critical point is that it
not be a forceful modal. The user should still maintain full control over their app's state, meaning a few things:

ul.lit: if the user is editing A and a conflict on B occurs, they users' workflow should not be affected. They should maybe see a little conflict count increase at the top of the screen (or something like that if anything at all), be able to finish what they're doing with editor A, then in their own time check out what the story is with B.
when a user has the conflict editor open, they should be able to close it and come back to it later

h3.lit: Accidental closure

value.lit: Going back to the original feedback, we also need to protect users from their own mistakes. If I close an editor and then realise

//.lit: "oh shit, that was the wrong editor/decision"

value.lit: , how can I get my old value back? Keep in mind that closing and editor to deliberate discard your changes and then opening it back up again is a very common ability that must be preserved. Even if we separated the "close editor" action from the "discard draft", it's the same problem in that how do we handle the user explicitly discarding their draft and then realising that was the wrong move? There's only so much protection we can provide (or at least is feasible for me to implement right now)...

desc.lit: Try out a few UCs for this...

h3.lit: What does Gmail do

value.lit: What does gmail do? It keeps drafts as separate entities that are manually discardable. Our situation is a bit different. We don't want floating, separate drafts. A draft is associated with exactly one editor — every where the editor goes, so too should the draft. We could associate multiple drafts with a single editor but that seems stupid. I imagine it would just be a proxy for users to manually manage changes they've made on different devices with using the conflict editor. Actually...

h3.lit: History UX per field

value.lit: ...as weird as it sounds that could be a path if you consider that editors always have multiple versions (i.e. it's committed history plus any drafts). I wonder if it would make sense to solve draft management and field history in the same stroke? We'd still need a conflict editor though because regardless of where the two states come from, we don't want users having to manually copy-paste to merge themselves with no automated support. Holy shit, this could actually turn into a real solution. Come to think of it, all of the above is written as if there are always, ever only two revisions needing a merge. What happens when a user has a local draft, a remote draft and a new event comes in? Now there are three sources. If we're saying that merges can be postponed does that mean there can also be an unsaved live change in the editor too, being a fourth source?
Interestingly, this might solve the accidental closure issue: even when a user discards a draft, just keep a non-current draft available in the remote store that the user can view in the history. (?)

//.lit: Fuck this is feeling a bit 'troublesome' in terms of tech, performance, architecture but don't worry about that. Know that I'm good enough to craft a good tech solution. Instead just focus of the user's experience.

h3.lit: Misc

ul.lit: look into some of Lamport's distributed clock stuff
what about the same user on multiple tabs
seriously, ensure some kind of max storage limit. We need to be safe to someone pasting 1GB of text
use TLA+ to ensure things like:
we never discard a newer draft in favour of an older one
U opens editor
U makes changes
U aborts
¿how does U get the old draft back?
U opens editor
U makes changes
field receives external update
¿how is U notified of the new change?
¿how do they merge their draft with the new change?

h4.lit: Ideas:

ul.lit: Have a little context menu or hamburger on each field, initially hidden, that appear on field hover. From there we could access all kinds of actions and views

Change fields to be like an imaginary flashcard: the latest value and editor on one side, and a bunch of utils/tabs/controls/menu on the other; one of those utils could be a way to show all history and drafts.

I'm very happy to have the entire screen consumed (i.e. be taken to another page) to view all a field's history, see my diffs, do merges. In which case we probably just need to make sure there's an efficient way of getting there.

h3.lit: Solution:

value.lit: Use a grammar like this:

cb.code:
scope  = "derivation" [ "(" field ")" ]
clause = [ "+" ] scope { "," scope } ":(" subFilter ")"

value.lit: Examples:

cb.code:
+derivation:(#abc)
derivation(X),derivation(Y):(#abc)

value.lit: Laws:

cb.code:
+derivation:(x)                  ≡  x derivation:(x)
derivation:(a) derivation:(b)    ≡  derivation:(a b)
derivation:(a) | derivation:(b)  ≡  derivation:(a | b)
-derivation:(a)                  ≡  derivation:(-a)
a | (b derivation:(c))           ≡  (a | b) | derivation:(c)
(a derivation:(c)) | b           ≡  (a | b) | derivation:(c)

Not nestable — derivation:(derivation:(x)) is illegal

(a +derivation:(b)) | (c +derivation:(d))
  = ((a b) derivation:(b)) | ((c d) derivation:(d))
  = ((a b) | ((c d) derivation:(d))) | derivation:(b)
  = ((a b) | (c d)) | derivation:(b) | derivation:(d)
  = ((a b) | (c d)) | derivation:(b | d)

value.lit: Features:

ul.lit: supports additional scopes
supports specific fields
the
allows us to apply the sub-filter to the normal content filter without us needing to introduce a new keyword
supports a sub-filter only applying to derivation without affecting the main content filter
supports use in sibling- and sub-clauses with reasonable laws.

//.lit: may be surprising and not what people want, but to maintain separate derivation filters per main-filter branch is too much work for now - it can always be changed in the future)

value.lit: When a filter is in place in the ReqTable screen:

ul.lit: for all derivative tags in the results, if all the sources are excluded by the filter: move the tag into the "reappeances" bucket so that
it isn't rendered with colour
it is excluded from sorting
if all tags for a req get moved into the "reappearances" bucket
it should be sorted as if ∅
just the tags should be rendered -- it shouldn't say "blank" or anything

derivation:(a) | derivation:(b)  ≡  derivation:(a | b)
-derivation:(a)                  ≡  derivation:(-a)
a | (b derivation:(c))           ≡  (a | b) | derivation:(c)
(a derivation:(c)) | b           ≡  (a | b) | derivation:(c)

Example:
  (a +derivation:(b)) | (c +derivation:(d))
    = ((a b) derivation:(b)) | ((c d) derivation:(d))
    = ((a b) | ((c d) derivation:(d))) | derivation:(b)
    = ((a b) | (c d)) | derivation:(b) | derivation:(d)
    = ((a b) | (c d)) | derivation:(b | d)

all edges in the user universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions

indicates that A has a member B with permission P
all owners are members – an owner is a member (child) with admin permission

value.lit: Drafts

ul.lit: what's required around draft deletion/resolution?
what happens when an offline user comes online, how exactly should the sync work? What if their local draft is older that the remote draft?
can we really resolve trivial conflicts without notifying the user? If so, how? What's trivial and what's not?
Do we need to provide any visual indicators that drafts exist, when a field isn't currently being edited? (I think not)
When an editor is open, we need to let users know when
a draft is available
multiple conflicting drafts are available
the field has received an external update
For all of the above, what's the process to resolution, including conflict resolution? How is the process impacted by trivial automatic merges (if implemented)?

When the user aborts an editor, the draft is saved (but marked as inactive), meaning that the user manually has to find and restore it if that's what they want. How many such drafts do we save? Just always the latest aborted draft? 3?

Users can view a list of past revisions and aborted drafts; what next? Do we start with a list, then go to a diff view or full rendering or what? Details. In the use cases we're planning at the moment, what flows/actions are required? What about in future? Stuff like restore (= undo), edit-from-here?
consider the effects wrt same user, multiple tabs
does opening an editor in one tab open it in the rest now??

what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and makes different changes in each. (Maybe we could save all editor drafts immediately on-change, but only do server syncs periodically?)
what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and aborts one?
what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and commits one?

value.lit: Let's start with the basics. We need to store the draft somewhere as the user types. We needn't save on every single keystroke (for performance reasons), but there should be some kind of window., likely temporal. Another way of looking at that window, is how much user change are we happy to lose in the case of disaster? 10, 20, 30 sec worth? Kind of arbitrary so just use a gut-feel to compromise between client performance, server impact, and data protection for the user. Don't forget that we'll also save on certain events like editor blur, tab closing beacon.
My first thought was local storage but there are some issues with that:

ul.lit: security risk -- we're talking about IP here although it's only a little I guess, plus encryption could be employed to reduce risk
drafts are bound to a single browser cache - that's pretty shitty for users. Users should be able to switch devices and access their drafts

value.lit: Meaning that we're going with a server-side solution. What options do we have?

ul.lit: DB - just chuck it all in Postgres
setup some other data store?

sec per concurrent user)

value.lit: It would be good to automatically resolve trivial conflicts without the user being notified.
Otherwise we need to popup a dialog for the user to deal with it. A critical point is that it
not be a forceful modal. The user should still maintain full control over their app's state, meaning a few things:

ul.lit: if the user is editing A and a conflict on B occurs, they users' workflow should not be affected. They should maybe see a little conflict count increase at the top of the screen (or something like that if anything at all), be able to finish what they're doing with editor A, then in their own time check out what the story is with B.
when a user has the conflict editor open, they should be able to close it and come back to it later

h3.lit: Accidental closure

value.lit: Going back to the original feedback, we also need to protect users from their own mistakes. If I close an editor and then realise

//.lit: "oh shit, that was the wrong editor/decision"

value.lit: , how can I get my old value back? Keep in mind that closing and editor to deliberate discard your changes and then opening it back up again is a very common ability that must be preserved. Even if we separated the "close editor" action from the "discard draft", it's the same problem in that how do we handle the user explicitly discarding their draft and then realising that was the wrong move? There's only so much protection we can provide (or at least is feasible for me to implement right now)...

h3.lit: What does Gmail do

value.lit: What does gmail do? It keeps drafts as separate entities that are manually discardable. Our situation is a bit different. We don't want floating, separate drafts. A draft is associated with exactly one editor — every where the editor goes, so too should the draft. We could associate multiple drafts with a single editor but that seems stupid. I imagine it would just be a proxy for users to manually manage changes they've made on different devices with using the conflict editor. Actually...

h3.lit: History UX per field

value.lit: ...as weird as it sounds that could be a path if you consider that editors always have multiple versions (i.e. it's committed history plus any drafts). I wonder if it would make sense to solve draft management and field history in the same stroke? We'd still need a conflict editor though because regardless of where the two states come from, we don't want users having to manually copy-paste to merge themselves with no automated support. Holy shit, this could actually turn into a real solution. Come to think of it, all of the above is written as if there are always, ever only two revisions needing a merge. What happens when a user has a local draft, a remote draft and a new event comes in? Now there are three sources. If we're saying that merges can be postponed does that mean there can also be an unsaved live change in the editor too, being a fourth source?
Interestingly, this might solve the accidental closure issue: even when a user discards a draft, just keep a non-current draft available in the remote store that the user can view in the history. (?)

//.lit: Fuck this is feeling a bit 'troublesome' in terms of tech, performance, architecture but don't worry about that. Know that I'm good enough to craft a good tech solution. Instead just focus of the user's experience.

ul.lit: look into some of Lamport's distributed clock stuff
what about the same user on multiple tabs
seriously, ensure some kind of max storage limit. We need to be safe to someone pasting 1GB of text
use TLA+ to ensure things like:
we never discard a newer draft in favour of an older one

Change fields to be like an imaginary flashcard: the latest value and editor on one side, and a bunch of utils/tabs/controls/menu on the other; one of those utils could be a way to show all history and drafts.

I'm very happy to have the entire screen consumed (i.e. be taken to another page) to view all a field's history, see my diffs, do merges. In which case we probably just need to make sure there's an efficient way of getting there.

value.lit: There have been some major changes that affect all requirements pertaining to deletion. Project storage is now via an append-only event-stream. As such history is never lost and can easily be made accessible to the user.
Deletion subjects:

ol.lit: Config

ul.lit: Tags
Issue types
Fields
Req types

ol.lit: Content

ul.lit: Generic requirements
Req code groups

h4.lit: Why would you delete a tag?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things. They often imprecise.

change all the time; after seeing how statuses are being used one might decide to merge some, remove some; tags representing components can be changed to cover different subsets of a solution. It's also why we need to see usage – tags that rarely get used are often potential candidates for deletion.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means it shouldn't be used anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: It should be removed from existing data. Simple enough when it's just attached to something but it's a bit more involving when it's being used in text, like “Machine
do ...”. In those cases you can't just remove it, you need to either change it to another tag that fits or reword it.

h4.lit: What if you couldn't delete tags?

value.lit: I'd have to rename them stupidly to avoid people using them anymore. I'd want them to display in some kind of obvious way, like big and red so that BAs could see that they are obviously wrong. In fact, maybe they could be part of the issue system, like how other types of issues are reported and easily actionable.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Maybe if it's still in use – because then it's not really deleted. And the intent of tag deletion is removal of use.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Tags are fickle things like I said. Plus sometimes there's trial and error involved. You want tags to be decently distributed; you might delete stuff, add new tags, allocate them and realise you're worse off.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd need to add the tag back (quick) and then re-tag everything (annoying).

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
tag → parent tag
tag → child tags
tag → field
tag → req/code-group tag-pool
tag → req/code-group text

h4.lit: Why would you delete an issue type?

value.lit: Like tags, it's just a fickle idea to reduce data into subsets.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means the issue shouldn't be usable anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Those issues either need to change type, or be removed.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete?

value.lit: Same as tags. Rename stupidly. Or actually, rename it the next time I want to create a new issue type.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: If it's in use still – because something needs to be done about those issues.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same as tags.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: Add it back (quick). Maybe change some existing issues to use it again.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code: issue type → req/code-group text

h4.lit: Why would you delete a field?

value.lit: I'd delete a field if it wasn't really being used in practice.
I'd delete a tag field if I deleted (without replacing) the underlying tag.

I'd delete an implication field if I changed the req-types, say if I merged two req types into one and they both had fields. Actually if I deleted the underlying req type for any reason, I'd need to get rid of its field. Although that should probably be automatic right?

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: That fields is gone. Can't add new data to it, can't see it anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Hmm, I guess I'd probably still want to be able to see it if I need to. Like if it's a text field, if I deleted the field I probably wouldn't move that field data around. I'd just leave it but one day I might need to read it again and extract anything useful.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete a field?

value.lit: Nothing I guess. But I'd want to rename the field at least to indicate not to add any new data to it.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Well I just wouldn't want to lose data. If it deleted the field and the data with it, that would be bad. Gathered BA data (usually from interviews and analysis) should never be lost.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: New requirements might come in that revalidate the existence of the field, so I'd wanna get it back again and populate new rows with it.
Or someone with different needs might say they really need it.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind after a field was deleted.

value.lit: I'd want to add it back again and not lose any data.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
field ← req type (implication)
field ← tag
field ← req types (applicability)
field → req field values

h4.lit: Why would you delete a req type?

value.lit: It might not be getting used. Or we might just look at our data differently and decide we have a better way of representing it.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: It means we shouldn't have any reqs allocated to it anymore.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: They should be reallocated to a different req type. Or rarely, just deleted.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete a req type?

value.lit: Rename it like my answers to the other questions.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: We might have printed a report, or stored references to our reqs in other systems. In both cases the external entity will have an ID to a req that contains the req type. If we deleted the req type, it would break their references. They should always be able to use those references to look up the same reqs. If a req type is deleted, reqs should still remember their old req types & IDs. We should still be able to delete req types though.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same reason for why I would delete one.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd want to add the req type back, maybe change reqs back to use it again.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
req type → field (implication)
req type → field (applicability)
req type → pubid register
req type → associated generic reqs
req type → refs in text

h4.lit: Why would you delete a req?

value.lit: An underlying (implying) requirement could change.
Or people's minds could change, or new information is discovered. Then existing requirements become obsolete and need to be deleted.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

value.lit: Turned off. Not visible anymore unless someone wants to search or look through stuff that's been removed.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Refs to it (from non-deleted stuff) should probably be considered errors. Not just text refs, but implications too. In that nothing alive should be implied by something dead. Wait, unless it's implied by something else in which case just ignore the dead implication.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete?

value.lit: Tag them as deleted.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Dunno.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

value.lit: Same reason you might delete one. Things change, life changes.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

value.lit: I'd probably just want to undo it. All it's old implications should be restored. It's req codes and their groups should come back. Text refs to it should be fine again (i.e. not an error anymore).

h4.lit: What if the req codes are in use now?

value.lit: I guess the ones being undeleted should be renamed. they should still form the same trees though – I wouldn't want one renamed but not others. Why? Cos I'd need to manually take care of the conflict and for that I'd want to look at what exists now and then the entire undeleted tree with its structure as it was.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
gen req ← refs in text
gen req ↔ gen reqs (implication)
gen req ↔ req codes associated
gen req - req code groups (indirectly)
gen req → req data (fields, tags, text)
gen req ← pubid register

h4.lit: Why would you delete an RCG?

ol.lit: If I reorganise my reqs and it becomes obsolete.
If I delete a bunch of reqs and the code group is only useful in that group of reqs.

h4.lit: What does it mean to be deleted? Can you explain without using the word delete?

ol.lit: I don't need it anymore. I don't want to see it anymore.
I want it removed with the reqs being removed.

h4.lit: What does that mean for existing data?

value.lit: Refs to it need to be removed. Children are fine if they still exist.

h4.lit: What would you do if you couldn't delete groups?

value.lit: I'd rename them, eventually recycle them and hate you.

h4.lit: Can you think of any cases where deletion shouldn't be allowed? If so, why? What if it happens anyway?

value.lit: Nope.

h4.lit: What is a reason you would change your mind about a deletion?

ol.lit: Reorg, becomes relevant again.
I undelete a group of reqs that came under the group.

h4.lit: What would you need to do if you changed your mind about a deletion.

ol.lit: Recreate it. Think of and re-enter a meaningful title again. Would be nice if I didn't have to do this.
Recreate all the req code groups I used to have. I'd have to remember titles or think of new ones. All of that would be annoying. Ideally they'd all just come back with the req undeletion.

h4.lit: Data relationships:

cb.code:
rcg → req code children
rcg → text refs

value.lit: Also we should be able to record a reason for deletion. For example I might select 10 things, delete them all at once and give the reason “FR-201 makes this redundant” or something.

h4.lit: Why?

value.lit: Because it's not always going to be obvious why something is deleted. You might search for something, find it dead and not be able to think of a reason why it shouldn't be alive, then you could undelete it without understanding that it's incompatible with another requirement.

h4.lit: What would you do if the system didn't support this?

value.lit: I guess I'd edit requirements before I delete them so include the reason. But that would be super annoying when deleting an entire block of reqs.

When an editor is open, we need to let users know when

ul.lit: a draft is available
multiple conflicting drafts are available
the field has received an external update

ol.lit: For all of the above, what's the process to resolution, including conflict resolution? How is the process impacted by trivial automatic merges (if implemented)?

When the user aborts an editor, the draft is saved (but marked as inactive), meaning that the user manually has to find and restore it if that's what they want. How many such drafts do we save? Just always the latest aborted draft? 3?

Users can view a list of past revisions and aborted drafts; what next? Do we start with a list, then go to a diff view or full rendering or what? Details. In the use cases we're planning at the moment, what flows/actions are required? What about in future? Stuff like restore (= undo), edit-from-here?
consider the effects wrt same user, multiple tabs

ul.lit: does opening an editor in one tab open it in the rest now??

what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and makes different changes in each. (Maybe we could save all editor drafts immediately on-change, but only do server syncs periodically?)
what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and aborts one?
what happens when the user has the same editor open in two tabs, and commits one?

h3.lit: Responses

ol.lit: When committing the change, the draft should be removed (locally and remotely).
When aborting a change, the draft should be retained as a rejected/inactive/aborted draft that the user can view in history.
The following answers will provide more clarity.

h3.lit: What's required around draft deletion/resolution?

value.lit: When committing the change, the draft should be removed (locally and remotely).
When aborting a change, the draft should be retained as a rejected/inactive/aborted draft that the user can view in history.
More detail in the follow up questions.

h3.lit: What happens when an offline user comes online, how exactly should the sync work?

value.lit: When an offline user comes online, the client should attempt to sync the drafts. Local drafts should be uploaded, remote drafts should be downloaded.

h4.lit: What if their local draft is older that the remote draft?

value.lit: Firstly, the user could have the same editor open in different browsers at the same time, meaning two open editors by the same user that have different local-storage stores when offline. Well firstly, what should happen when they are online? Wait there's a bunch to think about it here. Let's start with the easiest:

ul.lit: editor is dirty, external change occurs
i want to see the difference between what I started with vs the new change
ignore the update
reset editor to latest change
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
park/abort draft for now, maybe revisit later
cancel and leave things as they are
same editor open in two different tabs

value.lit: Immediately we're faced with a philosophical decision. If a user has the project open in different tabs or browsers (maybe home vs office):

ol.lit: should the user see the same state in all instances? eg. same editors open, same drafts in place

should editors only be open in the tabs where the user has explicitly opened editors? But because drafts are shared (and they need to be for maximal user benefit), when a user opens a editor, it resumes the latest draft.

value.lit: Ok after some thought, (1) is the way: just keep all client instances in sync. It seems simpler anyway. One of the problems with (2) is that if the editor's don't open, how does the user find which fields have active drafts? Don't want to add a new

screen. You could add some kind of visual indication to the field but that still requires users to search around for them. Solution (1) is much simpler in terms of consistency. If an editor is open, it's open everywhere. If an editor is closed, opening it always starts with the latest committed state. One exception to make is to treat open editors without changes as closed editors when it comes to synchronisation.
Also: state between tabs in the same browser instance/profile share the same local storage meaning that they're
in sync.
Ok done. Now back to the questions.

state is in sync between both
no problem - show the same state in both
state has diverged
add a
to both editors letting them know what the situations is
I want to see the difference between the drafts
choose one draft or the other
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
cancel and leave things as they are

value.lit: In some cases the client should be smart enough to discard/upgrade a draft by itself. Imagine this case:

ol.lit: user creates new draft A on laptop
client sends A to server
user loses internet on laptop without realising
user makes more local change to end up with draft B
user opens up page on desktop
client opens editor with draft A
user realises something is wrong - fixes internet on the laptop
laptop sends B to server
desktop receives draft B and is smart enough to automatically replace the untouched draft A with draft B

value.lit: The above is probably easy to do with a tab-parameterised Lamport timestamps.

h4.lit: Can we really resolve trivial conflicts without notifying the user? If so, how? What's trivial and what's not?

value.lit: Absolutely. Imagine 3 paragraphs; a change to paragraph 1 is trivially mergeable with a change to paragraph 3. If it can be done automatically with no conflicts, just do it. Otherwise we already have conflict resolution stuff planned. Will have to properly learn about diff'ing algorithms and merge strategies. Especially the line-based vs word-based diffs. Although ours should be done at the AST level right? Or as strings? The state of open editors is just a
. Investigate both. I guess it's an implementation detail really. The requirements needn't specify that.

value.lit: No, not anymore because we'll keep all client states per user in sync. When an active draft exists the editor will be open.

value.lit: Whatever the criteria, just make it configurable. Maybe just with the latest 1 and see how it goes. There's no real urgency to specify this immediately.

h3.lit: What if their local draft is older that the remote draft?

value.lit: Firstly, the user could have the same editor open in different browsers at the same time, meaning two open editors by the same user that have different local-storage stores when offline. Well firstly, what should happen when they are online? Wait there's a bunch to think about it here. Let's start with the easiest:

ul.lit: editor is dirty, external change occurs
i want to see the difference between what I started with vs the new change
ignore the update
reset editor to latest change
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
park/abort draft for now, maybe revisit later
cancel and leave things as they are
same editor open in two different tabs

value.lit: Immediately we're faced with a philosophical decision. If a user has the project open in different tabs or browsers (maybe home vs office):

ol.lit: should the user see the same state in all instances? eg. same editors open, same drafts in place

should editors only be open in the tabs where the user has explicitly opened editors? But because drafts are shared (and they need to be for maximal user benefit), when a user opens a editor, it resumes the latest draft.

value.lit: Ok after some thought, (1) is the way: just keep all client instances in sync. It seems simpler anyway. One of the problems with (2) is that if the editor's don't open, how does the user find which fields have active drafts? Don't want to add a new

screen. You could add some kind of visual indication to the field but that still requires users to search around for them. Solution (1) is much simpler in terms of consistency. If an editor is open, it's open everywhere. If an editor is closed, opening it always starts with the latest committed state. One exception to make is to treat open editors without changes as closed editors when it comes to synchronisation.
Also: state between tabs in the same browser instance/profile share the same local storage meaning that they're
in sync.
Ok done. Now back to the questions.

ul.lit: same editor open in two different tabs
state is in sync between both
no problem - show the same state in both
state has diverged
add a
to both editors letting them know what the situations is
I want to see the difference between the drafts
choose one draft or the other
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
cancel and leave things as they are

value.lit: In some cases the client should be smart enough to discard/upgrade a draft by itself. Imagine this case:

ol.lit: user creates new draft A on laptop
client sends A to server
user loses internet on laptop without realising
user makes more local change to end up with draft B
user opens up page on desktop
client opens editor with draft A
user realises something is wrong - fixes internet on the laptop
laptop sends B to server
desktop receives draft B and is smart enough to automatically replace the untouched draft A with draft B

value.lit: The above is probably easy to do with a tab-parameterised Lamport timestamps.

h3.lit: Can we really resolve trivial conflicts without notifying the user? If so, how? What's trivial and what's not?

value.lit: Absolutely. Imagine 3 paragraphs; a change to paragraph 1 is trivially mergeable with a change to paragraph 3. If it can be done automatically with no conflicts, just do it. Otherwise we already have conflict resolution stuff planned. Will have to properly learn about diff'ing algorithms and merge strategies. Especially the line-based vs word-based diffs. Although ours should be done at the AST level right? Or as strings? The state of open editors is just a
. Investigate both. I guess it's an implementation detail really. The requirements needn't specify that.

h3.lit: Do we need to provide any visual indicators that drafts exist, when a field isn't currently being edited? (I think not)

value.lit: No, not anymore because we'll keep all client states per user in sync. When an active draft exists the editor will be open.

h3.lit: When the user aborts an editor, the draft is saved (but marked as inactive), meaning that the user manually has to find and restore it if that's what they want. How many such drafts do we save? Just always the latest aborted draft? 3?

value.lit: Whatever the criteria, just make it configurable. Maybe just with the latest 1 and see how it goes. There's no real urgency to specify this immediately.

h3.lit: Users can view a list of past revisions and aborted drafts; what next? Do we start with a list, then go to a diff view or full rendering or what? Details. In the use cases we're planning at the moment, what flows/actions are required? What about in future? Stuff like restore (= undo), edit-from-here?

value.lit: I think it should be like Intellij and Github:

ol.lit: show a list of available revisions and their descriptions (in our case desc could be

ul.lit: also show change and let user view as diff or whole (just like github)

ol.lit: once one is selected or maybe a subject somehow, present a bunch of options:

ul.lit: restore to editor
merge with current editor value

h3.lit: Let's get into the generic elicitation questions: What would a highly successful solution do for you?

ul.lit: let me just close a tab at any point and resume my edit in a new tab later
let me recover from accidentally aborting an editor
let me handle external changes occurring mid-edit
let me feel safe that in the event of some bug that just crashes the client, users' drafts aren't lost
lay some groundwork for offline-mode

h3.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that the new system could cause?

value.lit: Oh yes.

ul.lit: if we need a new service like rolling CouchDB instances, then an increased surface area in production, meaning more potential for failure, and maybe even more infra costs
if we don't use a new service, then more load on existing instances and the DB
worry about increased necessary localStorage space
complex distributed algorithms - will almost certainly need to break out TLA+ when designing the solution

security considerations now that we'll be storing data on the client side - we should encrypt it so that another user on the same machine can't (with reasonable effort) extract another user's data. Maybe we could generate a key client side with something like
or have a

stored server-side per user that is their encryption key which we send to the client and they never store. Although I assume once we have offline mode that will require us to be able to re-open a project from local cache without any server interaction. So probably some clever deterministic key generation from client-side open data (like IDs and maybe an open disguised seed) would be best. Actually! The user's password should be required and part of the key, meaning that without the password, there's no way for an attacker to generate the key.

h3.lit: How is the product you envision similar to the way you do business now? How should it be different?

value.lit: Well the way editors already work shouldn't change. Things like:

ul.lit: dirty editor noted in toolbar
warning prompt to abort dirty editor
aborting an editor and reopening it => editor is reset, not dirty, aborted draft not used

value.lit: Some differences though:

ul.lit: an open editor in one is now open in all -

only explicitly opened editors should have autoFocus. If the client opens an editor itself in response to a sync, it should not use autoFocus. Example of why this would be bad is if a user has two tabs open, reading from one, editing in the other, opening an editor in tab 1 should autoFocus but whatever they're looking at the tab 2 should stay as it is because it's what they're reading. It would be super annoying that every time they edit something in tab 1 they have to re-scroll in tab 2 (or 3 or 4).
aborted drafts will be kept around - don't know how many revisions or for how long, but there'll be a means for users to recover from an accidental abort they made this week

until now we've kept no state or data on the client side. Now that we'll start doing it for drafts and for offline-mode in future, we could start using it for other reasons too. Eg. maybe we can cache project events and snapshots client-side now. That might be a really good idea seeing as we'll need to start providing the entire history of project events, as opposed to just a snapshot plus a few events on top.

h3.lit: What aspect of the product will be most valuable to you? Least valuable?

value.lit: Well all of it will be kind of equally important with the exception of recovering aborted drafts. It's important too but everything else is more important.
As usual, it's all intertwined in that I want to make sure that whatever work I do, is an important step for enabling
and

. I don't want to have to change much or design anything once I start focusing on those other features. I'd rather plan it all upfront if that's what's necessary. And I'm not just talking about architecture, I'm talking about UX too.

h3.lit: What's required around draft deletion/resolution?

value.lit: When committing the change, the draft should be removed (locally and remotely).
When aborting a change, the draft should be retained as a rejected/inactive/aborted draft that the user can view in history.
More detail in the follow up questions.

h3.lit: What's required around draft deletion/resolution?

value.lit: When committing the change, the draft should be removed (locally and remotely).
When aborting a change, the draft should be retained as a rejected/inactive/aborted draft that the user can view in history.
More detail in the follow up questions.

h3.lit: What happens when an offline user comes online, how exactly should the sync work?

value.lit: When an offline user comes online, the client should attempt to sync the drafts. Local drafts should be uploaded, remote drafts should be downloaded.

h3.lit: What if their local draft is older that the remote draft?

value.lit: Firstly, the user could have the same editor open in different browsers at the same time, meaning two open editors by the same user that have different local-storage stores when offline. Well firstly, what should happen when they are online? Wait there's a bunch to think about it here. Let's start with the easiest:

ul.lit: editor is dirty, external change occurs
I want to see the difference between what I started with vs the new change
ignore the update
reset editor to latest change
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
park/abort draft for now, maybe revisit later
cancel and leave things as they are
same editor open in two different tabs

value.lit: Immediately we're faced with a philosophical decision. If a user has the project open in different tabs or browsers (maybe home vs office):

ol.lit: should the user see the same state in all instances? eg. same editors open, same drafts in place

should editors only be open in the tabs where the user has explicitly opened editors? But because drafts are shared (and they need to be for maximal user benefit), when a user opens a editor, it resumes the latest draft.

value.lit: Ok after some thought, (1) is the way: just keep all client instances in sync. It seems simpler anyway. One of the problems with (2) is that if the editor's don't open, how does the user find which fields have active drafts? Don't want to add a new

screen. You could add some kind of visual indication to the field but that still requires users to search around for them. Solution (1) is much simpler in terms of consistency. If an editor is open, it's open everywhere. If an editor is closed, opening it always starts with the latest committed state. One exception to make is to treat open editors without changes as closed editors when it comes to synchronisation.
Also: state between tabs in the same browser instance/profile share the same local storage meaning that they're
in sync.
Ok done. Now back to the questions.

ul.lit: same editor open in two different tabs
state is in sync between both
no problem - show the same state in both
state has diverged
add a
to both editors letting them know what the situations is
I want to see the difference between the drafts
choose one draft or the other
do a 3-way merge
automatically merge changes into current draft if trivial
cancel and leave things as they are

value.lit: In some cases the client should be smart enough to discard/upgrade a draft by itself. Imagine this case:

ol.lit: user creates new draft A on laptop
client sends A to server
user loses internet on laptop without realising
user makes more local change to end up with draft B
user opens up page on desktop
client opens editor with draft A
user realises something is wrong - fixes internet on the laptop
laptop sends B to server
desktop receives draft B and is smart enough to automatically replace the untouched draft A with draft B

value.lit: The above is probably easy to do with a tab-parameterised Lamport timestamps.

h3.lit: Can we really resolve trivial conflicts without notifying the user? If so, how? What's trivial and what's not?

value.lit: Absolutely. Imagine 3 paragraphs; a change to paragraph 1 is trivially mergeable with a change to paragraph 3. If it can be done automatically with no conflicts, just do it. Otherwise we already have conflict resolution stuff planned. Will have to properly learn about diff'ing algorithms and merge strategies. Especially the line-based vs word-based diffs. Although ours should be done at the AST level right? Or as strings? The state of open editors is just a
. Investigate both. I guess it's an implementation detail really. The requirements needn't specify that.

h3.lit: Do we need to provide any visual indicators that drafts exist, when a field isn't currently being edited? (I think not)

value.lit: No, not anymore because we'll keep all client states per user in sync. When an active draft exists the editor will be open.

h3.lit: When the user aborts an editor, the draft is saved (but marked as inactive), meaning that the user manually has to find and restore it if that's what they want. How many such drafts do we save? Just always the latest aborted draft? 3?

value.lit: Whatever the criteria, just make it configurable. Maybe just with the latest 1 and see how it goes. There's no real urgency to specify this immediately.

h3.lit: Users can view a list of past revisions and aborted drafts; what next? Do we start with a list, then go to a diff view or full rendering or what? Details. In the use cases we're planning at the moment, what flows/actions are required? What about in future? Stuff like restore (= undo), edit-from-here?

value.lit: I think it should be like Intellij and Github:

ol.lit: show a list of available revisions and their descriptions (in our case desc could be

ul.lit: also show change and let user view as diff or whole (just like github)

ol.lit: once one is selected or maybe a subject somehow, present a bunch of options:

ul.lit: restore to editor
merge with current editor value

h3.lit: Let's get into the generic elicitation questions: What would a highly successful solution do for you?

ul.lit: let me just close a tab at any point and resume my edit in a new tab later
let me recover from accidentally aborting an editor
let me handle external changes occurring mid-edit
let me feel safe that in the event of some bug that just crashes the client, users' drafts aren't lost
lay some groundwork for offline-mode

h3.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that the new system could cause?

value.lit: Oh yes.

ul.lit: if we need a new service like rolling CouchDB instances, then an increased surface area in production, meaning more potential for failure, and maybe even more infra costs
if we don't use a new service, then more load on existing instances and the DB
worry about increased necessary localStorage space (use compression)
complex distributed algorithms - will almost certainly need to break out TLA+ when designing the solution

security considerations now that we'll be storing data on the client side - we should encrypt it so that another user on the same machine can't (with reasonable effort) extract another user's data. Maybe we could generate a key client side with something like
or have a

stored server-side per user that is their encryption key which we send to the client and they never store. Although I assume once we have offline mode that will require us to be able to re-open a project from local cache without any server interaction. So probably some clever deterministic key generation from client-side open data (like IDs and maybe an open disguised seed) would be best. Actually! The user's password should be required and part of the key, meaning that without the password, there's no way for an attacker to generate the key.

h3.lit: How is the product you envision similar to the way you do business now? How should it be different?

value.lit: Well the way editors already work shouldn't change. Things like:

ul.lit: dirty editor noted in toolbar
warning prompt to abort dirty editor
aborting an editor and reopening it => editor is reset, not dirty, aborted draft not used

value.lit: Some differences though:

ul.lit: an open editor in one is now open in all -

only explicitly opened editors should have autoFocus. If the client opens an editor itself in response to a sync, it should not use autoFocus. Example of why this would be bad is if a user has two tabs open, reading from one, editing in the other, opening an editor in tab 1 should autoFocus but whatever they're looking at the tab 2 should stay as it is because it's what they're reading. It would be super annoying that every time they edit something in tab 1 they have to re-scroll in tab 2 (or 3 or 4).
aborted drafts will be kept around - don't know how many revisions or for how long, but there'll be a means for users to recover from an accidental abort they made this week

until now we've kept no state or data on the client side. Now that we'll start doing it for drafts and for offline-mode in future, we could start using it for other reasons too. Eg. maybe we can cache project events and snapshots client-side now. That might be a really good idea seeing as we'll need to start providing the entire history of project events, as opposed to just a snapshot plus a few events on top.

h3.lit: What aspect of the product will be most valuable to you? Least valuable?

value.lit: Well all of it will be kind of equally important with the exception of recovering aborted drafts. It's important too but everything else is more important.
As usual, it's all intertwined in that I want to make sure that whatever work I do, is an important step for enabling
and

. I don't want to have to change much or design anything once I start focusing on those other features. I'd rather plan it all upfront if that's what's necessary. And I'm not just talking about architecture, I'm talking about UX too.

//.lit: "Need to be able to specify a set of users as a field value"

h5.lit: Right, ok. Let's start with why. Why is this necessary?

value.lit: It's a real-world concept that we want to model. People-to-req relationships arise. Organisationally, people are assigned to work on tasks, testers are assigned to test reqs, etc etc. Because it's a part of real social and organisational practices, you'd be telling users to rely on their own memories rather than a trustable tool.

h5.lit: Values being a 0-n set is a reasonable starting point. What additional constraints or variations can you think of?

value.lit: Aspects I can think of are min and max.

A maximum doesn't make a huge amount of sense to me. If there were a max you could limit the number of users but why would that be practically useful? I guess you might want to set the max to 1 so that there's always a clear, single user responsible for something, rather than a few and then they all bicker about who's ultimately responsible. In the similar vein, it would allow users to create fields such that there's a primary assignee (max 1), and then there are secondary assignees with no max. You'd still have the problem that the same user could be in both fields... actually even that seems trivially solvable by having a "Disjoint" option in the field config and declaring that both fields must be disjoint from each other. Or another idea: maybe have a setting to declare that field 2 must always subtract the contents of field 1. idk, whatever. The point is, yeah, a max might be useful after all.

Minimum is much clearer. It definitely needs to be supported. Min 1 is equivalent to making a field mandatory. You might also have rules where you need a minimum of 2 testers to sign off on something. I think a better solution of sign-offs will be coming later
but having a min of 2 still seems like a reasonable thing that users may want to do.
An interesting point about minimums is that they change depending the status. It makes sense for an FR that's
to have an assignee, but not for an FR that's

, right? Well actually, having it left over is kind of helpful, people can see what they implemented. But imagine you were planning a sprint. The way some teams work, at the end of sprint planning, all FRs in scope should be assigned to someone. FR's not in the scope don't need to have assignees. So it sounds like you'd want to have a filter specified in config for FR's that satisfy that filter, the minimum changes from 0 to 1. Let's completely ignore that for now because it sounds like an enhancement we can make later that doesn't just affect user fields, but any kind of field.
Oh btw, another reason for a disjointness constraint: you might want to enforce that reviewedBy is disjoint from implementedBy so people don't just sign-off their own work. (?)
So yeah, min/max useful. Everything else I mentioned shouldn't be done right now when there's already a huge mountain of work to get done.

h5.lit: What about this dynamic
concept?

value.lit: It makes no sense in a field value. A value that's intensionally assigned to everyone all the time. If anything, it would be a hack, an exploit to dichotimise reqs.

h5.lit: Can you think of any unexpected or adverse consequences that this feature could cause?

value.lit: Users can be removed from projects. In that case we would have user fields that could have inactive members in them. I think that's ok though. In fact, in some cases it needs to be because we don't just record pending work in ShipReq, but also a history of past work and if they implemented something before their removal, you'd still want to see their names as the implementor. You wouldn't want inactive user names in field values with certain tags. Similar to above, if you could specify a filter and say when this filter applies, users in this field must be active users, that would make sense and be useful. Like above though, it needn't and shouldn't be done now.

"When the IV is 96-bit long, the 4 byte counter for the internal CTR cipher is always 1. When the IV has a different length, the 4 byte counter for CTR is taken from the GHASH of the IV - so it is uniformly randomized across 32 bits. That means that - when the IV is taken from an RNG, which is what everybody does - due to the birthday paradox it seems more secure (though slower) to supply a non-standard 128-bit IV than a standard 96-bit IV" --

title.lit: Derivative tags forces me to make default tags explicit
I have to lie about derivative tags in order for the ReqGraph to be useful

value.lit: Me
Me
The default status for FRs is
, however when I create a bunch of FRs from
IV, all the FRs inherit the same value. So that the whole subtree isn't yellow or orange I need to explicitly specify

when I create the FRs so that it overrides the parent IV's status. Forgetting to do so can be very troublesome because when I reorder dependencies it can completely change the derived tag which is very confusing.
The default status for FRs is
