when I create the FRs so that it overrides the parent IV's status. Forgetting to do so can be very troublesome because when I reorder dependencies it can completely change the derived tag which is very confusing.
From a users' PoV it seems like a bug that I have to manually specify the default status in order to have expected behaviour. Like what's the point of a default.
I introduced a new tag:
so that when I look at the ReqGraph I could see what was in progress and what's not.
Now in terms of reality, the

status should cascade up to the parents so that the related MFs get the status too. The problem is when I'm looking at the ReqGraph I don't want to see it cascade - half the screen changes colour; I only want the nodes which are directly/explicitly marked as
to be coloured as such.
Preservation of reality should always be the primary concern which means I need a new option for the ReqGraph to display it the way I want.
Multi-user design!
Multi-user design
Notes:

ul.lit: Permissions are commented-out for now. The plan is to start with only three types of built-in permissions — adminᵖᵘ, (collaboratorᵖ | memberᵘ), read-onlyᵖ — and later when I have more users implement permissions as a DAG just like users & projects.

We'll omit the ability to invite a P* to a PG. Pretty obscure. Add it later for completeness but ignore for now, especially seeing as one can create an invitation page and accomplish the same thing (provided they have a means to contact admin of the project).

h4.lit: User universe

ul.lit: all paths in the user universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the user universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P
all owners are members – an owner is a member (child) with admin permission
all user groups have owners: either a user or another user group. Make sure that all UGs have at least one admin child.

all user groups must have at least one user as a transitive owner - we can't allow user groups to become orphans without human admins. The DAG already structure enforces finite paths. Ensure all admin paths end in Us and not UGs.

h4.lit: Project universe

ul.lit: all paths in the project universe should be finite - therefore a DAG
all edges in the project universe should have an explicit permission - otherwise would allow illegal states where entities are linked without any permissions
an edge from
indicates that A has a member B with permission P

all P* have owners: either a U* or a PG. Either we need an attribute on P*, or we merge the U* and P* DAGs... Adding an attribute to P* would allow cyclic graphs when U* & P* are combined. It doesn't seem like that should be ok but let's test it out...
Seems ok! Necessary even because this is a perfectly valid vase. When viewing access from the P* perspectives:

cb.code:
P* -> U*
========
* P1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* P2
  * UG2 (admin)
    * U1 (admin)
* PG1
  * U1 (admin)

ul.lit: What about from U* perspectives?

cb.code:
U -> UGs
========
* U1
  * UG1 (admin)
  * UG2 (admin)
* U2
  * UG1 (member)

U -> P*
=======
* U1
  * PG1 (admin)
    * P1 (admin)
    * P2 (member)
* U2
  (none)

ul.lit: Wow ok. Seems fine. I would've thought any cycles = bad. I should come up with an intuition as to why it's not just ok, but necessary. The reason this is ok is that we never just mindlessly traverse links:
from the project access PoV, we don't jump from UG back to P
from the project group PoV, we don't jump from UG back to P, nor do we show list PG's Ps
from the user group PoV, we don't jump from UG back to P, nor do we expand PGs
all P* have owners
all P* must have at least one user as a transitive owner

value.lit: Following on from

h3.lit: Scenario 1: Children shouldn't be affected by parent

cb.code: needs_analysis + ready_for_dev = needs_analysis

value.lit: Which means currently we have:

cb.code:
node[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2

value.lit: where as if we treat defaults and explicit tags the same we have:

cb.code:
MF1,IV1[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
FR1,FR2[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2

value.lit: Which actually makes sense given the new intuition of implication being

. IV1 represents more than just FR1 and FR2 and it's perfectly reasonable that the smaller parts (FRs) aren't affected by the larger. For example maybe FR3 will need to be created and then IV1 can be marked as

h3.lit: Scenario 2: Children should be affected by parent

value.lit: But then what about the case where you want to mark a parent as
? You would expect that all children would also be marked as
unless they have another non-deferred parent. This is a case where the parent
affect all its children, where as the case above we state that the parent shouldn't affect all its children.

cb.code:
{rank=same
MF1[fillcolor="#d4d4d4"]
MF2[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
}
IV1,FR1,FR2[fillcolor="#d4d4d4"]
FR3[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2,FR3
MF2 -> FR3

h3.lit: Scenario 3: Children should be affected by parent

value.lit: Honestly, even thinking about the first example I can imagine a case where you have

cb.code:
MF1,IV1,FR1,FR2[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2

value.lit: and then a spanner in thrown into the works after some new discovery about the requirements or implementation which invalidates the content of IV1. In which case you'd want to set IV1 to
and actually have it cascade until resolved, so that the resulting graph is this:

cb.code:
node[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2

h3.lit: Conclusion: Children must be affected by parents

value.lit: As it stands, there is no difference between this and scenario 1. The explicit and default tags are the same:

cb.code:
MF1[fillcolor="#ffffff"]
IV1[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
FR1,FR2[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> IV1 -> FR1,FR2

value.lit: But we expect different results depending on different external/real-world state. In other words, we need to have the ability to specify where a tag should affect children or not. Immediately what comes to mind is that this sounds like a boolean flag that affects all children, but for true flexibility users should be able to specify which children it affects. Probably these settings:

ul.lit: affect all children
affect all children specified
affect all children except specified
don't affect children

value.lit: Interestingly, we can simplify that to

ul.lit: affect these children (none specified = affect all)
don't affect these children (none specified = don't affect any)

value.lit: In other words:

ul.lit: affect :: Boolean
optional whitelist

value.lit: The whitelist could be a filter for maximum flexibility (oh god). Users have the ability to negate filters themselves so I guess it's more like:

cb.code: AffectChildren (Maybe Filter) | DontAffectChildren

value.lit: Which could work really well as text:

cb.code:
#analysed        - Affect no children
#analysed+       - Affect all children
#analysed+(FR2)  - Affect only child FR2
#analysed+(-FR2) - Affect all children except FR2

value.lit: Similarly, maybe children should have the inverse ability to decide whether to allow specify tags from specify parents. Sounds like a terrible idea though. Having one way of doing things is better, and choosing between the parent or child as the optimal place leads to the parent being a clear and obvious winner.

h3.lit: Scenario 4: FB -> IV

value.lit: Consider explicit tags:

cb.code:
MF1[fillcolor="#ffffff"]
FB1[fillcolor="#f5f100"]
IV1[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
FR1[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> FB1 -> IV1 -> FR1

analysed       + needs_analysis = needs_analysis
needs_analysis + ready_for_dev  = needs_analysis

FR1 not affected by parent: remains ready_for_dev
IV1 not affected by parent: needs_analysis + ready_for_dev = needs_analysis
FB1: analysed + needs_analysis (via IV1) = needs_analysis
MF1: needs_analysis (via FB1)

MF1,FB1,IV1[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
FR1[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> FB1 -> IV1 -> FR1

value.lit: That's imperfect. We have FB1 marked as needing analysis even though that's not true. The reality is actually this:

cb.code:
FB1[fillcolor="#f5f100"]
MF1,IV1[fillcolor="#ffc905"]
FR1[fillcolor="#ccf000"]
MF1 -> FB1 -> IV1 -> FR1

value.lit: Actually maybe I'm wrong... FB1 as an individual task is analysed, but FB1 holistically does actually still need analysis — it's just occurring in IV1. So there's a difference between the state of an individual node in isolation vs it's total/holistic state. So if a user were to ask the questions:

ol.lit: "What still needs analysis for me to work on?" FB1 shouldn't show up.
"Hey how's FB1 going? Is it done yet? Oh it still needs analysis. Ok cool they're working on it."
